articles.title: Nepali PM Balendra Shah’s Image as a Change Agent is at Risk
articles.full_text:
Shah has shown less concern for the health and wellbeing of the poor, and is preoccupied with appearing as a ruthless executor of public plans.
Nepal’s Prime Minister Balendra Shah (center) heads a meeting of the Council of Ministers in Kathmandu, Nepal, July 14, 2026.
When 37-year-old Prem Prasad Acharya died after setting himself on fire in January 2023, Balendra Shah was the mayor of Kathmandu, Nepal’s bustling national capital. Acharya had been frustrated by the repeated failures of his business ventures. At the time, Shah termed the incident a sign of “the state’s extreme failure.”
That comment is now coming back to haunt Shah, the country’s prime minister since March 2026.
On July 9 this year, it was the turn of 25-year-old Ganesh Nepali, a Dalit who was providing for his family by running a ride-hailing service on his motorcycle, to try to self-immolate. He chose to do so rather than pay 1,000 Nepali rupees (roughly $6.5) in parking fines. He died a day later. Nepali was seemingly under great financial stress as he had to support his family on his meager income while paying the bank installments on the bike. At the time of the incident, Nepali had been applying to work in the UAE.
Nearly two weeks later, Shah continues to feel the fallout from the incident.
The parking fine imposed by Kathmandu’s municipal police acted as the immediate trigger for Nepali, who was already under considerable financial strain.
During his term as mayor, Shah had given the municipal police dubious powers that are otherwise given only to traffic police, including the power to impose traffic fines — and those powers continue to be abused. Although the municipal police falls under the Kathmandu Metropolitan City and not under the federal government that Shah leads, its actions continue to be seen as part of Shah’s legacy as mayor.
The Ganesh Nepali controversy was especially damaging because the government at the time was defending proposals for a significant increase in the scope and amount of traffic violation fines, with top fines of 100,000 rupees for some offenses. Even before Nepali’s self-immolation, these proposed fines were widely being criticized as instruments of coercion rather than proportionate regulation.
Then there is the broader picture. Shah, who is now 36, came to power on the back of the youth protests partly fueled by concerns over unemployment and inequality. There has been no visible progress in addressing these problems. Young people remain frustrated by the lack of good jobs and are looking to leave the country the first chance they get.
There is no hard proof that the plight of those who are indebted or in precarious employment has gotten worse under the new government. Yet the youth frustration does expose the absence of a convincing government response to the old problems of indebtedness and joblessness.
Separately, soon after becoming prime minister, Shah started a campaign to raze Kathmandu’s riverside squatter settlements without any plan to resettle thousands of those who would be displaced. During the campaign, two squatters died by suicide.
Even during his tenure as the mayor of Kathmandu, the benefits of Shah’s efforts were most visible to the upper and middle classes, while their costs disproportionately fell on squatters and informal workers. The squatters were portrayed as usurpers of public land and bulldozers were turned on their homes. Other roadside vendors were also forcibly removed. His beautification and public-space campaigns were not matched by credible plans to protect housing and livelihoods.
As the prime minister, Shah has shown even less concern for the health and wellbeing of the poor. Rather, he seems determined to project an image of a ruthless executor of public plans. Critics of his government’s unjust plans and policies are often harassed by pro-Shah accounts online.
Another emblematic recent case of state excess has been the beating and detention of Majid Ansari, a young activist who had dared to speak up on the wretched plight of the displaced squatters. Ansari vociferously criticized the government when the temporary shelters where the squatters had been housed started flooding; the government had earlier moved them there despite warnings that the place was flood-prone.
Ansari was taken into custody on dubious charges — much to the anger of the Gen Z activists who had propelled Shah to power. Ansari was among the activists involved in the Gen Z movement that overthrew the old K.P. Sharma Oli government, an event that culminated in Shah’s election as prime minister in the March 2026 elections.
The Gen Z activists are not alone in criticizing the Shah government’s rather arbitrary and inhumane governance. Increasingly, even ruling-party lawmakers have been put off by the prime minister’s heavy-handedness and lack of foresight.
The government has yet to show that unemployment and inequality are among its urgent priorities; Nepali’s death crystallized the wider public frustrations.
Shah’s public image as a change agent is at risk. The prime minister always appears in public wearing his signature dark glasses. The glasses that once symbolized anti-establishment defiance have increasingly become a metaphor for a government unwilling to see those bearing the heaviest costs of its rule.

articles.title: New Delhi’s Jantar Mantar Is Not Tahrir Square, But It Can Become One
articles.full_text:
At its heart, this movement is demanding accountability, not upheaval.
Protestors gather at Jantar Mantar, New Delhi, India, July 21, 2026.
It is tempting to compare the students’ protest at New Delhi’s Jantar Mantar with Cairo’s Tahrir Square, the epicenter of the uprising that brought to an end Hosni Mubarak’s nearly 30-year rule.
The comparison, however, is only partly valid. Equating what is happening in New Delhi with Egypt’s iconic protest would be unrealistic, even if the two movements share striking similarities. In Egypt, calls to gather at Tahrir Square spread rapidly through social media, including YouTube and Facebook. Likewise, the Cockroach Janta Party, which emerged on Instagram, has used social media to mobilize thousands to assemble at Jantar Mantar and protest against the National Eligibility cum Entrance Test (NEET) examination paper leak, which has fueled widespread anger among students and, according to the movement, contributed to a tragic wave of student suicides.
The objectives of the two protests, however, are fundamentally different. The protesters at Jantar Mantar are demanding accountability from the government and the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan. The demonstrators in Tahrir Square sought nothing less than the end of Hosni Mubarak’s three-decade authoritarian rule. Yet the deeper similarity lies elsewhere: both movements are driven by the frustration of a generation that believes its government has become unresponsive to its aspirations and grievances.
The Jantar Mantar protest is not an attempt to overthrow the state, but a determined demand that the state honor its constitutional obligations and restore young people’s faith in public institutions. It is a physical mirror of a generation’s quiet desperation, where broken promises, shrinking opportunities, and unresponsive governance are finding a collective voice.
If the Egyptians were expressing their anger against Hosni Mubarak’s three-decade rule, during which every voice of dissent was often silenced by the butt of a gun, Jantar Mantar has lifted the lid on years of frustration over misgovernance and, perhaps more importantly, what many perceive as the arrogance of the Narendra Modi government over the past 14 years.
Calls for public accountability have frequently been met with disdain. Those seeking answers on corruption, administrative failures, or misgovernance are often dismissed, insulted, or humiliated rather than engaged with. The government’s response to successive examination paper leaks has done little to restore public confidence. Beyond transferring the education secretary to another department, no major structural reforms have been announced. Nor did Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan publicly reach out to assuage the feelings of the students who were forced to retake the examination after the paper leak.
Another feature that demonstrates public anger against the government is that people across India and abroad are using online food delivery apps such as Zomato and Swiggy to send bulk meals — ranging from pizzas and burgers to local snacks — directly to protesters at Jantar Mantar in New Delhi. This spontaneous act of solidarity has overwhelmed the protest site with food, prompting organizers to appeal to people to stop sending more. Perhaps the biggest sign of the movement’s growing public support is that people who are unable to join the protest physically are choosing to express their empathy and solidarity by sending food to the protesters.
The movement is gathering momentum with each passing day. As the authorities attempt to curb it through brute force, the protests have only grown stronger. The more the police and paramilitary forces resort to violence, the more determined and steadfast the protesters become. History offers many examples of governments inadvertently strengthening protest movements when they respond to public anger with force rather than dialogue. Democracies possess an advantage unavailable to authoritarian regimes — they can absorb dissent through accountability and reform. Whether this government chooses that path may determine whether Jantar Mantar remains a protest site or evolves into something far more politically significant.
Jantar Mantar today stands as a barometer of India’s social contract. When thousands of educated young people travel hundreds of miles to sleep on Delhi’s pavement, they are sending a clear message that the institutions meant to hear their voices — through petitions, representations, and democratic processes — have failed them.
At its heart, this movement is demanding accountability, not upheaval. It is asking the government to restore the promise that education, merit, and hard work will once again open the door to opportunity. Whether the Cockroach Janata Party survives as an organization is almost beside the point. The larger question is whether the government chooses to listen to the generation or not.
The roar of slogans at Jantar Mantar is growing louder. It is now for the government to break its silence before it’s too late.

Asif Ullah Khan is a veteran journalist who has held senior editorial positions at The Times of India, Khaleej Times, The Hindustan Times, and The Brunei Times. He currently writes for The Diplomat, The Wire, The India Legal, The ASEAN Post, and other international publications.

articles.title: Why China Is Investing in Planetary Defense
articles.full_text:
For China, planetary defense is about building its international image and strengthening national security. However, the same factors also constrain China’s global contribution.
Within the past several weeks, China has announced a series of initiatives related to planetary defense. At the end of June, it unveiled plans to establish an asteroid early-warning network for planetary defense. A few days ago, China also released further details about its asteroid deflection mission, which has been under development for several years. The mission is currently scheduled for 2027, although the launch date may still change. The mission is expected to involve sending a spacecraft to impact an asteroid in an attempt to alter its trajectory and, potentially, disrupt its structure.
These steps are part of broader long-term plans. In 2018, China joined the International Asteroid Warning Network (IAWN) and the Space Mission Planning Advisory Group (SMPAG), both of which operate under the auspices of the United Nations Office for Outer Space Affairs. The 2021 Space White Paper elevated planetary defense to one of China’s policy priorities for the coming years, outlining plans to develop a near-Earth object defense system by improving its monitoring, cataloguing, early warning, response, and disposal capabilities. 
In 2022, China established the Deep Space Exploration Laboratory (DSEL), which is responsible, among other tasks, for conducting research on planetary defense. DSEL is led by Wu Weiren, one of China’s leading space engineers and the chief designer of the Chinese Lunar Exploration Program. In February 2025, Chinese media reported that the government had opened three new positions for planetary defense experts. The following month, DSEL organized a conference on near-Earth asteroid defense, attended by more than 50 Chinese experts.
Planetary Defense and National Priorities
One may wonder why an authoritarian state such as China is paying increasing attention to planetary defense despite the low probability of a major asteroid impact. This raises the question of what political and strategic objectives these efforts serve. 
In a recent peer-reviewed article published in Space Policy, my co-authors and I examined China’s motivations for expanding its role in planetary defense. One important factor is the ambition to build China’s image as a strong space power. In 2022, Xi Jinping stated that “to explore the vast cosmos, develop the space industry and build China into a space power is our eternal dream.”
Engagement in planetary defense also enables China to present itself as a comprehensive space power, in line with the vision articulated in its 2016 Space White Paper of becoming “a space power in all respects.” At the same time, because planetary defense is widely perceived as an activity that serves the global public good, China’s growing involvement reinforces its image as a responsible power. In doing so, it aligns with Beijing’s broader narrative of building “a global community of shared future” for all of humanity.
Another important motivation is domestic legitimacy. The national space program serves as a powerful demonstration of China’s global influence and technological capabilities. The government also seems to be responding to public concerns. According to official sources, Chinese authorities announced plans for an asteroid early warning system in 2018 following a fireball that exploded over Yunnan Province in October 2017. Similarly, when state media reported the recruitment of new planetary defense experts in early 2025, they noted that there was “widespread concern on Chinese social media”’ about the possibility of an asteroid impact.
In addition, the development of planetary defense is closely linked to national security and the advancement of military technologies, as many planetary defense capabilities have dual-use applications. In 2017, Xi Jinping said that “integrated military and civilian development should be implemented in the fields of sea, outer space, cyber space, biology, and new energy.” In 2020, the National Development and Reform Commission identified space infrastructure as an example of a platform for military-civil fusion. 
The military is regarded by experts as the main actor controlling China’s space program. In 2024, China established the Aerospace Force as one of the seven main branches of the People’s Liberation Army, with responsibility for military space operations.
By investing in planetary defense, China can also promote its technologies internationally and support technology exports. For example, BeiDou, China’s alternative to GPS, was initially developed for military purposes but was later promoted for civilian and commercial applications. BeiDou is now used by governments and companies around the world.
China’s Own Experts Warn of Gaps in Its Planetary Defense
However, China’s planetary defense capabilities continue to face significant limitations. Compared with the United States, China remains less technologically and institutionally developed in this field and has been relatively reluctant to share information with international partners. An example is the above-mentioned recently announced asteroid early-warning network. It remains unclear whether the proposed network will incorporate dedicated space-based monitoring assets or rely on existing satellite infrastructure. It is also unknown how the network will be institutionally governed or to what extent it will share information with international partners.
China’s limitations in contributing to global planetary defense have been acknowledged even by Chinese experts, including Wu Weiren himself. In a 2022 article, of which Wu was a co-author, the authors argued that “China has low international contribution and influence in the field of planetary defense.” They noted that, of the approximately 40,000 near-Earth asteroids identified worldwide at the time, only 32 had been discovered by a Chinese observatory. They recommended that China play a more active role through United Nations mechanisms, participate in international coordination frameworks and expand data sharing. 
This article was not an aberration. For several years, Chinese experts have engaged in an active debate criticizing the country’s limited preparedness and calling for better coordination and greater international engagement in planetary defense.
Even domestically, China’s planetary defense program is institutionally fragmented. Although the China National Space Administration (CNSA) is often presented as China’s equivalent of NASA, it has relatively limited authority. Decision-making involves numerous government and semi-government agencies, the military, state-owned enterprises, and, ultimately, the Chinese Communist Party, which retains final decision-making authority. The institutional framework remains opaque and lacks effective coordination.
One of the principal limitations is that space policy, including planetary defense, is regarded as a matter of national security and military affairs, and as such, it is inherently characterized by a high degree of secrecy and limited transparency. Another factor limiting information sharing with foreign partners is the increasingly repressive environment under Xi Jinping. Regulations such as the Data Security Law (2021) and the Counter-Espionage Law (2023) impose strict penalties for sharing information related to national security.
China is likely to continue strengthening its planetary defense activities as it advances several of Beijing’s strategic objectives simultaneously. It supports China’s ambition to become a leading space power, reinforces its image as a responsible international actor, contributes to domestic legitimacy, and accelerates the development of dual-use technologies. However, China’s planetary defense program is likely to prioritize high-visibility projects and technologies with clear strategic or dual-use value over less visible forms of scientific research and international cooperation. 
At the same time, the close integration of planetary defense with China’s national security and military establishment, together with opaque governance structures and restrictive information policies, is likely to continue limiting Beijing’s contribution to global planetary defense efforts. 
Within the past several weeks, China has announced a series of initiatives related to planetary defense. At the end of June, it unveiled plans to establish an asteroid early-warning network for planetary defense. A few days ago, China also released further details about its asteroid deflection mission, which has been under development for several years. articles.title: India and Japan’s Emerging Shipbuilding Partnership
articles.full_text:
The future of India-Japan security cooperation lies in shipyards – and maybe with the Mogami-class frigate.
Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi welcomes Japanese Prime Minister Takaichi Sane to Hyderabad House, New Delhi, India, July 2, 2026.
The India-Japan defense partnership is entering a new phase. The two countries have deepened maritime cooperation for over a decade through regular naval exercises, defense dialogues, logistics agreements, and shared support for a Free and Open Indo-Pacific. Now the strategic demands of an increasingly contested maritime environment require the partnership to evolve beyond operational cooperation. Recent developments such as the transfer of Japan’s advanced Unified Complex Radio Antenna (UNICORN) mast technology to the Indian Navy, Japan’s relaxation of its arms exports policy, and discussions on co-developing Mogami-class frigates suggest that the future of India-Japan security cooperation also lies in the shipyards.
This shift reflects broader changes in the Indo-Pacific security landscape. China’s expansionist behavior and its increasing presence in the Indian Ocean have prompted like-minded countries to strengthen both deterrence and defense-industrial resilience. For both India and Japan, the challenge is now about building the industrial capacity needed to sustain long-term maritime power. Defense industrial cooperation, particularly in naval shipbuilding, provides a pathway to achieve that objective.
The agreement on Japan’s UNICORN mast system signed in November 2024 marks an important milestone in this regard. UNICORN is designed to integrate multiple communication, radar, and electronic warfare functions into a single mast to significantly improve warships’ operational efficiency while also reducing their radar signature. Importantly, the agreement showcases Japan’s growing confidence in transferring sophisticated defense technology to India as it signals an unprecedented level of strategic trust between the two nations. 
The UNICORN agreement serves as proof that India and Japan can move beyond a conventional buyer-seller relationship toward more collaborative defense production. The logical next step here is shipbuilding. India is a trusted strategic partner for Japan with an expanding defense industrial base capable of supporting larger collaborative projects. For India, which seeks to modernize its naval capabilities while advancing the objectives of Aatmanirbhar Bharat (self-reliance), access to Japanese technologies can complement indigenous shipbuilding programs. 
During Japanese Prime Minister Takaichi Sanae’s recent visit to India in July 2026 for the 16th India-Japan annual summit, the growing convergence between defense industrial cooperation and economic security was underscored. The summit emphasized enhancing defense industrial cooperation between the two countries. Both governments recognized that resilient supply chains, trusted technologies, and defense manufacturing are integral to national security. 
Shipbuilding sits at the intersection of these priorities. Joint shipbuilding creates long-term industrial linkages that involve advanced manufacturing, electronics, propulsion systems, artificial intelligence, and maintenance ecosystems. Such cooperation also strengthens both military capability and economic resilience. In this context, Japan’s proposal to jointly develop and produce Mogami-class frigates with India deserves serious consideration. The Mogami class represents one of the most advanced multi-mission frigates currently in production, combining stealth features, high levels of automation, anti-submarine warfare capabilities, and modular mission flexibility. 
For India, collaboration on such a platform may go well beyond acquiring another class of warship. Indian shipyards have progressed in he design and construction of destroyers, frigates, and offshore patrol vessels. However, Japan’s expertise in automation, integrated ship systems, and advanced maritime electronics could enhance India’s next generation of naval platforms. Moreover, co-development would facilitate technology transfer, strengthen domestic manufacturing, and contribute to the development of a competitive indigenous defense industrial ecosystem. Japan’s advanced design and production expertise could prove to be beneficial for India’s ambition to become a self-sufficient shipbuilding powerhouse. 
Despite possessing world-class maritime technologies, Japanese defense companies face the constraints of a relatively small domestic market. Therefore, partnering with India would provide greater production scale, strengthen supply-chain resilience and support long-term sustainability of Japan’s defense industrial base. The presence of Japan’s industrial companies like Suzuki, Mitsubishi and Honda in India shows that Japan’s technology and India’s manufacturing capacity can create a competitive production ecosystem. It would also align with Tokyo’s efforts to build defense industrial partnerships with trusted Indo-Pacific partners, complementing similar initiatives with Australia, the United Kingdom and Italy. 
Strategically, India-Japan shipbuilding cooperation would also deepen the regional maritime balance. Both countries occupy a crucial position along the Indo-Pacific’s critical sea lanes and share an interest in preserving freedom of navigation, respect for international law and a rules-based maritime order. Jointly developing naval platforms would improve interoperability across a range of missions, including anti-submarine warfare, humanitarian assistance and disaster relief, and the protection of critical sea lines of communication. 
Such cooperation would further reinforce the strategic objectives of the Quad as well. While the framework focuses on non-traditional security issues like critical technologies, resilient supply chains, and maritime domain awareness, stronger bilateral defense industrial partnerships among its members are vital for sustaining long-term regional deterrence. India-Japan collaboration in naval shipbuilding would complement these efforts by creating tangible military capabilities rather than relying on political coordination. 
However, strategic convergence alone does not guarantee successful defense industrial cooperation. Both countries will have to navigate challenges ranging from Japan’s evolving defense export regime and domestic political sensitivities to differences in procurement systems and intellectual property arrangements. Therefore, maintaining momentum will require sustained political support and closer engagement between government and defense industries. Nevertheless, the convergence of India’s manufacturing ambitions and Japan’s technological advancements presents a rare opportunity to build a collaborative defense industrial partnership. 
A dedicated India-Japan defense industrial framework focused on naval platforms, joint research and development, and workforce collaboration could provide the institutional architecture necessary to translate political intent into industrial outcomes. The coming decade regarding India-Japan security cooperation should focus more on jointly building the capabilities that underpin security in the Indo-Pacific. 
If the UNICORN agreement demonstrated what is possible, collaboration on platforms like Mogami-class frigates could establish a new benchmark for defense industrial cooperation between Asia’s leading democracies. At a time when industrial capacity shapes strategic power, building warships together may prove to be an important investment India and Japan can make in the future of the Indo-Pacific. 
The India-Japan defense partnership is entering a new phase. The two countries have deepened maritime cooperation for over a decade through regular naval exercises, defense dialogues, logistics agreements, and shared support for a Free and Open Indo-Pacific. Now the strategic demands of an increasingly contested maritime environment require the partnership to evolve beyond operational cooperation. Recent developments such as the transfer of Japan’s advanced Unified Complex Radio Antenna (UNICORN) mast technology to the Indian Navy, Japan’s relaxation of its arms exports policy, and discussions on co-developing Mogami-class frigates suggest that the future of India-Japan security cooperation also lies in the shipyards.
This shift reflects broader changes in the Indo-Pacific security landscape. China’s expansionist behavior and its increasing presence in the Indian Ocean have prompted like-minded countries to strengthen both deterrence and defense-industrial resilience. For both India and Japan, the challenge is now about building the industrial capacity needed to sustain long-term maritime power. Defense industrial cooperation, particularly in naval shipbuilding, provides a pathway to achieve that objective.
The agreement on Japan’s UNICORN mast system signed in November 2024 marks an important milestone in this regard. UNICORN is designed to integrate multiple communication, radar, and electronic warfare functions into a single mast to significantly improve warships’ operational efficiency while also reducing their radar signature. Importantly, the agreement showcases Japan’s growing confidence in transferring sophisticated defense technology to India as it signals an unprecedented level of strategic trust between the two nations. 
The UNICORN agreement serves as proof that India and Japan can move beyond a conventional buyer-seller relationship toward more collaborative defense production. The logical next step here is shipbuilding. India is a trusted strategic partner for Japan with an expanding defense industrial base capable of supporting larger collaborative projects. For India, which seeks to modernize its naval capabilities while advancing the objectives of Aatmanirbhar Bharat (self-reliance), access to Japanese technologies can complement indigenous shipbuilding programs. 
During Japanese Prime Minister Takaichi Sanae’s recent visit to India in July 2026 for the 16th India-Japan annual summit, the growing convergence between defense industrial cooperation and economic security was underscored. The summit emphasized enhancing defense industrial cooperation between the two countries. Both governments recognized that resilient supply chains, trusted technologies, and defense manufacturing are integral to national security. 
Shipbuilding sits at the intersection of these priorities. Joint shipbuilding creates long-term industrial linkages that involve advanced manufacturing, electronics, propulsion systems, artificial intelligence, and maintenance ecosystems. Such cooperation also strengthens both military capability and economic resilience. In this context, Japan’s proposal to jointly develop and produce Mogami-class frigates with India deserves serious consideration. The Mogami class represents one of the most advanced multi-mission frigates currently in production, combining stealth features, high levels of automation, anti-submarine warfare capabilities, and modular mission flexibility. 
For India, collaboration on such a platform may go well beyond acquiring another class of warship. Indian shipyards have progressed in he design and construction of destroyers, frigates, and offshore patrol vessels. However, Japan’s expertise in automation, integrated ship systems, and advanced maritime electronics could enhance India’s next generation of naval platforms. Moreover, co-development would facilitate technology transfer, strengthen domestic manufacturing, and contribute to the development of a competitive indigenous defense industrial ecosystem. Japan’s advanced design and production expertise could prove to be beneficial for India’s ambition to become a self-sufficient shipbuilding powerhouse. 
Despite possessing world-class maritime technologies, Japanese defense companies face the constraints of a relatively small domestic market. Therefore, partnering with India would provide greater production scale, strengthen supply-chain resilience and support long-term sustainability of Japan’s defense industrial base. The presence of Japan’s industrial companies like Suzuki, Mitsubishi and Honda in India shows that Japan’s technology and India’s manufacturing capacity can create a competitive production ecosystem. It would also align with Tokyo’s efforts to build defense industrial partnerships with trusted Indo-Pacific partners, complementing similar initiatives with Australia, the United Kingdom and Italy. 
Strategically, India-Japan shipbuilding cooperation would also deepen the regional maritime balance. Both countries occupy a crucial position along the Indo-Pacific’s critical sea lanes and share an interest in preserving freedom of navigation, respect for international law and a rules-based maritime order. Jointly developing naval platforms would improve interoperability across a range of missions, including anti-submarine warfare, humanitarian assistance and disaster relief, and the protection of critical sea lines of communication. 
Such cooperation would further reinforce the strategic objectives of the Quad as well. While the framework focuses on non-traditional security issues like critical technologies, resilient supply chains, and maritime domain awareness, stronger bilateral defense industrial partnerships among its members are vital for sustaining long-term regional deterrence. India-Japan collaboration in naval shipbuilding would complement these efforts by creating tangible military capabilities rather than relying on political coordination. 
However, strategic convergence alone does not guarantee successful defense industrial cooperation. Both countries will have to navigate challenges ranging from Japan’s evolving defense export regime and domestic political sensitivities to differences in procurement systems and intellectual property arrangements. Therefore, maintaining momentum will require sustained political support and closer engagement between government and defense industries. Nevertheless, the convergence of India’s manufacturing ambitions and Japan’s technological advancements presents a rare opportunity to build a collaborative defense industrial partnership. 
A dedicated India-Japan defense industrial framework focused on naval platforms, joint research and development, and workforce collaboration could provide the institutional architecture necessary to translate political intent into industrial outcomes. The coming decade regarding India-Japan security cooperation should focus more on jointly building the capabilities that underpin security in the Indo-Pacific. 
If the UNICORN agreement demonstrated what is possible, collaboration on platforms like Mogami-class frigates could establish a new benchmark for defense industrial cooperation between Asia’s leading democracies. At a time when industrial capacity shapes strategic power, building warships together may prove to be an important investment India and Japan can make in the future of the Indo-Pacific. 
The India-Japan defense partnership is entering a new phase. The two countries have deepened maritime cooperation for over a decade through regular naval exercises, defense dialogues, logistics agreements, and shared support for a Free and Open Indo-Pacific. Now the strategic demands of an increasingly contested maritime environment require the partnership to evolve beyond operational cooperation. Recent developments such as the transfer of Japan’s advanced Unified Complex Radio Antenna (UNICORN) mast technology to the Indian Navy, Japan’s relaxation of its arms exports policy, and discussions on co-developing Mogami-class frigates suggest that the future of India-Japan security cooperation also lies in the shipyards.
This shift reflects broader changes in the Indo-Pacific security landscape. China’s expansionist behavior and its increasing presence in the Indian Ocean have prompted like-minded countries to strengthen both deterrence and defense-industrial resilience. For both India and Japan, the challenge is now about building the industrial capacity needed to sustain long-term maritime power. Defense industrial cooperation, particularly in naval shipbuilding, provides a pathway to achieve that objective.
articles.summary: The future of India-Japan security cooperation lies in shipyards – and maybe with the Mogami-class frigate.

articles.title: Whatever Happened to ‘Wolf Warrior’ Diplomacy?
articles.full_text:
In recent years, Beijing has become a lot more selective in how, where, and when it deploys the assertive diplomatic tactic.
In recent years, Beijing has adopted a calmer tone in its foreign policy, as President Xi Jinping has called on officials to build a “credible, lovable, and respectable image of China.” This transition has led many observers to conclude that China has abandoned “wolf warrior” diplomacy.
Rather than retreating from its well-known “wolf warrior” diplomacy, which it adopted in 2019, Beijing has differentiated its diplomatic messaging according to audience, issue, and strategic objective. In short, China did not abandon wolf warrior diplomacy but has rather become far more selective in where, when, and against whom it deploys the tactic.
China has pursued a more assertive foreign policy since Xi Jinping came to power in 2012. Xi introduced the concept of “major country diplomacy with Chinese characteristics,” in which he highlighted two points: first, building a “community with a shared future for mankind”; and second, what Xi coined as the need to “dare to fight,” whereby China strives to defend its core interests and assert itself in international discourse. The 19th National Congress in 2017 mentioned “national sovereignty, security, and development interests” alongside the “core national interests,” but China has never published any official documents to more specifically identify its core interests. This leaves Beijing with room to maneuver. 
Starting in 2019, Chinese foreign policy became noticeably more assertive in its language. The trend was labeled “wolf warrior diplomacy,” named after the Chinese Rambo-style action film. Zhao Lijian, the spokesperson for the Foreign Ministry and a key figure of wolf warrior diplomacy, frequently used social media to publicly criticize foreign governments. Scholars Yaoyao Dai and Luwei Rose Luqiu found a “steady and sharp increase in the hostility in diplomatic sentiment during Xi’s presidency” focused on sovereignty, terrorism, human rights, and Taiwan/One China.
For instance, in 2019 China’s ambassador to Sweden said in a radio interview that “We treat our friends with fine wine, but for our enemies we’ve got shotguns.”
In March 2020, at the height of the COVID-19 pandemic, Zhao Lijian speculated on Twitter that “It might be US army who brought the epidemic to Wuhan,” fueling a conspiracy theory. “US owe us an explanation!” Zhao demanded.
During the 2021 United States-China talks in Alaska, Central Foreign Affairs Commission Director Yang Jiechi said that “the United States should not be ‘condescending’ in its tone when speaking to China, as the Chinese people never buy it.”
In 2023, Beijing warned Taiwan and its allies to stop “playing with fire.”
Since then, the headlines about jaw-dropping comments have dwindled. But Beijing did not abandon wolf warrior diplomacy; it simply became more selective about using it. China now tailors its messaging according to the strategic importance of the audience and the issue at hand.
The United States continues to face Beijing’s most assertive diplomatic rhetoric on issues that China sees as its core interests, particularly Taiwan and national security.
One example would be during U.S. President Donald Trump’s visit in May 2026. While the visit was mostly a showcase of cordiality, it did have a sharp edge. Xi Jinping described Taiwan as “the most important issue in China-U.S. relations,” warning that mishandling the issue could lead to “clashes and even conflicts” and push bilateral relations into a dangerous situation. At the same time, both governments emphasized the importance of maintaining a “stable relationship despite their differences, with discussions focusing on strategic stability, economic cooperation, and managing competition.”
On issues involving its core interests, such as Taiwan, Beijing continues to take a firm and uncompromising position. At the same time, it increasingly presents these positions alongside the language of dialogue, cooperation, and stability. This suggests that China’s position has remained the same, but the way it communicates that position has been moderate.
Even during the height of wolf warrior diplomacy, Europe was not China’s primary target. When Beijing did adopt a more confrontational tone toward European countries, it was usually in response to issues such as human rights, Taiwan, or criticism of China’s domestic policies.
China’s ambassador to France, Lu Shaye, became one of the most recognizable “wolf warrior” diplomats in Europe. In 2020, he was summoned by the French Foreign Ministry after the Chinese Embassy defended Beijing’s handling of the COVID-19 pandemic while sharply criticizing France’s response. In 2021, he was summoned again over what France described as “insults and threats” following European sanctions on Chinese officials over human rights abuses in Xinjiang.
Compared with the United States, Europe has generally maintained the One China policy and has been more cautious than Washington on Taiwan-related issues. This is partly because Europe has generally maintained fewer political ties with Taiwan than the United States and has much stronger economic ties with China. Taking a stronger political stance on Taiwan or challenging the One China policy would create unnecessary tensions with Beijing. 
Since 2023, Beijing increasingly reserves wolf warrior diplomacy for situations where it believes its core interests have been challenged. Since Europe is less likely to touch on these core interests, it has mostly been spared the fiery rhetoric.
This was evident during Foreign Minister Wang Yi’s visit to Europe in 2025. Although disagreements over electric vehicle tariffs, Russia’s war in Ukraine, and market access remained, Wang repeatedly described China and Europe as “partners, not rivals” and emphasized the importance of dialogue, multilateralism, and maintaining stable economic ties. 
As long as China’s core interests are not directly involved, maintaining stable relations with Europe is the higher priority.
Since Xi Jinping introduced the idea of building a “community with a shared future for mankind,” Beijing has increasingly presented itself as a partner of developing countries. The concept reflects China’s ambition to play a larger role in global governance while portraying itself as a country that supports the development of the Global South.
One example is through the Belt and Road Initiative that Xi unveiled in 2013, which has financed infrastructure projects across Asia, Africa, the Middle East, Latin America, and parts of Europe. Through these initiatives, Beijing has sought to position itself as a leader of the developing world and reshape the world order.
Unlike its approach toward the United States, China rarely uses wolf warrior diplomacy when engaging with the Global South. Instead, Beijing presents itself as a development partner, emphasizing infrastructure investment, trade, and economic cooperation. One example was the 2024 Forum on China-Africa Cooperation, where Xi announced 10 partnerships covering infrastructure, trade, healthcare, agriculture, and green development. Throughout the forum, Xi emphasized modernization, a “China-Africa community with a shared future,” and cooperation, rather than criticism or confrontation. This reflects China’s broader strategy toward the Global South: instead of using confrontational rhetoric, Beijing seeks to build long-term partnerships and expand its influence through development and economic cooperation.
Rather than abandoning wolf warrior diplomacy, China has incorporated it into a broader and more flexible diplomatic strategy. Examining when Beijing chooses to use it provides important clues both about China’s overall foreign policy and how Beijing defines its “core national interests.”
In recent years, Beijing has adopted a calmer tone in its foreign policy, as President Xi Jinping has called on officials to build a “credible, lovable, and respectable image of China.” This transition has led many observers to conclude that China has abandoned “wolf warrior” diplomacy.
Rather than retreating from its well-known “wolf warrior” diplomacy, which it adopted in 2019, Beijing has differentiated its diplomatic messaging according to audience, issue, and strategic objective. In short, China did not abandon wolf warrior diplomacy but has rather become far more selective in where, when, and against whom it deploys the tactic.
China has pursued a more assertive foreign policy since Xi Jinping came to power in 2012. Xi introduced the concept of “major country diplomacy with Chinese characteristics,” in which he highlighted two points: first, building a “community with a shared future for mankind”; and second, what Xi coined as the need to “dare to fight,” whereby China strives to defend its core interests and assert itself in international discourse. The 19th National Congress in 2017 mentioned “national sovereignty, security, and development interests” alongside the “core national interests,” but China has never published any official documents to more specifically identify its core interests. This leaves Beijing with room to maneuver. 
Starting in 2019, Chinese foreign policy became noticeably more assertive in its language. The trend was labeled “wolf warrior diplomacy,” named after the Chinese Rambo-style action film. For instance, in 2019 China’s ambassador to Sweden said in a radio interview that “We treat our friends with fine wine, but for our enemies we’ve got shotguns.”
In March 2020, at the height of the COVID-19 pandemic, Zhao Lijian speculated on Twitter that “It might be US army who brought the epidemic to Wuhan,” fueling a conspiracy theory. “US owe us an explanation!” Zhao demanded.
During the 2021 United States-China talks in Alaska, Central Foreign Affairs Commission Director Yang Jiechi said that “the United States should not be ‘condescending’ in its tone when speaking to China, as the Chinese people never buy it.”
articles.summary: In recent years, Beijing has become a lot more selective in how, where, and when it deploys the assertive diplomatic tactic.

articles.title: ASEAN Foreign Ministers Call for ‘Immediate Cessation’ of Middle East Hostilities
articles.full_text:
The fighting between Iran and the United States is set to have “immediate and long-term effects” on Southeast Asia and its people, the ministers said.
Flags of the 11 ASEAN member states stand on stage at the 59th ASEAN Foreign Ministers’ Meeting in Manila, Philippines, July 21, 2026.
Foreign ministers from the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) have expressed “serious concern” over the renewed hostilities between the United States and Iran and called for the resumption of free movement through the Strait of Hormuz.
In a joint statement released after the close of the 59th ASEAN Foreign Ministers’ Meeting in Manila on Tuesday, ministers from the 11-nation bloc said that they were “deeply concerned that these developments severely undermine the ongoing efforts of key mediators and diminish the prospects for a peaceful resolution through dialogue and diplomacy.”
“We called on all parties concerned to exercise utmost self-restraint and avoid any acts that may further aggravate the situation,” the statement said, while calling for “a complete and immediate cessation of hostilities across all fronts in the Middle East.”
The ceasefire between Iran and the U.S., which was initially agreed in April and then formalized in June, collapsed earlier this month after both sides accused the other of reneging on their agreements.
The U.S. has since resumed its air attacks on Iran, which have been met by increased Iranian attacks on shipping and targets across the region, one of which killed three U.S. military personnel in Jordan. As Al Jazeera reported, the conflict has in recent days “expanded beyond military targets, with strikes increasingly hitting civilian infrastructure while both sides continue to harden their public positions.”
Southeast Asian nations were hit hard by the initial phase of the war, which began with the joint U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran on February 28 and prompted Iran to block shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. This led to an immediate spike in the price of oil and gas from the Middle East, the source of around 60 percent of Asia’s crude oil imports.
While each nation’s degree of vulnerability to Middle Eastern oil imports differs considerably, ASEAN’s foreign ministers welcomed the initial cessation of hostilities in April, calling for both Iran and the U.S. to “continue negotiations that will lead to the permanent end of the conflict and lasting peace and stability in the region.”
Echoing ASEAN’s earlier statements about the conflict, the foreign ministers noted on Tuesday that the return to fighting, which came after a short resumption of shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, would “immediate and long-term effects” on the region’s people. This also stood to have negative impacts “on regional trade, energy, and food security, including energy markets and supply chains, agricultural production, and the stability of agricultural input supply chains, particularly fertilizers.”
Finally, the ministers stressed the importance of maintaining “maritime safety and security, and upholding freedom of navigation in and overflight,” and called for the restoration of the “safe, unimpeded, and continuous transit passage of vessels and aircraft in the Strait of Hormuz.”
The resumption of the U.S.-Iran war has overshadowed this week’s ASEAN-related meetings in Manila, which have brought together.
During his meeting with ASEAN and other regional leaders yesterday, U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio warned them that Iran’s demand to control and collect tolls in the Strait of Hormuz would threaten the world’s economy and set a precedent that could be repeated elsewhere in the world.
“If we create a precedent in the Middle East where a nation-state can decide that they are going to control an international waterway, charge a toll and if you don’t pay them, blow up your ships, we have created a very dangerous precedent which will repeat itself in other parts of the world, including in this region,” he said.
This view is likely to find some purchase within ASEAN. While the bloc’s initial statement on the war on March 4 explicitly noted that it had resulted from “attacks initiated by Israel and the United States,” this week’s statement made no such mention. This could suggest that the bloc now apportions equal responsibility to Iran for the breakdown of the ceasefire, and opposes its determination to control shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
At the same time, the region’s governments will not forget that Iran never would have taken such a step were it not for the brazen U.S.-Israeli attack in late February.
In any event, an immediate end to the fighting seems like a remote prospect. Rubio said Iran “has been reaching out to the United States, both directly and indirectly, to engage in talks about settling the differences.” While “the U.S. would love to reach a diplomatic settlement,” the Trump administration is “not going to sit by and let ships be blown up or Americans be attacked,” he said.
Southeast Asian governments, having just adjusted with some difficulty to the first phase of the conflict, now seem set a renewed – and possibly prolonged – spell of economic turmoil.
Foreign ministers from the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) have expressed “serious concern” over the renewed hostilities between the United States and Iran and called for the resumption of free movement through the Strait of Hormuz.
In a joint statement released after the close of the 59th ASEAN Foreign Ministers’ Meeting in Manila on Tuesday, ministers from the 11-nation bloc said that they were “deeply concerned that these developments severely undermine the ongoing efforts of key mediators and diminish the prospects for a peaceful resolution through dialogue and diplomacy.”
“We called on all parties concerned to exercise utmost self-restraint and avoid any acts that may further aggravate the situation,” the statement said, while calling for “a complete and immediate cessation of hostilities across all fronts in the Middle East.”

The U.S. has since resumed its air attacks on Iran, which have been met by increased Iranian attacks on shipping and targets across the region, one of which killed three U.S. military personnel in Jordan. As Al Jazeera reported, the conflict has in recent days “expanded beyond military targets, with strikes increasingly hitting civilian infrastructure while both sides continue to harden their public positions.”

Echoing ASEAN’s earlier statements about the conflict, the foreign ministers noted on Tuesday that the return to fighting, which came after a short resumption of shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, would “immediate and long-term effects” on the region’s people. This also stood to have negative impacts “on regional trade, energy, and food security, including energy markets and supply chains, agricultural production, and the stability of agricultural input supply chains, particularly fertilizers.”
Finally, the ministers stressed the importance of maintaining “maritime safety and security, and upholding freedom of navigation in and overflight,” and called for the restoration of the “safe, unimpeded, and continuous transit passage of vessels and aircraft in the Strait of Hormuz.”

During his meeting with ASEAN and other regional leaders yesterday, U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio warned them that Iran’s demand to control and collect tolls in the Strait of Hormuz would threaten the world’s economy and set a precedent that could be repeated elsewhere in the world.
“If we create a precedent in the Middle East where a nation-state can decide that they are going to control an international waterway, charge a toll and if you don’t pay them, blow up your ships, we have created a very dangerous precedent which will repeat itself in other parts of the world, including in this region,” he said.
articles.summary: The fighting between Iran and the United States is set to have “immediate and long-term effects” on Southeast Asia and its people, the ministers said.

articles.title: Malaysia Must Reject Hate and Confront Myanmar Over the Rohingya Crisis
articles.full_text:
Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim’s government has largely remained silent about the recent online “hate campaign” targeting Rohingya refugees.
Two elderly Rohingya refugees at their home in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia.
Last month, more than 400,000 people signed a petition on Change.org calling for the removal of Rohingya refugees from Malaysia. The petition is part of a widening hate campaign targeting Rohingya refugees and others, fed by fabricated allegations of criminality and dangerous misinformation about an already vulnerable community.
Malaysia has long been a refuge for various communities from Myanmar, including the Rohingya, Chin, Bamar, Karen, Rakhine and other ethnicities. No refugee community should be subjected to hostility or abuse, and the recent targeting of Rohingya refugees reflects a broader failure to protect people fleeing violence and persecution in Malaysia. The Rohingya, who have fled decades of genocide at the hands of the Myanmar military and renewed violence in Rakhine State by the Arakan Army, have been singled out for intense xenophobic hostility by the Malaysian public and some officials.
People are entitled to express concerns about public policy on migration and refugees. But freedom of expression is not a license to incite discrimination, hatred, or violence.
The danger is greater when prominent public figures lend legitimacy to this hateful rhetoric, calling Rohingya a “cancer,” “dirty” “threats or criminals,” and “intruders.” This is despite the fact that only 89 Rohingya have been convicted for criminal offences since 2024, according to the latest government statistics, making up a tiny 0.02 percent of all criminal cases in Malaysia during that time.
Nevertheless, the Deputy Inspector-General of Police has described the Rohingya issue as a barah (“cancer”) requiring urgent action. Elected officials have endorsed expelling Rohingya from Malaysia, an act that contravenes the principle of non-refoulment in international refugee law. Some have called for the demolition of Rohingya homes, the severing of water and electricity supplies, and the forced expulsion of refugees to Myanmar or Bangladesh.
Yet Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim’s government has largely stayed silent as the hostility has escalated. The authorities should actively counter misinformation, fabricated crime allegations, and other false narratives targeting Rohingya refugees, and hold those responsible for inciting discrimination or violence accountable in accordance with the law.
Malaysia has long presented itself as a defender of persecuted Muslims globally. Remaining indifferent to and failing to protect Rohingya refugees, who are predominantly Muslim, risks undermining that reputation.
Even before the recent wave of hate, many refugees have described living in constant fear of arbitrary arrest, detention, and abuse by the Malaysian authorities. Rohingya refugees are particularly vulnerable to indefinite detention. A recent Fortify Rights investigation found that refugees had been detained without meaningful access to legal representation, fair judicial review, or any realistic prospect of release. Detainees are often subjected to ill-treatment and poor conditions, including inadequate healthcare, lack of clean water and food, and squalid conditions in detention, sometimes enduring such horrific conditions for years. Some have been forcibly repatriated to Myanmar or Bangladesh by Malaysian authorities.
Amid growing hostility against Rohingya refugees, Home Minister Saifuddin Nasution Ismail’s remarks on June 8 gave hope for a more constructive approach: “We cannot avoid managing this issue. It must be handled wisely and humanely, while safeguarding the country’s security, the people’s interests, and our international standing.”
This is the approach Malaysia should embrace. The Rohingya refugee crisis requires empathy, pragmatism, and strong regional cooperation – not the criminalization or vilification of refugees fleeing persecution.
First, Malaysia should continue providing protection to Rohingya refugees fleeing ongoing atrocities in Myanmar and inhumane camp conditions in Bangladesh. This includes adopting a zero-tolerance approach and taking strict action against hate campaigns to send a clear message that discrimination and incitement have no place in the country.
Malaysia should accelerate refugee registration, including for those who remain undocumented, and develop pathways to legalize their stay. Proper registration would provide authorities with accurate information, give proper control, strengthen protection, and reduce management challenges. Fortify Rights urged the government to ensure that the newly established Dokumen Pendaftaran Pelarian program, which seeks to register refugees present in Malaysia, is used to strengthen refugee protection, rather than facilitate the misuse of personal data, detention, or forced returns.
Furthermore, Malaysia should confront Myanmar and press it to restore Rohingya citizenship and end the ongoing atrocities in Myanmar.
Malaysia, together with a coalition of governments in the region, including ASEAN, should create a coalition of ASEAN governments, must exert diplomatic pressure on Myanmar to restore Rohingya citizenship, swiftly issue identification cards and passports to Rohingya, thereby providing them with legality, dignity, safety, and a future, and guarantee their rights and end ongoing atrocities.
With the enactment of Myanmar’s 1982 Citizenship Law by the former military regime under Ne Win, the Rohingya were deprived of citizenship and subjected to severe restrictions on movement, education, employment, marriage, and access to public services. In effect, the Citizenship Law stopped recognizing Rohingya as citizens of Myanmar, a policy that remains in place today. These policies created conditions that forced many Rohingya to flee Myanmar in search of safety, protection, and opportunity elsewhere in the region.
The situation deteriorated further in 2016, and 2017, when Myanmar’s military and local Rakhine collaborators carried out widespread attacks that forced some 700,000 Rohingya to flee to Bangladesh. Rohingya villages were burned, civilians were killed, and entire communities were displaced. The result was one of the largest refugee crises in recent history.
The United Nations, Fortify Rights, and numerous other international human rights organizations have documented widespread atrocities committed against the Rohingya, including genocide. The International Court of Justice is expected to issue its final judgment later this year in the case brought by The Gambia, which alleges that Myanmar violated the Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide.
The growing number of Rohingya seeking safety in Malaysia is the direct consequence of the world’s negligence and inaction in response to decades of persecution, exclusion, and violence in Myanmar. Mass atrocity crimes have been perpetrated with impunity for far too long. The crisis has long since crossed Myanmar’s borders and become a regional challenge affecting governments in South and Southeast Asia.
For that reason, Malaysia can no longer treat the Rohingya crisis as an internal matter of Myanmar. The principle of non-interference should not prevent action when mass atrocities, forced displacement, and statelessness have regional consequences. Malaysia must abandon its policy of non-interference with respect to the Rohingya crisis and confront Myanmar and all relevant actors to restore Rohingya citizenship, guarantee equal rights, provide legal protection, create safe conditions, and ensure the dignified return of refugees.
Last month, more than 400,000 people signed a petition on Change.org calling for the removal of Rohingya refugees from Malaysia. The petition is part of a widening hate campaign targeting Rohingya refugees and others, fed by fabricated allegations of criminality and dangerous misinformation about an already vulnerable community.

Even before the recent wave of hate, many refugees have described living in constant fear of arbitrary arrest, detention, and abuse by the Malaysian authorities. Rohingya refugees are particularly vulnerable to indefinite detention. A recent Fortify Rights investigation found that refugees had been detained without meaningful access to legal representation, fair judicial review, or any realistic prospect of release. Detainees are often subjected to ill-treatment and poor conditions, including inadequate healthcare, lack of clean water and food, and squalid conditions in detention, sometimes enduring such horrific conditions for years. Some have been forcibly repatriated to Myanmar or Bangladesh by Malaysian authorities.
Amid growing hostility against Rohingya refugees, Home Minister Saifuddin Nasution Ismail’s remarks on June 8 gave hope for a more constructive approach: “We cannot avoid managing this issue. It must be handled wisely and humanely, while safeguarding the country’s security, the people’s interests, and our international standing.”
articles.summary: Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim’s government has largely remained silent about the recent online "hate campaign" targeting Rohingya refugees.

articles.title: Philippine, Chinese Foreign Ministers Exchange Protests Over South China Sea Clash
articles.full_text:
Both nations have accused the other for instigating the incident at Second Thomas Shoal, which has overshadowed this week’s ASEAN meetings in Manila.
Philippine Foreign Secretary Ma. Theresa Lazaro and Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi shake hands during a meeting in Manila, Philippines, July 22, 2026.
The foreign ministers of the Philippines and China yesterday exchanged “strong” protests over an encounter between the two countries’ ‌vessels at a disputed shoal in the South China Sea earlier this week.
In a meeting with Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi on the sidelines of the 59th ASEAN Foreign Ministers’ Meeting in Manila, Foreign Secretary Ma. Theresa Lazaro reiterated the ​protest over China Coast Guard (CCG)’s “unacceptable actions against Filipino personnel,” she said in a post on X.
The incident in question took place on Monday at Second Thomas Shoal in the Spratly Islands, during a confrontation between Philippine marines and personnel from the CCG. The Armed Forces of the Philippines (AFP) claimed that a Philippine marine sustained a significant head injury after being struck with a baton by a CCG sailor.
According to the AFP, the confrontation took place after a CCG boat carrying eight personnel came within close range of the BRP Sierra Madre, a grounded Philippine warship that serves as the Philippines’ outpost on the shoal. Two Philippine dinghies then attempted “calmly and non-confrontationally” to drive it away, and a melee ensued. China disputed this claim, asserting that the Philippines side was to blame for instigating the incident and ramming the CCG boat.
During her meeting with Wang, Lazaro “conveyed the Philippines’s consistent and principled positions on the South China Sea, and raised concerns over actions that have infringed on the maritime rights and entitlements, including incidents affecting the safety of Filipino personnel,” the Philippine Department of Foreign Affairs (DFA) said in a statement. “The Philippines emphasized the need to exercise restraint, avoid actions that increase tensions, and resolve disputes peacefully in accordance with international law.”
Lazaro also registered her protest over a recent AI-generated video that depicted the Philippines as a karaoke-singing monkey. The 58-second video, which was posted online by the state-controlled China Daily on July 10, was “deeply offensive, distressing, and unacceptable,” the DFA paraphrased her as saying.
The video shows a timid monkey in Filipino traditional dress being forced by two faceless figures representing the United States and Japan to parrot the arbitral award that was handed down by a tribunal in The Hague in 2016.
While Chinese Ambassador Jing Quan attempted to distance his government from the China Daily video, Lazaro “emphasized that disagreements over legal and political issues do not justify resorting to disturbing imagery, drawing a firm line at the depiction of Filipinos as monkeys,” the DFA stated.
China’s Foreign Ministry later said that Wang registered his own “strong protest” over the incident at Second Thomas Shoal and urged the Philippines “to immediately stop provocative actions on maritime issues and make concrete efforts to stabilize bilateral relations,” Chinese state media reported.
Stating that the bilateral relationships was at a “crossroad,” Wang said that the Philippines needed to make a “correct and rational” choice regarding ​its future direction. He added that “allowing external forces to meddle in regional affairs and deliberately hyping up maritime disputes will only reduce the Philippines to a pawn manipulated by others.”
The incident near Second Thomas Shoal overshadowed this week’s ASEAN-related meetings in Manila. During a meeting with his Quad partners from the U.S, Australia, and India, Japanese Foreign Minister Toshimitsu Motegi voiced strong opposition to any attempts to change the status quo by force in the region, including in the South China Sea. Australian Foreign Minister Penny Wong concurred, adding that “We have real concerns about behavior that is destabilizing and risky.”
Canada’s Foreign Minister Anita Anand also described the incident near Second Thomas Shoal as “troubling,” adding, “We do not agree with intimidation as a means of addressing disputes.”
U.S. Secretary of State Rubio raised the issue in his own sideline meeting with Wang yesterday, stating that the incident was “escalatory” and “benefited no one.” He also reminded Wang that Washington had a defense treaty with Manila “and intends to abide by it.”
Rubio separately met with Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr., during which they “underscored the importance of upholding freedoms of navigation and overflight in the South China Sea,” the State Department said in its official readout. They also noted the recent 10th anniversary of Arbitral Award in the South China Sea, which ruled that China’s expansive maritime claims in the South China Sea had no basis under international law.
In spite of the recent incidents, Lazaro and Wang expressed their “continued willingness” to conclude negotiations on the long-expected ASEAN-China Code of Conduct for the South China Sea, according to the Philippine DFA. They also “underscored the importance of maintaining open channels of communication, enhancing mutual understanding, and strengthening confidence-building measures.”
The Code is intended to reduce the risk of conflict in the South China Sea, where China’s expansive “nine-dash line” maritime claim clashes with those of four ASEAN member states: Vietnam, Malaysia, the Philippines, and Brunei. It has been under negotiation for two decades, and pledges of its speedy completion are a hardy perennial of ASEAN-China meetings.
On Tuesday, ASEAN’s foreign ministers welcomed the progress in negotiations for the Code, and Malaysian Foreign Minister Mohamad Hasan later told reporters that it could be finalized at the 49th ASEAN Summit in Manila in November. Given the current state of tension between China and the Philippines, and the long line of lapsed deadlines, this very much remains to be seen.

articles.title: The First Japan-Brazil Foreign Ministers’ Strategic Dialogue
articles.full_text:
An important first step, with much work still ahead.
The Japan-Brazil Foreign Ministers’ Strategic Dialogue was held in Tokyo on May 18 between Japanese Foreign Minister Toshimitsu Motegi and his Brazilian counterpart Mauro Vieira. Both countries are working to strengthen their relationship under the “Strategic and Global Partnership Action Plan Japan-Brazil (2025-2030).” reflecting a shared belief that the relationship has substantial untapped potential.
With a population of over 200 million and roughly 2 million people of Japanese descent, Brazil has long been an important Latin American partner for Japan, as demonstrated by repeated visits from Japanese leaders and members of the Imperial Family. The relationship, however, has not lived up to its economic and political potential. Trade remains narrowly concentrated: Brazil exports iron ore, chicken, and coffee, while Japan exports automotive parts and machinery. Japan’s share of Brazil’s overall foreign trade has been declining (currently standing at 2 percent). The Japan Business Federation (Keidanren), which continues to engage in discussions with the industry in Brazil, has been pushing for an economic partnership agreement (EPA) with Mercosur (Southern Common Market) for over a decade, viewing Brazil as a crucial partner in leading regional integration.
As the U.S., once the standard-bearer of global free trade, turns toward protectionism, Brazil is moving in the opposite direction, having signed a free trade agreement with the EU in January after years of negotiations. This presents a real opportunity: Brazil seeks to diversify its trading partners, while Japan aims to grow trade volume, broaden the range of goods traded, and secure agricultural products and rare earths for economic security. The recent Strategic Dialogue, progress was made in discussions on entering EPA negotiations, and for Japan, it was a significant achievement to have Brazil’s agreement on economic security cooperation.
Among Asian powers, China has already established a close relationship with Brazil. Its rapid economic growth has driven significant imports of food, feed crops, and mineral resources from Brazil, alongside growing exports of Chinese goods. China has become Brazil’s largest trading partner by a wide margin over the U.S., with Chinese investment in clean energy also rising in recent years. That the Trump administration’s 50 percent tariff, introduced last summer to pressure Brazil, had minimal impact illustrates just how far U.S. leverage has diminished as China’s economic ties with Brazil have deepened. China and Brazil countries also stand together politically and diplomatically as leading voices of the Global South, opposing a U.S.-dominated world order.
Brazil, however, is pursuing a multipolar international order, and is not seeking to distance itself from Japan, nor treat it as an adversary, even as it deepens ties with China. Brazil’s foreign policy emphasizes autonomy and avoids excessive reliance on any single country, including China. Latin American nations were invited to join the Belt and Road Initiative in 2017, but Brazil has notably declined to participate, even as others in the region have signed on. If anything, the more Brazil depends on China, the more it seeks to diversify its economic relationships with other major partners, including Japan. Japan and Brazil also share common diplomatic objectives, including advocacy for U.N. Security Council reform, and uphold similar values, including democracy, human rights, and the non-use of force — values China does not share.
Brazil has long considered itself part of the West and is working toward OECD membership, positioning itself as a bridge between the Global North and South, collaborating with both. More recently, in Asia, Brazil it has been developing its strategic partnership with India, another major Global South power, into meaningful economic and technological collaboration. While unlikely to join the Free and Open Indo-Pacific (FOIP) initiative, given its China-containment focus, Brazil should nonetheless be able to embrace the underlying principles of freedom and the rule of law. Drawing Brazil closer to the Asia-Pacific and cultivating a productive strategic relationship would serve Japan’s national interests and contribute to a peaceful, stable regional order.
The recent Strategic Dialogue marks a meaningful step in that direction. Realizing the relationship’s latent potential, however, will require building social mechanisms that translate shared values and existing pro-Japanese sentiment into durable engagement. That means sustained investment, over the medium to long term, in people who understand both countries; continued effort from government and business organizations alike; and practical support for companies navigating the relationship. Young Brazilians can encounter Japanese manufacturing and craftsmanship through exhibitions at Japan House in São Paulo. For the Japanese government, the task is to give them a path to build careers, businesses, and friendships that bind the two countries together more closely.
