articles.title: China Coast Guard Exercises ‘Jurisdiction’ in Japan’s Exclusive Economic Zone
articles.full_text:
Japan now needs to formulate its response.
The Japanese Asahi Shimbun reported on June 9, 2026, that a China Coast Guard (CCG) vessel had been spotted navigating within Japan’s exclusive economic zone (EEZ) south of Yonaguni Island in Okinawa Prefecture, as announced at a press conference by Chief Cabinet Secretary Kihara Minoru that same day. Kihara explained that the CCG vessel had been detected by a Japan Coast Guard (JCG) patrol vessel. When the JCG queried the CCG vessel, it responded that it was conducting a “routine patrol” and asserted Chinese exercise of jurisdiction over the area. Moreover, China’s Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lin Jian confirmed at a regular press conference on June 9 that the CCG vessel had been “conducting patrols in accordance with the law,” before going on to declare that “Japan and the Philippines bypassing China to initiate the so-called maritime delimitation talks constitutes a severe violation of UNCLOS and other international laws and basic norms governing international relations. It also seriously infringes on China’s maritime rights and interests. China will by no means accept this.”
China has long made claims in Asian waters that are not grounded in the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), and since June this year it has been conducting “marine scientific research” (MSR) and patrols in accordance with Chinese domestic laws in Japan’s EEZ south of Yonaguni Island and east of Taiwan. The situation in these waters appears to be shifting from China merely asserting claims to a new phase of carrying out concrete activities — a gradual accumulation of facts on the water, as has been happening in the disputed parts of the South China Sea. The commencement of negotiations to delimit the maritime boundary between Japan and the Philippines has provided China with an “excuse” to advance to this new phase.
Chinese MSR conducted in Japan’s EEZ without Japanese consent violates UNCLOS and evidently constitutes a breach of international law.
Meanwhile, CCG “patrols” in Japan’s EEZ are more complex, and may not immediately be regarded as violations of international law.
Unlike territorial waters, coastal states possess sovereign rights and jurisdiction only over specific matters in the EEZ. The EEZ regime was created primarily to pursue the economic interests of coastal states. Article 56 (1) of UNCLOS stipulates the sovereign rights of coastal states over natural resources in the EEZ and places them at the core of the EEZ regime. Meanwhile, it stipulates that EEZ coastal states have jurisdiction over MSR, “the establishment and use of artificial islands, installations and structures” and “the protection and preservation of the marine environment.”
An EEZ is a maritime area where coastal states are granted sovereign rights and jurisdiction over what was conventionally considered high seas, and excepting the specific matters stipulated by UNCLOS, those areas retain the characteristics of high seas. Article 58 (1) of UNCLOS states that all countries enjoy the “freedoms of navigation and overflight” within EEZ. The same article also stipulates that the freedoms enjoyed by ships (including warships and other government vessels) are equivalent to the “freedom of the high seas” as stipulated in Article 87 (1) of UNCLOS. At the same time, Article 58(3) stipulates that any state is obliged under the EEZ to “In exercising their rights and performing their duties under this Convention in the exclusive economic zone, States shall have due regard to the rights and duties of the coastal State.”
Because of this, warships and other government vessels can exercise enforcement jurisdiction over their own vessels according to the flag state principle on the high seas, but when exercising such jurisdiction within a country’s EEZ, they are obliged to have “due regard” to the sovereign rights and jurisdiction of the coastal state of that EEZ.
The inclusion of the obligation to have “due regard” in Article 58 (3) of UNCLOS was a result of adjustments made in the convention drafting process. However, there remains ambiguity regarding which activities of EEZ non-coastal states may or may not be considered to fulfill this obligation.
Actions that fail to “have due regard” to the sovereign rights and jurisdictions of EEZ coastal states include, among other things, (1) obstructing the exercise of sovereign rights by EEZ coastal states for exploration, development, conservation, and management of natural resources and (2) obstructing the exercise of sovereign rights by EEZ coastal states for creating and using artificial islands, facilities, and structures.
Japan must carefully examine, analyze, and evaluate the specific contents of the CCG’s patrols conducted in the Japanese EEZ south of Yonaguni Island and east of Taiwan — including the purpose of those patrols and the Chinese domestic laws they purport to enforce — and, on the basis of those findings, take coherent measures against CCGs in the relevant maritime areas.

articles.title: The World Cup and the G2 Reality
articles.full_text:
The international sporting event showcased American strengths. It’s a reminder that both the U.S. and China should stop betting on each other’s decline.
A Fan Festival at the LA Memorial Coliseum for the first game of the U.S. Men’s National Team during the 2026 FIFA World Cup, June 12, 2026.
Some of the World Cup’s most revealing American moments came before and after U.S. matches. Players and tens of thousands of fans stood with hands over their hearts to sing the national anthem. After U.S. victories, they joined in “Take Me Home, Country Roads,” filling stadiums with a song about returning home.
The United States has not overcome polarization. A stadium is not a nation, and a song is not a political settlement. But as the World Cup comes to an end, the tournament offers an opportunity to look again at a country usually described through decline, dysfunction, and social fragmentation. It displayed great wealth, organizational capacity, and a sense of national belonging that American politics has not destroyed.
The United States hosted the tournament across 11 metropolitan areas largely through stadiums, airports, highways, and service systems it already possessed. Despite high ticket prices, demand remained intense; FIFA reported that stadiums were 99.6 percent full after the first 44 matches. Many visitors from Europe remarked on the scale and abundance of ordinary American life: large homes and cars, enormous supermarkets and sports facilities, reliable air-conditioning, free refills, and round-the-clock conveniences Americans barely notice.
American problems – household insecurity, homelessness, public debt, institutional paralysis, and political extremism – are real as well. The viral Chinese social media metaphor of an American “kill line,” in which one unexpected expense can push an ordinary family toward collapse, captures a real anxiety. But the World Cup also showed how incomplete that picture is.
A visitor to the United States in the late 1960s might easily have concluded that the country had lost its future. The civil rights struggle, the antiwar movement, urban unrest, political assassinations, campus protests, and the women’s liberation movement exposed deep racial, generational, and ideological conflict. Yet the decades that followed brought a technological revolution, renewed economic growth, major social change, and the eventual end of the Cold War on terms highly favorable to the United States.
That history should not be romanticized. Many injustices remained unresolved, and American power produced serious mistakes at home and abroad. But it shows a central feature of national resilience: the capacity to absorb crisis, adapt, and generate new sources of strength.
The United States still possesses deep reserves of such strength in technology, finance, higher education, entrepreneurship, alliances, and civil society. Its central weakness is not that it has run out of power. It is that polarization and institutional paralysis increasingly make it difficult to convert power into coherent and sustained policy.
In the same way, narratives of China’s weaknesses are overblown. Yes, China faces serious economic and social pressures: slower growth, a troubled property market, local government debt, an aging population, and weak consumer confidence. These challenges are substantial, but they do not erase the foundations of China’s resilience.
China’s rise was not an accident. It was built through more than four decades of reform and opening, the accumulation of infrastructure and industrial capacity, vast manufacturing networks, expanding technological capabilities, and generations of investment in education. China’s social resilience also draws in part on a Confucian cultural tradition that places unusual value on learning, hard work, family responsibility, and investment in the next generation. These qualities help explain why China has repeatedly adapted to hardship.
America’s success was not accidental, either. Both countries have benefited from accumulated institutions, social habits, human capital, and the hard work of their people. Both have serious internal problems. Neither should assume that those problems will automatically overwhelm the other’s capacity to recover and adapt.
Yet social media in both countries increasingly encourages precisely that conclusion. Chinese platforms circulate images of homelessness, political chaos, aging infrastructure, and social division as evidence that American decline is irreversible. As the United States marks its 250th anniversary, many discussions in Chinese media and policy circles have focused on national decline, lost hegemony, or the image of an aging beauty past her prime.
American platforms, meanwhile, focus on China’s property problems, local debt, slowing growth, and demographic pressures as proof that China has already peaked. Some in the United States believe that these economic and social pressures will sharply limit China’s future development and gradually remove the long-term strategic challenge it poses.
These narratives are most visible online, but they do not remain there. Assumptions about irreversible decline influence public expectations, enter elite debate, and narrow what policy communities regard as possible. Some in China may conclude that American polarization will eventually force Washington to retreat from long-term competition. Some in the United States may assume that China’s present economic difficulties will permanently restrict its future growth and strategic influence. In both cases, a partial picture of the other country becomes a comforting forecast of its future. 
Decline stories are attractive because they simplify difficult choices. If the rival is destined to fail, compromise looks unnecessary, restraint appears weak, and waiting begins to look like a strategy.
That temptation is especially dangerous given the emerging G2 reality. The G2 does not mean that Washington and Beijing will jointly govern the world, divide it into spheres of influence, or exclude other powers from major decisions. It describes a structural condition in which the United States and China are bound by competitive coexistence. Each can restrict, punish, and disrupt the other, but neither can dominate or exclude the other. 
In the western Pacific, the United States cannot push China out of its own region, but China cannot remove the United States and its alliances. In technology and trade, Washington can slow China’s access to some advanced capabilities, but it cannot exclude China from the global economy or prevent it from developing alternatives. Beijing can use its manufacturing scale, market power, and control of critical minerals to impose costs, but it cannot push the United States out of Asia’s economic order without imposing major costs on itself.
Recognizing the G2 reality means rejecting a dangerous idea that still appeals to some on both sides: the myth that achieving a decisive strategic victory over the other is both desirable and feasible. In both China and the United States, narratives emphasizing the other country’s decline trap make this belief look reasonable. However, a strategy built on the expectation of the other side’s eventual defeat is more likely to produce escalation than victory.
The World Cup did not negate the United States’ divisions, economic insecurity, or institutional dysfunction. It revealed that these problems coexist with great wealth, scale, organizational capacity, and a common identity that has not disappeared. The same intellectual discipline should shape U.S. assessments of China. China’s current economic difficulties are real, but so are China’s deep national capacity, accumulated experience, and social resilience.
Neither Washington nor Beijing can afford a strategy based on waiting for the other to fail. The first step toward peaceful coexistence is a harder form of realism: recognizing that the rival is likely to remain powerful, troubled, and resilient for a very long time.
Some of the World Cup’s most revealing American moments came before and after U.S. matches. Players and tens of thousands of fans stood with hands over their hearts to sing the national anthem. After U.S. victories, they joined in “Take Me Home, Country Roads,” filling stadiums with a song about returning home.
The United States has not overcome polarization. A stadium is not a nation, and a song is not a political settlement. But as the World Cup comes to an end, the tournament offers an opportunity to look again at a country usually described through decline, dysfunction, and social fragmentation. It displayed great wealth, organizational capacity, and a sense of national belonging that American politics has not destroyed.
The United States hosted the tournament across 11 metropolitan areas largely through stadiums, airports, highways, and service systems it already possessed. Despite high ticket prices, demand remained intense; FIFA reported that stadiums were 99.6 percent full after the first 44 matches. Many visitors from Europe remarked on the scale and abundance of ordinary American life: large homes and cars, enormous supermarkets and sports facilities, reliable air-conditioning, free refills, and round-the-clock conveniences Americans barely notice.
American problems – household insecurity, homelessness, public debt, institutional paralysis, and political extremism – are real as well. The viral Chinese social media metaphor of an American “kill line,” in which one unexpected expense can push an ordinary family toward collapse, captures a real anxiety. But the World Cup also showed how incomplete that picture is.
A visitor to the United States in the late 1960s might easily have concluded that the country had lost its future. The civil rights struggle, the antiwar movement, urban unrest, political assassinations, campus protests, and the women’s liberation movement exposed deep racial, generational, and ideological conflict. Yet the decades that followed brought a technological revolution, renewed economic growth, major social change, and the eventual end of the Cold War on terms highly favorable to the United States.
That history should not be romanticized. Many injustices remained unresolved, and American power produced serious mistakes at home and abroad. But it shows a central feature of national resilience: the capacity to absorb crisis, adapt, and generate new sources of strength.
The United States still possesses deep reserves of such strength in technology, finance, higher education, entrepreneurship, alliances, and civil society. Its central weakness is not that it has run out of power. It is that polarization and institutional paralysis increasingly make it difficult to convert power into coherent and sustained policy.
In the same way, narratives of China’s weaknesses are overblown. Yes, China faces serious economic and social pressures: slower growth, a troubled property market, local government debt, an aging population, and weak consumer confidence. These challenges are substantial, but they do not erase the foundations of China’s resilience.
China’s rise was not an accident. It was built through more than four decades of reform and opening, the accumulation of infrastructure and industrial capacity, vast manufacturing networks, expanding technological capabilities, and generations of investment in education. China’s social resilience also draws in part on a Confucian cultural tradition that places unusual value on learning, hard work, family responsibility, and investment in the next generation. These qualities help explain why China has repeatedly adapted to hardship.
articles.summary: The international sporting event showcased American strengths. It's a reminder that both the U.S. and China should stop betting on each other’s decline.

articles.title: India’s High-Stakes Gamble in the SCO
articles.full_text:
In an era of diminishing multilateralism, can the SCO actually bring results?
A group photo of national leaders during the SCO Summit 2025 in Tianjin, China, Sep. 1, 2025.
On July 19, India’s Minister for Culture Gajendra Singh Shekhawat, speaking in Cholpon-Ata, Kyrgyzstan, at the meeting of culture ministers from the member states of the Shanghai Cooperation Organization (SCO), framed culture as a “powerful force for dialogue, trust and sustainable development.” He reaffirmed India’s commitment to preserving the “shared civilizational heritage” and strengthening cooperation among the members of the group. The meeting ended with a gala concert and a guided tour of the Rukh-Ordo cultural complex, which serves as an exhibition venue to showcase Kyrgyzstan’s cultural heritage, history, and traditions. There was also a proposal for an SCO international theater festival.
Earlier, in April, the defense ministers of the SCO countries met in Bishkek. Next will be the foreign ministers meeting in Cholpon-Ata on July 23-24. Minister of State for External Affairs Kirti Vardhan Singh will represent India at the meeting, which will prepare the ground for the SCO Leadership Summit, scheduled to be held in Bishkek from August 31 to September 1. The foreign ministers will review progress in key areas of SCO cooperation and exchange views on matters of regional and international significance. On September 1, Kyrgyzstan will pass the chairmanship of the organization to Pakistan. 
The SCO just marked its 25th anniversary. It was originally founded in June 2001 when the “Shanghai Five” – China, Russia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, and Tajikistan – were joined by Uzbekistan and the name was changed to the Shanghai Cooperation Organization. The SCO was meant to catalyze collaborative efforts on regional, geopolitical, and geostrategic challenges. It has over the years added four more member states – India, Pakistan, Iran, and Belarus – bringing the total to 10. As a result, the organization is now the largest pan-Eurasian regional grouping. Not surprisingly, meetings of the SCO, especially those of the heads of states, continue to attract immense attention in the region and beyond. 
Nevertheless, beyond media headlines, the SCO’s actual performance and delivery potential on its 10 identified “goals and tasks” has remained suboptimal. Saddled with internal contradictions, divergent world views, and competing geoeconomic and security interests, the SCO has remained mostly a talk shop providing photo opportunities, diplomatic signaling, and at times hedging. 
On July 20, I spoke with Kirti Vardhan Singh, the minister who will represent India at the upcoming foreign ministers meeting, to probe where SCO figures in India’s strategic calculus. “India considers SCO as a structured multilateral forum as advantageous to India, in enhancing outreach with Central Asian nations to expand our presence and visibility in SCO geography at large,” Singh said. 
To that end, he added, “India continues to maintain its focus on areas of common interest and resolutely defends any move against India’s core interests that may emanate from certain SCO geographies.” 
Still, the fact remains that SCO mirrors most of the deficiencies that plague any large multilateral grouping. The lack of unanimity on key goals such as “joint[ly] combating terrorism” and “jointly searching for solutions to the problems that would arise in the 21st century” makes the SCO long on rhetoric but short on delivery. 
In particular, the India-Pakistan differences repeatedly cast a long shadow on the SCO’s workings, as do China-India disagreements concerning the China-Pakistan Economic Corridor (CPEC). During the defense ministers’ meeting in Bishkek, India’s Defense Minister Rajnath Singh spoke about “fractured world views” of “inward-looking countries,”  but also about how India “demonstrated its firm resolve that terrorism epicenters are no longer immune to justifiable punishment” – indirectly hinting at Pakistan. 
On July 17, India participated in the 12th Meeting of the Heads of Border Services of SCO member states, hosted in Islamabad under Pakistan’s chairmanship. The meeting was meant to exchange assessments on border security and finalize plans for the SCO’s joint border operation, “Solidarity 2026.” New Delhi’s long-standing apprehension that the SCO remains a forum for China-Pakistan nexus vis-à-vis India is bound to grow once the baton of SCO leadership passes over to Islamabad for the next year.   
Further, the wars in Ukraine and Iran have obligated the SCO to take a position on conflicts that have affected the economies of the world. This is in sync with the SCO’s identified goal of “cooperating in the prevention of international conflicts and their peaceful settlement.” However, having parties to these conflicts like Russia and Iran as members hasn’t led to much progress. 
It could be argued that such a state is hardly unique to SCO. Similar challenges afflict other such large groupings where members fail to balance collective goals against individual national interests. Multilateralism around the world has come under increasing strain and is mostly faltering. 
Existing global forums hardly reflect the economic and power realities of the changing world order. There is no agreement among nations over existential global issues, no agreement on fair trade rules, and no qualms about using violence to settle differences. Global institutions don’t have the funds to address the dire needs of the Global South in areas like health, education, and climate, even as billions are poured into military spending and ceaseless wars that serve only to secure the short-term interests of great powers.
Can regional organizations be an alternative to the downward trend of multilateralism? The SCO is surely facing a litmus test on this count.
There have been calls in Indian strategic circles for India to withdraw from the SCO and form its own regional organization. The reason why New Delhi has ignored such opinions isn’t merely due to its trust in the potential of the grouping but due to the advantages the SCO provides. Singh told me that India’s participation “enhances New Delhi’s outreach with Central Asian nations to expand its presence and visibility in SCO geography at large.” This allows India to loop in Afghanistan as a bridge into regional trade and connectivity projects to help the economic stabilization of the country. 
Moreover, the SCO’s avowed priorities against terrorism, separatism, extremism, and radicalization through the Regional Anti-Terrorist Structure (RATS) have helped mainstream India’s approaches on these matters, a matter of substantive national interest, even when the organization’s ability to take a common stand is a rarity. 
Within the SCO framework, India continually advocates for robust mechanisms to tackle transborder terrorism and extremism, as well as for fair trade, secure supply chains, and regional connectivity. India also pushes to make English an official SCO language to broaden global representation, while reaffirming its commitment to shared civilizational heritage. This needs to be contextualized within New Delhi’s substantial investment in regional forums for a rules-based regional as well as global order. India has advocated for open, inclusive and transparent connectivity projects such as the International North South Transport Corridor (INSTC), which potentially provides leeway to regional countries to choose their preferred connectivity projects. 
What’s true for India also holds true for the other SCO members. Each has tried to leverage the organization to amplify its interests and used the forum to advance bilateral as well as minilateral interests. It is this advantage that keeps the SCO going. Twenty-five years on, the SCO endures because of these quiet calculations of individual national gain. Now, it is time for this pan-Eurasian organization to step up to the plate and address the needs of the region collectively.
On July 19, India’s Minister for Culture Gajendra Singh Shekhawat, speaking in Cholpon-Ata, Kyrgyzstan, at the meeting of culture ministers from the member states of the Shanghai Cooperation Organization (SCO), framed culture as a “powerful force for dialogue, trust and sustainable development.” He reaffirmed India’s commitment to preserving the “shared civilizational heritage” and strengthening cooperation among the members of the group. The meeting ended with a gala concert and a guided tour of the Rukh-Ordo cultural complex, which serves as an exhibition venue to showcase Kyrgyzstan’s cultural heritage, history, and traditions. There was also a proposal for an SCO international theater festival.

The foreign ministers will review progress in key areas of SCO cooperation and exchange views on matters of regional and international significance. On September 1, Kyrgyzstan will pass the chairmanship of the organization to Pakistan.

articles.title: Why a Tibetan Protester Set Himself on Fire Outside the United Nations
articles.full_text:
Loga Rangzen’s self-immolation was intended to force Tibet back onto the international agenda. Instead, it exposed the challenges Tibetans face amid China’s growing global influence.
Members of New York’s Tibetan community protest outside the United Nations three weeks after Lobga Rangzen’s self-immolation, calling for renewed international attention to Tibet.
Lobga Rangzen chose perhaps the world’s most recognizable symbol of international diplomacy as the place of his death. Dressed in traditional Tibetan clothing, he planted a Tibetan flag on the sidewalk across from the entrance to the the United Nations headquarters in New York on July 2. There, he set himself on fire, livestreaming his self-immolation on Facebook. His final act was intended to draw attention to Tibet’s decades-long struggle under Chinese rule.
Rangzen, 52, was born in Tibet and fled to India in the 1990s, where he lived as a monk before immigrating to the United States in 2006. Over the next two decades, he became an active member of New York’s Tibetan community. His self-immolation was the first known by a Tibetan on U.S. soil.
The protest came one day after China implemented its Law on Promoting Ethnic Unity and Progress. Amnesty International argued that the legislation further institutionalizes assimilation policies targeting Tibetans, Uyghurs, Mongolians, and other ethnic minorities while expanding the legal basis for transnational repression.
Although Rangzen’s self-immolation was intended to draw international attention to Tibet, the response from the very institution he chose to stand before was limited.
Rangzen’s choice of site was carefully considered. For more than seven decades, Tibetans have looked to the United Nations in hopes that the international body would address China’s rule over Tibet. The Tibetan government first appealed to the U.N. in 1950 after Chinese forces entered Tibet. Although the issue reached the General Assembly, the organization took no immediate action. After the People’s Republic of China assumed China’s U.N. seat in 1971, Tibet gradually disappeared from the organization’s political agenda.
For Tenzin Dorjee, a researcher at the Tibet Action Institute and lecturer in political science at Columbia University, Rangzen’s decision was a direct appeal to the international community rather than to Beijing. “Instead of choosing the Chinese consulate, he purposely chose the United Nations as his site of protest,” Dorjee said. “The U.N. is the institution that is supposed to represent the international community’s promotion of peace, defense of human rights, and protection of cultural rights. I think he went to the U.N. because it was an appeal to the international community.”
At a U.N. press briefing the day after the self-immolation, spokesperson Stéphane Dujarric described the incident as “absolutely tragic” and expressed condolences, but did not mention Tibet or the political message behind Rangzen’s protest. Beyond that brief acknowledgment, neither the United Nations nor most governments issued substantive public responses to an act intended to draw renewed attention to Tibet.
For many Tibetans, that silence reinforced a long-held frustration. “The fact that the U.N. spokesperson refused to mention Tibet reaffirms the suspicion of the Tibetan community that the U.N. has been silent on the Tibet issue,” said Dorjee.
Amnesty International was among the few international human rights organizations to publicly respond. “Our thoughts are with everyone who knew and loved the man who has died and the broader Tibetan community,” said Sarah Brooks, Amnesty International’s deputy regional director for Asia. 
“Self-immolation as a protest by Tibetans has persisted for many years, and it does not happen in a vacuum,” she added. “It reflects the depth of desperation felt by people who see no other way to draw attention to ongoing human rights violations.”
New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani also acknowledged Rangzen’s protest, saying he was “heartbroken” by the death. “No one should feel that such a desperate act is the only way to make the world listen,” Mamdani wrote on X. “New York stands for the dignity and human rights of all people.”
Why Tibet Has Faded From the International Agenda
Although Rangzen’s self-immolation drew international headlines in the days immediately following his self-immolation on July 2, attention quickly faded. Yet demonstrations continued. In New York, Tibetans gathered regularly outside the United Nations and the Chinese Consulate, while solidarity protests and prayer vigils were organized by Tibetan communities in India, Europe, and cities across the United States. 
For many activists, the brief burst of media attention underscored a larger challenge: how to keep Tibet on the international agenda once the headlines move on.
Three Tibetan youth activists affiliated with Students for a Free Tibet chained themselves to the vehicular entrances of U.N. headquarters, demanding that the organization publicly address Rangzen’s protest and ongoing human rights concerns in Tibet. The activists blocked U.N. vehicles before being arrested on charges of disorderly conduct and refusal to disperse. Hundreds of members of New York’s Tibetan community gathered outside the U.N. in support.
Among those arrested was Tsela Zoksang, campaigns director at Students for a Free Tibet. 
“His final act took place outside of the United Nations, a building that generations of Tibetans have stood outside of for 67 years in exile, calling on it to uphold its mandate of protecting peace, freedom, and human rights,” Zoksang said. “In the case of Tibet, it has failed miserably.”
For Dorjee, the issue is not simply whether the United Nations responds, but what those responses represent.
“The statements that have come from the U.N. are inadequate, for sure, but they are not ineffective,” he said.
The General Assembly adopted resolutions on Tibet and U.N. human rights experts raised concerns in 2023 over the separation of an estimated one million Tibetan children into state-run boarding schools. Those interventions have produced few immediate policy changes, yet Dorjee argued that even limited statements from international institutions remain significant.
“It may not lead to direct changes on the ground immediately, but what it does is it denies legitimacy to the Chinese government,” he said. “It denies legitimacy to Chinese rule over Tibet.”
For many Tibetan advocates and scholars, the United Nations’ muted response to Rangzen’s protest reflects a broader shift in which Tibet has steadily receded from the organization’s political agenda while China’s influence within international institutions has grown. Those concerns extend beyond Tibetan advocacy groups. A March 2026 report by the U.S. House Select Committee on the Chinese Communist Party argued that Beijing has expanded its influence across the U.N. system through growing financial contributions, leadership positions in U.N. agencies, participation in peacekeeping operations, and networks of state-linked organizations active in U.N. forums. The report argued that these efforts have enabled China to shape discussions on issues it considers politically sensitive, including human rights. 
China has rejected allegations that it improperly influences the United Nations, while many scholars note that the organization’s decisions ultimately reflect negotiations among its member states rather than the actions of any single country. Nevertheless, the House committee’s findings added to a broader debate over how major powers shape international institutions.
Dorjee said China’s growing diplomatic and economic influence has made many governments increasingly reluctant to criticize Beijing’s policies in Tibet – and elsewhere.
He pointed to Beijing’s diplomatic efforts in Africa, Latin America, and the Pacific Islands, where 10 countries have shifted diplomatic recognition away from Taiwan over the past decade. Dorjee argued that similar economic and political leverage has discouraged governments from speaking more forcefully on Tibet.
“China uses the influence or the attraction of trade to get countries to turn their back on Taiwan,” he said. “This is precisely the same tactic they try to use on Tibet as well.”
However, Dorjee believes China’s influence is only part of the explanation for Tibet’s diminishing international visibility.
“People are basically giving their lives in order to make their voices heard,” he said. “Tibetans have felt for a very long time that their suffering is being ignored by the world. The repression inside Tibet has intensified while international attention has declined.”
articles.summary: Loga Rangzen’s self-immolation was intended to force Tibet back onto the international agenda. Instead, it exposed the challenges Tibetans face amid China's growing global influence.

articles.title: Indian Army’s Integrated Battle Groups Are Crystallizing ‘Cold Start’ Doctrine
articles.full_text:
Given the uncertainty in the region’s escalation ladder, IBGs may be the answer if the need for a quick ground retort were to arise.
Indian Army soldiers near the Line of Actual Control in Arunachal Pradesh, India.
“Let me be clear that we cannot fight the next war like we fought our last,” said late General Bipin Rawat, then the Indian Army chief, in 2018. Rawat, who later became India’s first Chief of Defense Staff (CDS), spoke of four transformation efforts that he initiated in the Indian Army, one of which was the creation of Integrated Battle Groups (IBGs) for swift strikes. This was finally realized on July 1, when the Indian Army formally raised the first five IBGs and a Fire Support Group with long-range firepower. The new units are under the Eastern Command’s 17 Mountain Strike Corps facing China and located in two states, Arunachal Pradesh and Sikkim. This enables the Indian Army to launch a blitzkrieg option. The China focus underscores where India sees its biggest threats.
The process is expected to take a year to complete. Eventually, IBGs will also be created in other sectors.
IBGs are meant to be agile, all-encompassing strike formations that can mobilize at a short notice of 12-48 hours. An IBG has around 5,000 personnel, which places it between a typical brigade (3,000-3,500) and division (10,000-12,000). Headed by a major general-rank officer, the key factor is that an IBG has all elements incorporated— infantry, armor, artillery, air defense, logistics, and so on—making it self-sufficient for swift action. Each IBG would be tailored on three Ts — Threat, Terrain and Task — as stated by officials in the past.
The doctrine and concept of IBGs have been extensively test-bedded and validated under the Yol-based 9 Corps on the Western front and the 17 Mountain Strike Corps during exercises. However, the larger modalities and the formal sanction from the government were quite delayed, so much so that in January 2025, then-Indian Army Chief Gen. Upendra Dwivedi said that a timeline was difficult to give because “wherever the bureaucracy gets involved, it will take time.” He added: “We are hopeful that by 2025, we will either create this IBG or cancel this project completely.” With the formal government sanction letter now issued, the process has commenced.
The initial ideation on IBGs was heavily focused on Pakistan, especially given its continued support for cross-border terrorism and the prevailing “no war, no peace” scenario.
However, the initial IBGs raised are facing China and that too on the eastern front, and not the Ladakh sector where the two countries have seen significant tensions since the 2020 standoff that is still underway. It is in sync with India’s recent focus on strengthening military posture and defenses in this axis given China’s increased activity and provocations in the eastern sector, and its advantage in asymmetry in areas facing the 3 Corps area. The topography and terrain in the region being mountainous makes swift mobilization a challenge for India, and an IBG gives the maneuverability for quick shallow ingress to hold swathes of territory in case of any misadventure by the People’s Liberation Army.
The one resounding lesson from the 2020 standoff with China is that Beijing only talks when it is at a disadvantage and the other side holds leverage. China started serious negotiations toward ending the standoff only after the tactical actions by the Indian Army along the North and South Banks of Pangong Tso in late August 2020. The 3,488-km-long Line of Actual Control (LAC) between India and China is divided into three sectors — the western sector spanning Ladakh, the middle sector comprising Himachal Pradesh and Uttarakhand, and the eastern sector, comprising Sikkim and Arunachal Pradesh.
The IBGs are organized on similar lines to the Combined Arms Brigades (CAB) of the People’s Liberation Army (PLA), created as part of its 2017 reforms. Like the IBG, the CAB consists of 5,000-6,000 personnel and brings together various strike and support elements. Since the 2020 standoff, the PLA has deployed CABs in Tibet facing Eastern Ladakh.
The IBGs also find mention in the Indian Army’s Land Warfare Doctrine 2018, which states that when forced to prosecute a conventional war, all combat operations will function as IBGs, under the command of a combined arms operational headquarters.
The IBGs fit into the overall transformation of the Indian Army in tune with the realities of changing warfare. This effectively formalizes and also upgrades the long espoused “Cold Start” doctrine. Its existence was acknowledged by Rawat in January 2017. Cold Start was formulated from the debacle of Operation Parakram, launched after the terrorist attack on the Indian Parliament in 2001. The Army’s strike formations that were deep in the hinterland took weeks to mobilize, resulting in a cascade effect — loss of the element of surprise, the international community stepping in, and Pakistan resorting to nuclear signaling. Cold Start was seen as an instrument to make space for swift conventional operations under the nuclear overhang of the subcontinent.
The IBGs follow several other transformative initiatives rolled out by the Army in the last year, post-Operation Sindoor in May 2025. They include Rudra Brigades, Bhairav Battalions, Shaktibaan drone regiments and Divyastra drone batteries for the Regiment of Artillery. Last September, current Army Chief Gen. Dhiraj Seth, then a lieutenant general and heading the Southern Army Command, observed that with the Rudra brigade, the “time has come to change the cold start to cold strike one.” The IBGs certainly give a new impetus to this.
As witnessed during Operation Sindoor, the days of large-scale mobilization are over, with conflict moving to long-range, precision strikes from standoff ranges. Large-scale boots on the ground and ground maneuvers across borders seem passé from recent conflicts. However, given the uncertainty in the escalation ladder in the subcontinent, IBGs may be the answer if the need for a quick ground retort were to arise.
While Rawat’s initiative as army chief finally takes shape, his bigger effort, the reorganization of the military into Integrated Theater Commands as India’s first CDS, is now moving forward.
“Let me be clear that we cannot fight the next war like we fought our last,” said late General Bipin Rawat, then the Indian Army chief, in 2018. Rawat, who later became India’s first Chief of Defense Staff (CDS), spoke of four transformation efforts that he initiated in the Indian Army, one of which was the creation of Integrated Battle Groups (IBGs) for swift strikes. This was finally realized on July 1, when the Indian Army formally raised the first five IBGs and a Fire Support Group with long-range firepower. The new units are under the Eastern Command’s 17 Mountain Strike Corps facing China and located in two states, Arunachal Pradesh and Sikkim. This enables the Indian Army to launch a blitzkrieg option. The China focus underscores where India sees its biggest threats.
The process is expected to take a year to complete. Eventually, IBGs will also be created in other sectors.
IBGs are meant to be agile, all-encompassing strike formations that can mobilize at a short notice of 12-48 hours. An IBG has around 5,000 personnel, which places it between a typical brigade (3,000-3,500) and division (10,000-12,000). Headed by a major general-rank officer, the key factor is that an IBG has all elements incorporated— infantry, armor, artillery, air defense, logistics, and so on—making it self-sufficient for swift action. Each IBG would be tailored on three Ts — Threat, Terrain and Task — as stated by officials in the past.
The doctrine and concept of IBGs have been extensively test-bedded and validated under the Yol-based 9 Corps on the Western front and the 17 Mountain Strike Corps during exercises. However, the larger modalities and the formal sanction from the government were quite delayed, so much so that in January 2025, then-Indian Army Chief Gen. Upendra Dwivedi said that a timeline was difficult to give because “wherever the bureaucracy gets involved, it will take time.” He added: “We are hopeful that by 2025, we will either create this IBG or cancel this project completely.” With the formal government sanction letter now issued, the process has commenced.
The initial ideation on IBGs was heavily focused on Pakistan, especially given its continued support for cross-border terrorism and the prevailing “no war, no peace” scenario.
However, the initial IBGs raised are facing China and that too on the eastern front, and not the Ladakh sector where the two countries have seen significant tensions since the 2020 standoff that is still underway. It is in sync with India’s recent focus on strengthening military posture and defenses in this axis given China’s increased activity and provocations in the eastern sector, and its advantage in asymmetry in areas facing the 3 Corps area. The topography and terrain in the region being mountainous makes swift mobilization a challenge for India, and an IBG gives the maneuverability for quick shallow ingress to hold swathes of territory in case of any misadventure by the People’s Liberation Army.
The one resounding lesson from the 2020 standoff with China is that Beijing only talks when it is at a disadvantage and the other side holds leverage. China started serious negotiations toward ending the standoff only after the tactical actions by the Indian Army along the North and South Banks of Pangong Tso in late August 2020. The 3,488-km-long Line of Actual Control (LAC) between India and China is divided into three sectors — the western sector spanning Ladakh, the middle sector comprising Himachal Pradesh and Uttarakhand, and the eastern sector, comprising Sikkim and Arunachal Pradesh.
The IBGs are organized on similar lines to the Combined Arms Brigades (CAB) of the People’s Liberation Army (PLA), created as part of its 2017 reforms. Like the IBG, the CAB consists of 5,000-6,000 personnel and brings together various strike and support elements. Since the 2020 standoff, the PLA has deployed CABs in Tibet facing Eastern Ladakh.
The IBGs also find mention in the Indian Army’s Land Warfare Doctrine 2018, which states that when forced to prosecute a conventional war, all combat operations will function as IBGs, under the command of a combined arms operational headquarters.
The IBGs fit into the overall transformation of the Indian Army in tune with the realities of changing warfare. This effectively formalizes and also upgrades the long espoused “Cold Start” doctrine. Its existence was acknowledged by Rawat in January 2017. Cold Start was formulated from the debacle of Operation Parakram, launched after the terrorist attack on the Indian Parliament in 2001. The Army’s strike formations that were deep in the hinterland took weeks to mobilize, resulting in a cascade effect — loss of the element of surprise, the international community stepping in, and Pakistan resorting to nuclear signaling. Cold Start was seen as an instrument to make space for swift conventional operations under the nuclear overhang of the subcontinent.
The IBGs follow several other transformative initiatives rolled out by the Army in the last year, post-Operation Sindoor in May 2025. They include Rudra Brigades, Bhairav Battalions, Shaktibaan drone regiments and Divyastra drone batteries for the Regiment of Artillery. Last September, current Army Chief Gen. Dhiraj Seth, then a lieutenant general and heading the Southern Army Command, observed that with the Rudra brigade, the “time has come to change the cold start to cold strike one.” The IBGs certainly give a new impetus to this.
As witnessed during Operation Sindoor, the days of large-scale mobilization are over, with conflict moving to long-range, precision strikes from standoff ranges. Large-scale boots on the ground and ground maneuvers across borders seem passé from recent conflicts. However, given the uncertainty in the escalation ladder in the subcontinent, IBGs may be the answer if the need for a quick ground retort were to arise.
While Rawat’s initiative as army chief finally takes shape, his bigger effort, the reorganization of the military into Integrated Theater Commands as India’s first CDS, is now moving forward.
“Let me be clear that we cannot fight the next war like we fought our last,” said late General Bipin Rawat, then the Indian Army chief, in 2018. Rawat, who later became India’s first Chief of Defense Staff (CDS), spoke of four transformation efforts that he initiated in the Indian Army, one of which was the creation of Integrated Battle Groups (IBGs) for swift strikes. This was finally realized on July 1, when the Indian Army formally raised the first five IBGs and a Fire Support Group with long-range firepower. The new units are under the Eastern Command’s 17 Mountain Strike Corps facing China and located in two states, Arunachal Pradesh and Sikkim. This enables the Indian Army to launch a blitzkrieg option. The China focus underscores where India sees its biggest threats.
The process is expected to take a year to complete. Eventually, IBGs will also be created in other sectors.
articles.summary: Given the uncertainty in the region's escalation ladder, IBGs may be the answer if the need for a quick ground retort were to arise.

articles.title: Keep an Eye on China’s AI Governance Offensive
articles.full_text:
Beijing’s intensifying efforts to shape international norms on AI are aimed at catching up on the technology and exporting China’s illiberal values.
China’s President Xi Jinping (front center, in blue suit) poses for a group photo with other world leaders at the 2026 World AI Conference in Shanghai, China, July 17, 2026.
Beijing’s intensifying efforts to shape international norms on AI are calibrated to promote itself as a responsible global leader, but they are largely self-serving, aimed at catching up on the technology and exporting China’s illiberal values. Democracies need to get their act together in response.
President Xi Jinping delivered a keynote speech at the 2026 World AI Conference and High-Level Meeting on Global AI Governance on July 17, making the case for the promotion of AI globally “for the positive, for good and for humanity.” He called for “a people-centered approach,” saying that “AI is an important driver for shared prosperity and common security” that required states to join hands “to build a just and equitable system for global AI governance.”
So Xi said all the right words. After all, China has never been short on its rhetoric. At last year’s edition of the conference, Premier Li Qiang made a strong case for global regulations governing AI using language designed to appeal to the Global South.
Noting the vast opportunities and challenges that come from the adoption of new technologies, Xi raised four points on AI development and governance: he promoted openness, collaborating, and sharing; recognized the various risks associated with AI adoption and the resultant need for AI always to be under human control; encouraged inclusiveness and the promotion of mutual learning between civilizations; and called for true multilateralism and an important role for the United Nations as well as a consensus-based governance framework in writing new global rules and technical standards.
His emphasis on international cooperation and openness has definite appeal. But it also appears to be somewhat self-serving, as China still depends on others. Henry Gao, a professor of law at Singapore Management University, said that China “wants access to global models, talent, chips, research, open-source ecosystems and technical knowledge because that is what it needs at this stage.” Alexander Evans, former cyber head at Britain’s Foreign and Commonwealth Office and former adviser to then-Prime Minister Boris Johnson, similarly noted that China needed international cooperation because “no country has complete vertical control.”
Xi’s opposition to “overstretching the national security concept in the field of AI” is a rather brazen effort to target U.S. restrictions on allowing China to access the United States’ AI technology. This is particularly rich considering Beijing’s own role in securitizing crucial assets such as trade, data, finance, markets, and access to rare earths – areas where China has deliberately created advantages for itself.
Much of the speech was aimed at the Global South. These nations will appreciate Xi’s call for international cooperation to help them with capacity building to avoid AI capability gaps and bridge existing digital divides. But they should not be fooled: Xi’s efforts are geared toward exporting what it considers responsible norms, rules, and standards, which will pay no heed to universal principles such as human or political rights.
Xi has convinced some regional blocs to support China’s vision by saying that, in the next five years, it will assist developing countries “with 5,000 opportunities in AI training and seminar programs “and “develop international AI application cooperation centers with the Association of Southeast Asian Nations, the League of Arab States, the African Union, the Community of Latin American and Caribbean States, the Shanghai Cooperation Organization, and BRICS.” It’s too early to say how this will play out and whether these organizations will endorse the emerging Chinese version of the AI order.
China is already globally exporting its tech, such as 5G network gear, drones, smart cars, and renewable energy equipment. Democracies cannot afford for it to succeed similarly with AI.
In his speech, Xi also announced the establishment of the World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization for facilitating international cooperation on AI governance and development. On July 16, 29 countries – including, Pakistan, Russia, Malaysia, and Indonesia – became founding signatories. This is an impressive achievement for China, but its future remains to be seen.
Remember that China’s efforts in global governance of other domains haven’t always borne fruit. China and Russia proposed a draft Treaty on the Prevention of the Placement of Weapons in Outer Space and of the Threat or Use of Force Against Outer Space Objects in 2008 and a revised text was introduced in 2014, but there haven’t been many takers. Even those countries that have argued for legally binding mechanisms did not support the treaty. This may show how China’s AI governance journey could pan out.
While countries should be concerned that without any regulations, AI will do more harm than good, it is also necessary to come up with the right norms and rules. Allowing China – an illiberal, authoritarian, and aggressive great power with a penchant for centralization and hegemony – to determine the rules for AI governance is likely to be dangerous everywhere, including in the Global South. Xi’s speech should sound the tocsin to liberal powers across the world to get their act together. These powers need to collectively strengthen AI governance, including by promoting ethical and responsible use of the technology, before China can write the global AI rulebook.
This article was originally published in ASPI’s The Strategist.

articles.title: How South Korea’s Africa Pledges Are Faring on the Ground
articles.full_text:
In western Uganda, a project funded by the Korea International Cooperation Agency (KOICA) serves as a test case for what South Korea can offer the continent.
Participants in the program “Support for Vocational Training Schools for Persons with Disabilities and Raising Disability Awareness in Western Uganda,” funded by KOICA and KFHI.
In early June, foreign ministers and senior representatives from 50 African countries gathered in Seoul for the first Korea-Africa Foreign Ministers’ Meeting, the follow-up mechanism promised at the inaugural Korea-Africa Summit of 2024. At that summit, Seoul pledged to expand its official development assistance (ODA) to the continent to $10 billion by 2030, alongside $14 billion in export financing. The June meeting closed with a joint statement reaffirming those commitments and a proposal to convene a second summit in 2029. 
Notably, the initiative has survived the most turbulent political transition in South Korea’s recent history. Conceived under the previous conservative government, the Africa pivot has been carried forward by the progressive administration that followed, which framed the June gathering as part of its pursuit of diplomatic diversification. Two years in, the architecture to back up the Korea-Africa summit is visibly taking shape, and it belongs to no single administration.
Skepticism, however, has shadowed the initiative from the outset, and it is not unfounded. Africa still accounts for a mere 1 to 2 percent of South Korea’s trade and investment, a shortfall acknowledged openly at the 2024 summit itself. The agenda at the foreign ministers’ meeting this June, which ranged from critical minerals to supply chain resilience, looked like an inventory of what South Korea seeks from the continent rather than what it offers. Observers have pointed out that South Korea is a latecomer replicating the summit diplomacy of China and Japan, and that pledges made at such gatherings have a well-documented tendency to outrun actual disbursement. Judged by communiqués alone, it remains an open question whether South Korea’s engagement with Africa amounts to anything more than resource diplomacy in the language of development.
Nevertheless, summit halls are a poor vantage point from which to assess a development partnership. A more instructive view can be found some 10,000 kilometers from Seoul, in the districts of western Uganda, where a project funded by the Korea International Cooperation Agency (KOICA) has spent the past two years testing what a distinctly South Korean contribution might look like. The field is not minerals or megaprojects but one of the least contested and most neglected corners of the development agenda: vocational training for persons with disabilities. 
The project, implemented with a Ugandan partner organization and the National Council for Persons with Disabilities, supports students with disabilities at three vocational institutes across districts as they train in trades ranging from tailoring and carpentry to welding and hairdressing. The support itself is deliberately unglamorous: tuition, training materials, assistive devices, and preparation for Uganda’s government-certified trade examinations, which allow workers to earn a living in their chosen craft.
The project’s second-year results exceeded most of its target indicators. In the first year, 146 students with disabilities enrolled across three technical institutes; 145 remained in training. The goal is that graduates will convert certification into employment or self-employment – and that surrounding communities will come to regard such an outcome for persons with disabilities as ordinary rather than exceptional.
The project may look small compared to South Korea’s pledged $10 billion in funding. Disability-inclusive development generates no minerals, secures no shipping lanes, and produces few headlines; it is absent from the playbooks of the great power competition now unfolding across the continent. Why does such a modest project in an overlooked area merit attention in a discussion of grand strategy? 
In a conversation with the author, Kim Hyo-je, deputy country director of KOICA’s Uganda office, acknowledged the underlying reality: persons with disabilities are “the most marginalized among the marginalized,” a population overlooked not only by geopolitics but, all too often, by mainstream development programming itself. That double neglect, however, is an opening. If South Korea’s declared comparative advantage in Africa is its own development experience, then technical and vocational education, which served as an engine of the country’s postwar transformation, is arguably where that experience translates most credibly when extended to the population most systematically excluded from it. It signals, in a way that no critical minerals dialogue can, that the partnership is not organized solely around extraction.
The setting matters as well. On paper, Uganda’s disability legislation is among the more progressive on the continent, but the gap between statute and implementation is wide and, in places, widening. The 2020 revision of the Persons with Disabilities Act quietly dropped the earlier requirement that no less than 10 percent of educational expenditure be committed to special needs education. Tax incentives for employers of persons with disabilities have been diluted from a 15 percent reduction under the 2006 Act to what amounts, under current tax law, to a 2 percent deduction. The statutory instrument required to operationalize the employment quota has never been issued. In such an environment, where implementation of existing policy is the need of the hour, patient, field-level cooperation can make a meaningful difference.
Suppose, however, that the donor exits tomorrow. This is the question that every development project must eventually answer, and the honest response is that no one can be certain what would remain. Any project claiming otherwise should be treated with suspicion. What can be said is that this initiative has structured itself around that eventuality rather than ignoring the possibility. Instead of building parallel structures, it works through Uganda’s own statutory disability council. Instead of simply delivering assets, it has cultivated a coalition of local disability organizations intended to carry coordination and advocacy beyond the funding cycle. A small ICT hub nearing completion in Kasese illustrates the logic: the project financed the basic renovation, a local disability union furnished the space, and the national council secured the equipment. No single actor owns it, and therefore no single actor’s departure can close it.
Needless to say, none of this guarantees sustainability, and the uncertainties do not all originate in Uganda. South Korea’s own ODA architecture is in flux: civil society partnership schemes are being restructured, and multi-year projects can see their planned scope narrowed midstream. A pivot measured in decades will have to reconcile the long horizons that inclusion requires with the short cycles in which aid is budgeted and rebranded. It is in that tension, not in the size of the next pledge, that the seriousness of Seoul’s Africa policy will be tested.
When leaders reconvene for the proposed 2029 summit, the temptation will be to announce larger numbers. The better measure of the partnership will be whether the commitments of 2024 actually materialized on the ground. In western Uganda, the 145 students with disabilities who stayed in training, along with the certificates, workshops, and livelihoods that will follow, offer one early answer. 
For South Korea, disability inclusion is not a philanthropic footnote to its Africa policy. It may be the most credible evidence available that the partnership is what its declarations claim it to be.

articles.title: Beyond a ‘Non-Red’ Supply Chain, Taiwan Should Export a Drone Ecosystem
articles.full_text:
To build a real “drone shield,” Taiwan cannot merely export hardware or components.
A drone on display at the Taipei International Aerospace  Defense Industry Exhibition at the Nangang Exhibition Center in Taipei, Taiwan, Sep. 20, 2025.
Taiwan’s government has made drone development and exports a national priority, with a particular emphasis on Taiwan’s role in the “non-red” supply chain: drones and components free of Chinese units. Taiwan’s Executive Yuan has framed this goal as part of Taiwan’s effort to become an Asia-Pacific hub for “democratic drone supply chains.” The policy reflects lessons from the Russia-Ukraine war, Taiwan’s own need to strengthen deterrence against China, and growing demand among countries and firms globally worried about the security risks of Chinese-made systems.
The logic behind this move is obvious: Chinese firms, especially DJI, still dominate the global commercial drone market, even as the U.S.  Department of Defense has stated that DJI systems pose potential national security threats. Such security concerns have created opportunities for alternative suppliers. Taiwan’s “non-red” drones are often more expensive than Chinese competitors, but the market is beginning to emerge. Taiwan’s drone exports have surged in Poland and Czechia, with possible links to wartime usage in Ukraine. Recent trade data also showed a record sale of Taiwanese drones to Saudi Arabia. 
Taiwan has advantages in the global drone market. Its semiconductor, ICT, battery, and precision manufacturing sectors give it a strong foundation for drone production. Its small and medium enterprises can move and adapt quickly. Industry leaders and politicians increasingly speak of the sector as a possible “drone shield,” a next-generation complement to Taiwan’s “silicon shield.”
However, if drones are to become a real shield that connects Taiwan more deeply with the world, Taiwan cannot merely export hardware or components; it needs to export an entire drone ecosystem.
First, in the military domain, the drone itself is only one part of overall combat effectiveness. A serious drone capability also requires operator training, simulation systems, testing fields, certification standards, logistic pipelines, secure communications protocols, software update channels, and compatibility with partners’ weapon systems. These aspects determine whether drones can be used flexibly under real battlefield pressure. 
Taiwan’s military has already begun moving in this direction. The army has formed drone battalions in several combat zones, and its drone training center has been upgraded into an Unmanned Systems Training Command responsible for education, testing, evaluation, and standards. But the key point here is whether Taiwan can build and export a complete doctrine, training, testing, and sustainment system that is insulated from Chinese interference. 
Second, drone design and algorithms are deeply shaped by operating environments. Taiwan’s geography offers unusually rich testing conditions. The Taiwan Strait brings strong winds and humidity. Taiwan’s cities combine intertwined residential and commercial spaces with communication dead zones. The island also has high-salt sea air, afternoon thunderstorms, and steep mountains. Taiwan could use its own geography as a living laboratory for drone testing, training, and adaptation. 
Third, Taiwan must make “non-red” more than a slogan. Some reporting has worried that not all Taiwan-made drones are free of Chinese components. If Taiwan wants to compete on trust rather than price, it needs credible tracing, testing, documentation standards, and certification mechanisms that foreign buyers can understand and examine. 
Fourth, Taiwan should more actively develop and export the legal and regulatory side of drone use. Taiwan is not at war yet, and both the military and civilians are already using drones more frequently. As a democracy, Taiwan has an opportunity to show how drone governance can balance civil empowerment, public safety, and national defense. 
In Taiwan, the Civil Aviation Administration has rules covering drone registration, operator certification, activity applications, insurance, and flight restrictions. These regulations should be examined and updated regularly with the advance of drone technology and practice. Meanwhile, they can also become part of Taiwan’s exportable knowledge: how a democracy integrates civilian drone use, emergency response, commercial innovation, and security concerns without treating every drone operator as a suspect.
The early signs of such an ecosystem are already there. Civil defense groups such as Kuma Academy have started drone-related training. Disaster-response organizations and local fire departments are using drones for rescue and emergency operations. Drone soccer is also spreading into schools and youth programs. These efforts are still small, but they show that drones are becoming part of Taiwan’s society and the foundation of the ecosystem.
These aspects are where Taiwan can build a long-term advantage in the drone ecosystem. It will be difficult to beat Chinese competitors purely on cost, but Taiwan can compete on trust, adaptability, training, certification, and democratic governance.
Three steps would make this strategy more concrete. First, Taiwan could consider building overseas maintenance, training, and testing hubs. Second, Taiwan could develop a transparent non-red certification system. Third, Taiwan should integrate civil defense, disaster response, education, and commercial users into its drone policy rather than treating them as separate from national security. These efforts would strengthen Taiwan’s economic security, defense cooperation, and international relevance, making the “drone shield” become more than a slogan.
Taiwan’s government has made drone development and exports a national priority, with a particular emphasis on Taiwan’s role in the “non-red” supply chain: drones and components free of Chinese units. Taiwan’s Executive Yuan has framed this goal as part of Taiwan’s effort to become an Asia-Pacific hub for “democratic drone supply chains.” The policy reflects lessons from the Russia-Ukraine war, Taiwan’s own need to strengthen deterrence against China, and growing demand among countries and firms globally worried about the security risks of Chinese-made systems.
The logic behind this move is obvious: Chinese firms, especially DJI, still dominate the global commercial drone market, even as the U.S.  Department of Defense has stated that DJI systems pose potential national security threats. Such security concerns have created opportunities for alternative suppliers. Taiwan’s “non-red” drones are often more expensive than Chinese competitors, but the market is beginning to emerge. Taiwan’s drone exports have surged in Poland and Czechia, with possible links to wartime usage in Ukraine. Recent trade data also showed a record sale of Taiwanese drones to Saudi Arabia. 
Taiwan has advantages in the global drone market. Its semiconductor, ICT, battery, and precision manufacturing sectors give it a strong foundation for drone production. Its small and medium enterprises can move and adapt quickly. Industry leaders and politicians increasingly speak of the sector as a possible “drone shield,” a next-generation complement to Taiwan’s “silicon shield.”
However, if drones are to become a real shield that connects Taiwan more deeply with the world, Taiwan cannot merely export hardware or components; it needs to export an entire drone ecosystem.
First, in the military domain, the drone itself is only one part of overall combat effectiveness. A serious drone capability also requires operator training, simulation systems, testing fields, certification standards, logistic pipelines, secure communications protocols, software update channels, and compatibility with partners’ weapon systems. These aspects determine whether drones can be used flexibly under real battlefield pressure. 
Taiwan’s military has already begun moving in this direction. The army has formed drone battalions in several combat zones, and its drone training center has been upgraded into an Unmanned Systems Training Command responsible for education, testing, evaluation, and standards. But the key point here is whether Taiwan can build and export a complete doctrine, training, testing, and sustainment system that is insulated from Chinese interference. 
Second, drone design and algorithms are deeply shaped by operating environments. Taiwan’s geography offers unusually rich testing conditions. The Taiwan Strait brings strong winds and humidity. Taiwan’s cities combine intertwined residential and commercial spaces with communication dead zones. The island also has high-salt sea air, afternoon thunderstorms, and steep mountains. Taiwan could use its own geography as a living laboratory for drone testing, training, and adaptation. 
Third, Taiwan must make “non-red” more than a slogan. Some reporting has worried that not all Taiwan-made drones are free of Chinese components. If Taiwan wants to compete on trust rather than price, it needs credible tracing, testing, documentation standards, and certification mechanisms that foreign buyers can understand and examine. 
Fourth, Taiwan should more actively develop and export the legal and regulatory side of drone use. Taiwan is not at war yet, and both the military and civilians are already using drones more frequently. These efforts are still small, but they show that drones are becoming part of Taiwan’s society and the foundation of the ecosystem.
These aspects are where Taiwan can build a long-term advantage in the drone ecosystem. It will be difficult to beat Chinese competitors purely on cost, but Taiwan can compete on trust, adaptability, training, certification, and democratic governance.
Three steps would make this strategy more concrete. First, Taiwan could consider building overseas maintenance, training, and testing hubs. Second, Taiwan could develop a transparent non-red certification system. Third, Taiwan should integrate civil defense, disaster response, education, and commercial users into its drone policy rather than treating them as separate from national security. These efforts would strengthen Taiwan’s economic security, defense cooperation, and international relevance, making the “drone shield” become more than a slogan.
