articles.title: Amnesty: Deadly Pakistan Air Strikes on Kabul Drug Rehab Center a Possible War Crime
articles.full_text:
The human rights organization’s new report found “no evidence of Pakistan’s claims” that the site hit on March 16 was a military target.
Four months after calling on Pakistan’s authorities to explain how and why they targeted a Kabul drug rehabilitation center on March 16, killing at least 269 civilians, Amnesty International has published a new investigation calling for the attack to be investigated as a possible war crime. 
The organization found no evidence to support the Pakistan military’s claims that Omid Rehabilitation and Treatment Center was being used to store weapons and ammunition or for other military purposes at the time of the attack. The air strikes violated the special protection afforded to healthcare facilities under international humanitarian law, as well as the principles of distinction, precaution and proportionality designed to protect civilians.
“The attack on the Omid Center constitutes a serious violation of international humanitarian law – possibly amounting to a war crime. Given the widely available information that the site had been used as a medical facility for more than a decade, this reflects a major failure in military planning by Pakistan that led to a significant loss of civilian life,” said Isabelle Lassee, Amnesty International’s acting regional director for South Asia.
“This was a lethal failure by Pakistan to comply with its obligation to do everything feasible to verify whether the target was a military objective. Pakistan must urgently conduct a transparent, thorough, independent, and impartial investigation into the attack – especially the decision-making process that led to the targeting of the facility – and make the results public.
“Where sufficient evidence exists, competent authorities should prosecute any person suspected of criminal responsibility, including under the doctrine of command responsibility.”
The attack was one of a series of air strikes conducted by the Pakistan military inside Afghanistan since the escalation of cross-border hostilities with the Taliban in October 2025. Between January and March 2026 alone, the United Nations Assistance Mission in Afghanistan (UNAMA) documented more than 750 civilian casualties, with 372 killed and 392 others injured in Afghanistan. The June 28 airstrikes in Paktya, Paktika, and Kunar provinces killed at least 28 civilians according to UNAMA. 
While there is no consolidated figure for civilian casualties in Pakistan, media reports indicate that at least seven civilians have been killed through mortar shelling originating from Afghanistan territories.
“All parties to the conflict must respect international humanitarian law and take all feasible precautions to protect civilians and civilian objects, with particular care afforded to medical facilities and other specially protected sites. Failure to investigate serious violations, such as the attack on the Omid Center, shows an alarming disregard for international law and risks entrenching impunity, leading to further unlawful operations and loss of civilian life,” said Lassee.
Amnesty International analyzed and verified more than 60 photos and videos, most of which were captured in the immediate aftermath of the attack on the Omid Center, including videos released by Pakistan’s military to support its claim that the site was being used as a storage facility for weapons and ammunition. The organization also reviewed more than 30 satellite images of the area, as well as verified videos documenting the operations at the Omid Center prior to the attack.
Amnesty International conducted 11 interviews, including with employees and former employees of the Omid Center and the adjacent Ibn-e-Sina Hospital. Interviewees also included individuals who visited the center immediately after the attack and victims’ family members. Some of the interviewees witnessed the strikes, and some were also involved in the evacuation effort.
On July 9, Amnesty International contacted the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Pakistan to request details about what the government has done to investigate any failure in targeting, and whether those involved in the process were held to account, including any information to substantiate their claim that the target was a “weapon and ammunition store.” No response had been received at the time of publication.
Prohibition of Attacks on Medical Facilities
The principle of distinction in international humanitarian law requires parties to distinguish between civilians and combatants, and between civilian objects and military objectives. Deliberate attacks against healthcare facilities and on their personnel are specifically prohibited, unless a facility is being used outside its humanitarian function. When there is doubt as to whether medical facilities are being used to commit acts “harmful to the enemy” – the only case in which they could lose their protected status – they should be presumed not to be so used.
In a post on X the day after the strike, Attaulah Tarar, Pakistan’s federal minister for information and broadcasting, claimed that the Pakistan military had targeted: “technical support infrastructure and ammunition storage facilities” in two locations in Kabul (one of them being the Omid Center). He also claimed that secondary detonations indicated that large ammunition depots were hit in the strikes.
Videos and photographs of bomb remnants shared directly with Amnesty International were analyzed by the organization’s weapons experts, who concluded that at least one of the munitions used was a Takbir precision-guided bomb. This finding is consistent with statements by the Pakistani military that precision-guided munitions were used in the strike, suggesting that the attack was the result of a deliberate targeting decision.
The Omid Center was established in 2016 on the site of a former NATO base. It originally operated as a vocational training facility for people being treated for substance dependence. After the Taliban returned to power in 2021, the site was expanded into a 2,000-bed treatment center for people with drug-dependence problems. Ibn-e-Sina Hospital, which provided essential healthcare services to Omid Center patients, is also located on the site.
There is a wide range of publicly available information on the Omid Center’s work, including a 2023 Al Jazeera documentary showing patients undergoing treatment at the center.
Amnesty International identified at least two large in-local-language signboards, located above the main gate and inside the camp, clearly indicating that the location was a rehabilitation and treatment center. The organization also verified a video recorded in 2025 and captured inside the center, showing some of the hangars where patients were housed, and analyzed satellite images of the Omid Center between April 2023 and March 15, 2026. On March 13, just three days prior to the attack, imagery shows large groups of people present.
“The Pakistani authorities should have known the facility was a civilian object and that any aerial attack could result in large-scale civilian casualties. Even if the Pakistani military had any reliable information of military use, it was clear that the civilian harm would be excessive,” said Lassee.
The strikes occurred at around 9 p.m., just as the evening tarawih prayers for Ramadan were being concluded, according to people who spoke to the organization. On the night of the attack, according to several interviewees who worked in the center, more than a thousand patients were housed there.
Under international humanitarian law, attacking forces have an obligation to do everything feasible to verify whether their intended target is a military objective and, if there is doubt in this respect, to refrain from launching an attack or to cancel or suspend it. Even if a medical facility is being used for non-humanitarian purposes, an attack can only be justified after an effective warning has been issued and ignored. Amnesty International found no indication that any warning was issued, and the timing of the attack at evening prayers ensured civilian presence and left no opportunity for evacuation.
In his post on X, Tarar, Pakistan’s information minister, included several videos that he said corroborated the Pakistani military’s claims that the site was being used to store weapons and ammunition.
Amnesty International’s open-source investigators and weapons experts analyzed these videos, as well as evidence directly received from local sources and online. The videos consist of black and white drone and fighter aircraft feeds that show the air strikes from various angles. None of these videos, nor footage gathered from ground sources, allow the identification of conclusive evidence of weapons, ammunition, or other explosive goods stored in the area. The footage does not show secondary explosions immediately following the strikes, which would constitute evidence of a possible ammunition storage.
“Our investigation strongly indicates that this was not a legitimate military target under international humanitarian law. The ‘evidence’ Pakistan has made public so far does not rebut the conclusion that this was an unlawful attack causing harm to civilians and civilian objects,” said Lassee.
Digital evidence and witness testimonies indicate that at least three locations within the site were heavily damaged or destroyed as a result of the air strikes.
Satellite imagery from March 13 shows the Omid Center three days before the strikes. The east and west sides of the complex are separated by a controlled gate. Many people are visible on the east side of the gate in the imagery, which according to testimony is the main living area for patients.
The March 23 satellite imagery shows three heavily damaged and burned areas. The video posted by Tarar shows three of the impacted buildings being struck: the large building in the northeast and two buildings in the northwest. Satellite imagery also shows heavy burning and destruction in the southwest.
At one of these locations, the destruction included a large hangar situated in the northeast of the site. Several interviewees said that the site housed patients undergoing treatment, as well as the kitchen and dining hall. Videos and photos captured in the immediate aftermath of the attack and during the evacuation process indicate that the dead bodies were mostly recovered from this area.
A second location, in the northwest of the site, was used by Taliban security personnel and for food storage, according to several interviewees. The third location, in the southwest of the site, was, according to several sources, used for storing items such as coal, wood, and essentials such as clothing. It also accommodated a tailoring center.
articles.summary: The human rights organization’s new report found “no evidence of Pakistan’s claims” that the site hit on March 16 was a military target.

articles.title: The Limits – and Dangers – of the China-Russia Partnership
articles.full_text:
The Diplomat author Mercy Kuo regularly engages subject-matter experts, policy practitioners, and strategic thinkers across the globe for their diverse insights into U.S. Asia policy. This conversation with Mathieu Boulègue – senior fellow with the Transatlantic Defense and Security Program at the Center for European Policy Analysis (CEPA) and co-author of “The China-Russia Meta Threat: The Architecture of Authoritarian Power” (CEPA 2026) – is the 518th in “The Trans-Pacific View Insight Series.”
How have China-Russia relations changed since the start of the Ukraine war in 2022?
I think the relationship evolved significantly after 2014 and Russia’s first invasion of Ukraine, rather than in 2022 per se, after the full-scale invasion. The weight of international sanctions and Moscow’s growing isolation pushed Russia closer to China. This consequently forced the Kremlin to replace Western dependence with dependence on Beijing and effectively created a new form of over-reliance. 
Over-reliance applies to economic subsidies, investments, and in the military sphere, to the procurement – legal and illegal – of dual-use and military technology, spare parts, and equipment. Supply chain dependency is particularly evident in microelectronics, semiconductors, circuitry, and machine-building tools used to sustain Russia’s war machine. Now the issue for Moscow is that trading one dependence for another is not ideal, but they don’t have much choice but to court China for continued support. 
Examine the “asymmetric interdependence” of China-Russia economic relations. 
The report we recently published underscores the idea that Russia can only be a “junior partner” to China, and never an equal. No amount of great power propaganda from either side can hide the fact that Russia needs China more than China needs Russia. 
Such a relationship, far from being “no limit,” is also quite fragile. The Russian leadership is perfectly aware of the increasingly asymmetric nature of bilateral relations. Meanwhile, Beijing must navigate the intricacies of using Russia as a barking dog while avoiding antagonizing Moscow or bruising Putin’s ego. Call it genuine authoritarian diplomacy. 
Military cooperation might be the area where Russia is the least dwarfed by China, mainly because Beijing is in a listen-and-learn mode. 
Analyze the “parallel play” nature of military cooperation between China and Russia.
“Parallel play” is an interesting concept, reflecting how kids in early childhood tend to play side by side but never fully together. I think it illustrates precisely how Russia and China approach military cooperation at large – interaction without the full extent of actual interaction. 
Western analyses generally tend to assume Russia-China military relations should take the form of what we see at NATO level for instance, and we tend to placate known concepts of cooperation such as “interoperability” or “strategic alliances.” Beijing and Moscow don’t need any of that to have meaningful military cooperation. 
Western-style interoperability is not something they are trying to replicate. They drill together, conduct joint patrols and overflights, etc. But it does not mean they are seeking to go further with joint security guarantees, integrated command and control, or joint operations. Actually, there are areas where their respective military interests may clash, such as in Africa, Central Asia, or the Arctic.
Identify the obstacles to closer China-Russia security ties. 
It’s important to frame Russia-China relations by what they don’t do together rather than by how they cooperate. Friction points, limitations, and obstacles in this relationship equally define the nature of their interaction. There are several obstacles in defense and security ties that can be made out. 
First, historical mistrust. There’s a long history of mistrust, especially from the Russian side, regarding military-technical reverse engineering and technology theft. Furthermore, Moscow is none too happy about perceived Chinese inroads in what the Kremlin defines as its “sphere of influence,” for instance in Central Asia and in the Arctic.
Second, the absence of shared strategic culture that could cement greater integration. Simply put, Russia thrives on military coercion, while Beijing is more subtle in its sharp power. Both countries have focused on ironing out the easy things (for instance, border issues, nonaggression, and institutional and legal frameworks) but they have not pushed for deeper military integration. From a geographical perspective, the centers of gravity are also misaligned – Russia focuses on the “NATO border,” while China focuses on the Asia-Pacific region. 
Finally, a key factor is that both Moscow and Beijing are too independent to enter an alliance that could infringe on their sovereignty and impose self-restraint. They will not be fighting each other’s wars anytime soon.
Assess the semblance of China-Russia “strategic alignment” on the trajectory of global governance and implications for the United States and European Union. 
I don’t think the alignment is “strategic” in the sense that we generally understand it. Russia-China relations are not those of NATO, the EU, or ASEAN, for that matter. It remains a fundamentally opportunistic relationship based on self-interest and scoring short-term wins. 
On top of that, they both want us to talk about it – symbols and signaling are just as crucial as substance in their relations. Beijing and Moscow want to inflate the reality of their cooperation as a form of informational-psychological warfare. This is part and parcel of how they frame their relation as anti-U.S. Yet if we look at “strategic” aspects, such as future governance models, trade, etc., it appears both countries are not aligning on long-term goals. 
It remains that even a modicum of cooperation between both countries represents a potential threat to U.S. and European interests. Exploring the limitations of the relationship should not overshadow the force-multiplier effect it has in disrupting Western governance. 
The Diplomat author Mercy Kuo regularly engages subject-matter experts, policy practitioners, and strategic thinkers across the globe for their diverse insights into U.S. Asia policy. This conversation with Mathieu Boulègue – senior fellow with the Transatlantic Defense and Security Program at the Center for European Policy Analysis (CEPA) and co-author of “The China-Russia Meta Threat: The Architecture of Authoritarian Power” (CEPA 2026) – is the 518th in “The Trans-Pacific View Insight Series.”

articles.summary: Insights from Mathieu Boulègue.

articles.title: America Can’t Win the AI Race Behind Closed Doors
articles.full_text:
With China’s latest launch of frontier model Kimi K3, the U.S. must also focus on open source AI.
China just fired another shot in the most important technological competition of the 21st century – and Washington should pay attention.
Last week, Chinese startup Moonshot AI unveiled Kimi K3, a powerful open-source AI model that independent evaluators say rivals or even exceeds some of the latest systems from OpenAI and Anthropic. At nearly the same moment, Chinese President Xi Jinping stood before an international audience in Shanghai and proclaimed that artificial intelligence should not be “a solo performance by a single country but a symphony of global collaboration.”
This was no coincidence. China is making a strategic bet that open-source AI – not closed-source – will determine who wins the global technology race.
The United States should be making the same bet.
The geopolitical stakes could hardly be higher. Artificial intelligence is rapidly becoming the defining technology of our era, with implications for economic growth, military power, scientific discovery, and national security. To keep its edge, Washington has focused on restricting China’s access to advanced semiconductors. Yet while the United States fights to maintain its lead in computing power, China is competing on something equally important: adoption.
History shows that the technologies that become global standards are often the ones that are easiest to use, modify, and build upon. Linux became the backbone of the internet. Android dominates the world’s smartphones. Both were open-source software; they created ecosystems rather than customers.
Chinese companies such as Moonshot, DeepSeek, Zhipu, and Alibaba are increasingly releasing powerful open AI models that anyone with the right technical skills can download, modify, and deploy. Every developer, startup, university, and foreign government that builds on those systems becomes part of China’s expanding technological ecosystem.
Meanwhile, the United States’ leading AI firms are moving in the opposite direction.
Ironically, the company called OpenAI is no longer open. Its original charter emphasized broad access and ensuring that artificial intelligence benefits all of humanity. Today its most advanced models are proprietary, accessible only through paid interfaces and cloud services. 
Anthropic has adopted a similar strategy, arguing that keeping frontier AI closed is necessary for safety. But commercial considerations appear to be the more compelling explanation.
Safety considerations for such powerful tools deserve rigorous attention. But concentrating the world’s most important technology inside a handful of private corporations creates risks of its own. Artificial intelligence has become increasingly controversial because so much power is being concentrated in so few hands. 
Fortunately, this is not the only path forward.
Open-source models are rapidly becoming more efficient. They increasingly require less computing power and cost less to operate. That means lower barriers for startups, universities, hospitals, and manufacturers, and broader participation across the economy.
Open-source models foster competition and innovation while closed-source ones hinder it. Startups can customize open-source models for specialized industries. Researchers can inspect them for flaws. Hospitals can run them securely without transmitting sensitive patient data to outside companies. Defense agencies can audit their behavior rather than relying on the black boxes of closed AI. Security researchers can identify vulnerabilities before adversaries do.
Perhaps most importantly, open-source AI democratizes innovation itself. Some of the internet’s greatest breakthroughs did not come from established technology giants. They came from unexpected innovators building on open foundations. Nobody predicted that Linux would become the operating system powering much of the global internet. Nobody foresaw Android’s dominance when it began as an open-source project. The same generative innovation is possible with artificial intelligence – but only if the underlying technology remains accessible.
This is not simply an economic argument. It is a democratic one. When only a handful of companies control the models, the computing infrastructure, and increasingly the information people consume, transparency becomes more difficult, competition weakens, and public trust erodes. Open systems allow independent experts to examine how models work, identify bias, improve security, and hold developers accountable in ways proprietary systems simply cannot.
The United States still possesses enormous advantages. It leads the world in advanced chips, venture capital, research universities, entrepreneurial talent, and high-performance computing. But those strengths will matter only if they translate into broad national capability rather than remaining locked behind proprietary walls.
If Washington allows short-term corporate incentives to dictate long-term national strategy, it risks repeating a familiar mistake. Closed-source models may maximize revenue for a few companies, but they do little to spread innovation throughout the broader economy. Worse, they create an opening for Chinese open-source models to become the world’s default platform.
China has recognized that global influence comes not merely from building the best AI, but from building the AI that the world chooses to use.
China just fired another shot in the most important technological competition of the 21st century – and Washington should pay attention.
Last week, Chinese startup Moonshot AI unveiled Kimi K3, a powerful open-source AI model that independent evaluators say rivals or even exceeds some of the latest systems from OpenAI and Anthropic. At nearly the same moment, Chinese President Xi Jinping stood before an international audience in Shanghai and proclaimed that artificial intelligence should not be “a solo performance by a single country but a symphony of global collaboration.”
This was no coincidence. China is making a strategic bet that open-source AI – not closed-source – will determine who wins the global technology race.
The United States should be making the same bet.
The geopolitical stakes could hardly be higher. Artificial intelligence is rapidly becoming the defining technology of our era, with implications for economic growth, military power, scientific discovery, and national security. To keep its edge, Washington has focused on restricting China’s access to advanced semiconductors. Yet while the United States fights to maintain its lead in computing power, China is competing on something equally important: adoption.
History shows that the technologies that become global standards are often the ones that are easiest to use, modify, and build upon. Linux became the backbone of the internet. Android dominates the world’s smartphones. Both were open-source software; they created ecosystems rather than customers.
Chinese companies such as Moonshot, DeepSeek, Zhipu, and Alibaba are increasingly releasing powerful open AI models that anyone with the right technical skills can download, modify, and deploy. Every developer, startup, university, and foreign government that builds on those systems becomes part of China’s expanding technological ecosystem.
Meanwhile, the United States’ leading AI firms are moving in the opposite direction.
Ironically, the company called OpenAI is no longer open. Its original charter emphasized broad access and ensuring that artificial intelligence benefits all of humanity. Today its most advanced models are proprietary, accessible only through paid interfaces and cloud services. 
Anthropic has adopted a similar strategy, arguing that keeping frontier AI closed is necessary for safety. But commercial considerations appear to be the more compelling explanation.
articles.summary: With China’s latest launch of frontier model Kimi K3, the U.S. must also focus on open source AI.

articles.title: Security Issues Are Overshadowing Kashmir’s Worsening Climate Crisis
articles.full_text:
Kashmir’s mountains are going to keep getting hotter whether anyone notices or not.
Popular perceptions of Kashmir center on soldiers, checkpoints, and decades-old fights over land. Sometimes tourism edges into the discourse. Far less attention is paid to the fact that Kashmir’s mountains are heating up rapidly.
A new study published in Nature’s Scientific Reports pulled together and analyzed 45 years of weather records from 1980 to 2024 and collected surface temperature records from ten India Meteorological Department stations. It found that the high mountain towns of Pahalgam and Gulmarg, which are also famous tourist sites, have warmed by nearly a full degree Celsius in just the last 20 years. One town, Bhaderwah, is heating up even faster at roughly 0.3 degrees per decade. And the nights are warming fastest of all. 
Meanwhile, down in the flat lowland city of Jammu, temperatures have barely moved. Some seasons there have even gotten a little cooler.
Scientists have a name for this pattern: elevation-dependent warming. Simply put, the higher up a mountain you go, the faster it warms. This happens for a simple reason. Snow is white and bounces sunlight back into space. When snow melts away, it uncovers dark rock and dirt underneath, and dark surfaces soak up heat instead of bouncing it away. Less snow means more heat, which means even less snow. It is the same thing scientists have already found happening on the Tibetan Plateau, the giant high plain north of the Himalayas. Kashmir is  as much a part of that story – but it rarely gets talked about.
This is partly because of logistics. It is hard to maintain, much less to expand weather stations in Kashmir, thanks to its checkpoints, restricted roads, and internet shutdowns. But it’s also a dynamic of Kashmir’s contested status – and not for the obvious reasons. Money and attention follow whatever the world already agrees to worry about. In international discourse, Kashmir is often treated primarily as a tale of “conflict” or “repression,” rather than a “climate” or “environment” issue. 
As a result, Kashmir is under-represented in global discussions of the warming Himalayan region, even though some researchers, journalists, and local advocates have long highlighted the region’s environmental and climate impacts. And because Kashmir’s climate gets less attention, it also doesn’t get the funding, the research teams, or the adaptation help that “hotspot” regions get.
A person in Kashmir uses a tiny fraction of the energy an average person in the United States or Europe uses. They didn’t build the factories or drive the cars that heated up the planet. But they are the ones watching the snow disappear earlier every year. A farmer plants his crops based on when the water usually arrives, and now it arrives late, or all at once in a dangerous flood, or barely at all. 
But this isn’t only Kashmir’s problem. The mountains of the wider Himalayan region hold the third-largest amount of frozen freshwater anywhere outside the North and South Poles. Up to 2 billion people depend on rivers originating in the Hindu Kush Himalaya region, and these rivers are sustained by annual ice melt. When the ice and snow don’t come back in the winter, that means reduced water flows into the rivers powering and feeding much of South Asia.
There are three steps to take, right now, to begin addressing Kashmir’s climate crisis. 
First, scientists need real support to study Kashmir’s mountains the same way they already study Nepal and the rest of the Himalayas, or the rest of India. Whatever they learn should be shared openly (like the Scientific Reports paper) in open access, followed by funds allocation for mitigation and adaptation.
Second, stop treating demilitarization and climate adaptation as separate conversations in the Kashmir region. They are the same conversation, because who gets to gather information and who gets to act on it are both, in the end, questions of power.
Third, when countries meet at big climate conferences and talk about who should pay to help places already hurt by climate change, Kashmir needs to be part of that conversation – both from the climate justice side as well as colonialism side. Someone has to ask directly: who pays to fix the water systems and rebuild after floods in a place still caught in conflict?
Kashmir’s unresolved future cannot be an excuse for ignoring what is happening to its mountains right now. Kashmir’s mountains are going to keep getting hotter whether anyone notices or not. The glaciers are not waiting for a diplomatic settlement, and neither should the people whose water and lands depend on them.
Popular perceptions of Kashmir center on soldiers, checkpoints, and decades-old fights over land. Sometimes tourism edges into the discourse. Far less attention is paid to the fact that Kashmir’s mountains are heating up rapidly.
A new study published in Nature’s Scientific Reports pulled together and analyzed 45 years of weather records from 1980 to 2024 and collected surface temperature records from ten India Meteorological Department stations. It found that the high mountain towns of Pahalgam and Gulmarg, which are also famous tourist sites, have warmed by nearly a full degree Celsius in just the last 20 years. One town, Bhaderwah, is heating up even faster at roughly 0.3 degrees per decade. And the nights are warming fastest of all. 
Meanwhile, down in the flat lowland city of Jammu, temperatures have barely moved. Some seasons there have even gotten a little cooler.
Scientists have a name for this pattern: elevation-dependent warming. Simply put, the higher up a mountain you go, the faster it warms. This happens for a simple reason. Snow is white and bounces sunlight back into space. When snow melts away, it uncovers dark rock and dirt underneath, and dark surfaces soak up heat instead of bouncing it away. Less snow means more heat, which means even less snow. It is the same thing scientists have already found happening on the Tibetan Plateau, the giant high plain north of the Himalayas. Kashmir is  as much a part of that story – but it rarely gets talked about.
This is partly because of logistics. It is hard to maintain, much less to expand weather stations in Kashmir, thanks to its checkpoints, restricted roads, and internet shutdowns. But it’s also a dynamic of Kashmir’s contested status – and not for the obvious reasons. Money and attention follow whatever the world already agrees to worry about. In international discourse, Kashmir is often treated primarily as a tale of “conflict” or “repression,” rather than a “climate” or “environment” issue. 
As a result, Kashmir is under-represented in global discussions of the warming Himalayan region, even though some researchers, journalists, and local advocates have long highlighted the region’s environmental and climate impacts. And because Kashmir’s climate gets less attention, it also doesn’t get the funding, the research teams, or the adaptation help that “hotspot” regions get.
A person in Kashmir uses a tiny fraction of the energy an average person in the United States or Europe uses. They didn’t build the factories or drive the cars that heated up the planet. But they are the ones watching the snow disappear earlier every year. A farmer plants his crops based on when the water usually arrives, and now it arrives late, or all at once in a dangerous flood, or barely at all. 
But this isn’t only Kashmir’s problem. The mountains of the wider Himalayan region hold the third-largest amount of frozen freshwater anywhere outside the North and South Poles. Up to 2 billion people depend on rivers originating in the Hindu Kush Himalaya region, and these rivers are sustained by annual ice melt. Second, stop treating demilitarization and climate adaptation as separate conversations in the Kashmir region. They are the same conversation, because who gets to gather information and who gets to act on it are both, in the end, questions of power.
Third, when countries meet at big climate conferences and talk about who should pay to help places already hurt by climate change, Kashmir needs to be part of that conversation – both from the climate justice side as well as colonialism side. Someone has to ask directly: who pays to fix the water systems and rebuild after floods in a place still caught in conflict?
Kashmir’s unresolved future cannot be an excuse for ignoring what is happening to its mountains right now. Kashmir’s mountains are going to keep getting hotter whether anyone notices or not. The glaciers are not waiting for a diplomatic settlement, and neither should the people whose water and lands depend on them.
Popular perceptions of Kashmir center on soldiers, checkpoints, and decades-old fights over land. Sometimes tourism edges into the discourse. Far less attention is paid to the fact that Kashmir’s mountains are heating up rapidly.

It found that the high mountain towns of Pahalgam and Gulmarg, which are also famous tourist sites, have warmed by nearly a full degree Celsius in just the last 20 years. One town, Bhaderwah, is heating up even faster at roughly 0.3 degrees per decade. And the nights are warming fastest of all. 
David Sathuluri is a founder and co-director of the Laboratory of AI, Climate & Society, Columbia University’s Lamont-Doherty Earth Observatory, where he coordinates interdisciplinary research on AI and climate justice. He writes extensively on climate justice, caste, and AI governance for outlets including The Los Angeles Times, The Hindu, The Quint, and The Diplomat.
articles.summary: Kashmir's mountains are going to keep getting hotter whether anyone notices or not.

articles.title: Transnational Repression, Organized Crime, and Conflict in Southeast Asia
articles.full_text:
A conversation with Phil Robertson, a Bangkok-based human rights advocate.
Phil Robertson, the director of the Asia Human Rights and Labour Advocates.
Chinese dissident Zhang Xinyan was detained by authorities in Thailand last week while boarding her scheduled flight from Bangkok to Canada for resettlement, despite holding a refugee visa from the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees and all required approvals from Ottawa.
Human rights groups immediately leapt to her defense, adding there are three more Chinese dissidents – Bai Zhaodong, Tan Yixiang, and Zhou Junyi – currently being held in Thai detention centers who were at risk of being forcibly returned if local authorities bow to pressure from Beijing.
Phil Robertson, director of Asia Human Rights and Labour Advocates, returns to Beyond the Mekong to speak with The Diplomat’s Luke Hunt about their plight and the region-wide issue of transnational repression.
“If they, these four refugees, are sent back, including Zhang and others, they would face persecution, arrest, likely torture and detention, and long prison terms,” he said.
Robertson said Zhang Xinyan’s detention had again raised concerns in the United States, European Union, Australia, and Canada, which was expected to lead diplomatic efforts in securing her release. Their plight was also reminiscent of the 40 Uyghurs that Thailand forcibly repatriated to China last year.
“If Thailand, at the last minute pulls back a case like this, because the autocratic dictatorship [in China] has demanded the return of their people, it raises concerns about whether Thailand has any seriousness in the protection of refugees,” he said.
Bangkok-based Robertson, who has 30 years of experience working in human rights, also speaks about organized crime, human trafficking networks, and scam compounds in mainland Southeast Asia, as well as cross-border relations between Thailand and Cambodia.
He said both countries are suffering, particularly economically, as the border remains shut after they fought a bitter six-month conflict across the frontier last year, ending in a December truce.
But Robertson says Cambodia has not done enough to end the scourge of organized crime – a key plank in the ceasefire – and has failed to hold accountable the country’s business tycoons, some of which have been named and sanctioned by the United States and others for their ties to scam compounds.
“They continue to be too big to touch,” he said, adding the crackdown is selective and Cambodia was trying to create an impression for the international community that it was tackling organized crime while trying to maintain the bigger, well connected scam centers.
“The international community right now is not buying it,” he said.

Luke Hunt is a Southeast Asia correspondent for The Diplomat. He has spent three decades working in the region and produces the Beyond the Mekong podcast. He can be followed on Patreon and X – formerly Twitter.
articles.summary: A conversation with Phil Robertson, a Bangkok-based human rights advocate.

articles.title: Does Myanmar Still Have One Government to Recognize?
articles.full_text:
Much of the world still views Myanmar through a narrow diplomatic lens that cannot encompass the full political complexity of its conflict.
Last month, Myanmar’s new military-backed government rejected a request from the Philippines’ Foreign Secretary Ma. Theresa Lazaro to meet with the country’s detained civilian leader Aung San Suu Kyi, citing her prison sentence. Days later, Min Aung Hlaing, who led the 2021 coup and anointed himself president in April, traveled to Laos on an official bilateral visit, his first to an Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) member state since the coup.
Although ASEAN itself has not officially recognized the new government, nor accepted the result of the election held in December and January, it still structures every visit and appointment around the notion that the junta is Myanmar’s sole government.
That is usually interpreted as ASEAN being cautious, compromised, or slow to react to the situation on the ground inside Myanmar. It instead reflects a diplomatic practice that recognizes one government per territory. Instead of asking whether ASEAN should recognize the military regime, or which of Myanmar’s competing authorities it might recognize instead, a more useful question may be whether looking for one government to recognize is the best or only way to think about the situation.
ASEAN’s founding non-interference norm took for granted a settled order of single territorial states protected from outside interference. This was codified as a principle in the 1976 Treaty of Amity and Cooperation, but the founders did not foresee a situation in which a member has a contested government.
International law distinguishes a state’s existence from recognition of its government. States continue to exist under contested or divided control without losing statehood. Myanmar is not the only case. Somalia has sustained divided control for decades, and Libya has been contested by rival governments since 2014. Afghanistan’s seat at the United Nations has been frozen since 2021. The gap exists in diplomatic practice, which treats a settled state as the default because it is the more common case.
The statistics on Myanmar show how the gap has widened. As of mid-2026, the military regime administers a territory covering between a fifth and a third of the country, albeit nearly all the major population centers. This includes areas where it maintains only a military presence with no administration. Beyond this, close to half the country has slid into a form of “warlordism,” with power fracturing into rival centers rather than converging on one single entity. The Arakan Army administers nearly all of Rakhine State, and it maintains its own independent relations with Bangladesh, although the latter states that this does not amount to diplomatic recognition.
The junta, the National Unity Government (NUG), and ethnic resistance organizations, whether or not they are aligned with the NUG, are all establishing competing centers of authority, each running their own initiatives in parallel rather than competing to be recognized as the sole government. The NUG’s own claims about how many townships it controls have shifted and been difficult to verify. In March it formed a new coordinating body with four ethnic armed organizations, in an effort to consolidate administrative control.
Describing these developments as simply how Myanmar’s politics currently works prompts an obvious objection: armed control of a territory does not automatically confer a mandate to govern it That standard, applied literally, would also implicate the NUG, whose presence on the ground depends on an armed wing, the People’s Defense Force, that it had to build after the coup, just as the junta’s authority depends on the Myanmar armed forces. But the junta’s control and the NUG’s electoral mandate are different types of claims, and neither claim resolves who should be held responsible for what has already happened, nor excuses the actions taken to retain or regain control.
Refusing to deal with an armed group does not alter the reality on the ground; it only changes which entity the external actor is willing to call the government.
Prospects for a political settlement under the “one government” model remain remote, yet diplomatic interactions are still centered around this framework. Take, for example, the Myanmar seat at the United Nations. Since a decision-making process designed for a single government cannot resolve a case without one, the U.N. Credentials Committee’s inability to choose between the junta and the NUG has simply defaulted to the current occupant.
China maintains the most consistent contact with a coalition of the biggest non-signatory ethnic armed organizations. It has not actually accepted the “one government” model and it is unclear whether Beijing even needs a single government to pursue its strategic and economic interests in Myanmar. Instead, China is pursuing local deals with individual armed groups rather than any unified outcome, since groups enjoying real autonomy already have little reason to cede ground in negotiations with Naypyidaw. For instance, the Arakan Army is currently seeking confederal status along the lines of the autonomous Wa State, instead of taking part in a federal union.
ASEAN’s stance resets with each rotating chair, and the actions of its members have not converged. Some analysts read the recent visits to Naypyidaw of Thailand and Indonesia’s foreign ministers as a de facto acceptance of the post-election government. At the same time, Singapore continues to withhold recognition while waiting for a government it considers legitimate, as does Timor-Leste, which is also pursuing a war crimes complaint against the junta. Each still assumes Myanmar has one government to recognize, but does not agree which one it is. It seems unlikely that a single government would satisfy each, let alone all, of these positions.
India’s approach is different from ASEAN and its members. After President Min Aung Hlaing’s visit to New Delhi last month, India’s foreign secretary said that India’s policy was “not intended to be a commentary on the internal political arrangements” in Myanmar. Before the visit, the NUG had asked India not to receive Min Aung Hlaing, but the government did not respond. China and India extract the function each of them needs from Myanmar’s competing authorities, without waiting for, or requiring, any of them to be recognized as its government.
The reality is that there may not be a single approach to engaging with Myanmar.

Theresa Lazaro to meet with the country’s detained civilian leader Aung San Suu Kyi, citing her prison sentence. articles.title: China’s Live-Fire Drill Near Okinotorishima Tests Japan’s Maritime Nerves
articles.full_text:
The exercise highlights growing China-Russia naval interoperability while exposing competing interpretations of maritime law.
Okinotorishima, the small feature that is Japan’s southernmost territory.
Japan’s Ministry of Defense said on July 21 that a Chinese naval vessel had conducted a machine-gun firing exercise two days earlier inside Japan’s claimed EEZ in the Pacific. It was first time the ministry has publicly announced a case involving a foreign warship conducting a live-fire exercise inside Japan’s EEZ.
According to the Joint Staff, the Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force detected three Chinese vessels – the Type 055 (Renhai-class) destroyer Anshan, the Type 052D (Luyang III-class) destroyer Kaifeng, and the Type 903A replenishment ship Kekexilihu – and the Russian Navy’s Steregushchiy-class frigate Rezkiy sailing northeast about 330 kilometers southwest of Okinotorishima, Japan’s southernmost territory. The Joint Staff later confirmed that the Kaifeng conducted the machine-gun firing roughly 180 kilometers southwest of the island, squarely inside Japan’s EEZ. 
The same four ships had passed southward between Okinawa’s main island and Miyako Island on July 16.
The Chinese destroyer radioed nearby vessels shortly before the drill to announce its location and duration, a transmission the MSDF destroyer Samidare, on monitoring duty, picked up. Tokyo lodged a diplomatic protest, arguing the exercise could endanger nearby shipping.
The key point is that Japan did not allege a violation of international law. The firing occurred well beyond Japan’s 12-nautical-mile territorial sea, in waters Tokyo regards as part of its EEZ, and no damage to vessels was reported. Under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea, an EEZ grants coastal states sovereign rights over natural resources, not full sovereignty over all activity within it. Foreign militaries generally retain freedom of navigation there, and military exercises in another state’s EEZ are not automatically unlawful – a principle the United States and other maritime powers rely on, even as China has long pressed for greater coastal state control over foreign military activity in waters it claims as its own.
That makes Beijing’s response here somewhat ironic. Foreign Ministry spokesperson Lin Jian dismissed Japan’s protest as groundless and accused Tokyo of exaggerating external threats to justify its own military buildup. He also revived China’s long-standing argument that Okinotorishima is a rock, not an island capable of generating an EEZ under UNCLOS Article 121. Japan has reinforced Okinotorishima with concrete to preserve its claim to islet status.
The legal disagreement therefore runs deeper than the question of whether military exercises are permissible in another state’s EEZ. Beijing rejects Japan’s entitlement to an EEZ around Okinotorishima altogether, arguing that the surrounding waters are part of the high seas and that China’s activities there are fully consistent with international law and customary international practice. At the same time, China is invoking a permissive interpretation of naval freedom that it typically resists when the United States and its allies conduct military operations in the South China Sea.
For Japan, the immediate concern is safety: a live-fire drill, even with prior notice, creates risks for commercial and fishing vessels when warning windows are brief. The larger concern is strategic signaling.
The Kaifeng was operating alongside the Russian frigate during a joint maritime patrol following the two navies’ “Joint Sea 2026” exercise near Qingdao, underscoring how bilateral drills are increasingly extending into coordinated operations across the Western Pacific.
Chinese military activity and China-Russia operations around Japan have grown more frequent and sophisticated in recent years, including joint bomber flights, China’s submarine-launched ballistic missile test on July 6, and repeated transits through the Miyako and Tsushima straits.
China-Russia military cooperation used to be largely symbolic demonstrations of political solidarity between Beijing and Moscow. Today, their activities are approaching genuine operational interoperability, with the two navies coordinating command, tempo, and geography across an ever wider stretch of the Western Pacific.
That context is why Tokyo treated the drill as more than an isolated gunnery exercise. Defense Minister Koizumi Shinjiro, speaking in Britain, framed it as one element of deepening China-Russia military cooperation. Chief Cabinet Secretary Kihara Minoru said Japan would keep monitoring China-Russia activity around the archipelago “with concern.”
Japanese officials increasingly view such episodes not as isolated events but as evidence that a bilateral exercise near China’s coast can extend seamlessly into operational training deep in the Western Pacific.
On July 17 Chinese Defense Ministry spokesperson Jiang Bin defended the broader China-Russia military partnership as resting on principles of non-alliance, non-confrontation, and non-targeting of third parties. He described the exercise and subsequent patrol as part of an annual cooperation plan rather than a response to current international or regional tensions.
The incident also exposes a dilemma for Tokyo. Japan wants to defend its maritime claims and deter risky behavior, but must avoid legal arguments broad enough to undercut the freedom of navigation principles that Japan and its allies rely on elsewhere, including in the South China Sea.
The more durable Japanese argument, then, is not that China’s drill was unlawful, but that it was unsafe and deliberately timed for effect. That distinction keeps Tokyo aligned with the maritime order it seeks to defend, while still spotlighting how Beijing and Moscow are using ostensibly routine naval operations to normalize pressure around Japan.
Beijing portrays the patrol as a routine activity conducted under the two militaries’ annual cooperation plan. Tokyo sees something different: another indication that China-Russia naval cooperation is steadily expanding beyond symbolic diplomacy into coordinated operations that increasingly challenge the strategic position of the United States and its allies in the Western Pacific.
Okinotorishima is tiny, but the waters around it have become a testing ground for competing interpretations – not only of maritime law, but also of the evolving strategic balance in the Western Pacific.

articles.title: UNCCD Executive Secretary Yasmine Fouad on Mongolia, Desertification, and COP17
articles.full_text:
COP17, which will be hosted by Mongolia in August, “comes at a pivotal moment for global action on land.”
UNCCD Executive Secretary Dr. Yasmine Fouad meets with Prime Minister of Mongolia Uchral Nyam-Osor.
This year, Mongolia will host the 17th Session of the Conference of the Parties (COP17) to the United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification (UNCCD) from August 17-28. COP17 includes segments on finance, water, land and people, and food systems, which are all crucial elements for Mongolia’s fight against desertification – and also issues that intertwine with the country’s economic goals and policies.
In an exclusive interview, The Diplomat’s Bolor Lkhaajav spoke with UNCCD Executive Secretary Dr. Yasmine Fouad on range of issues facing Mongolia, the significance of Mongolia hosting COP17, and what policy recommendations UNCCD has for the country’s policymakers. 
What do countries, leaders, and participants hope to get out of COP17? What outcomes Mongolia should expect? 
COP17 comes at a pivotal moment for global action on land. Across the world, countries increasingly recognize that desertification, land degradation, and drought are no longer solely environmental concerns. They affect economies, food production, water security, biodiversity, and the resilience of communities.
I believe countries will come to Mongolia with a shared understanding that the challenge is no longer understanding the problem – we know what is at stake. The priority now is implementation. Countries have already committed to ambitious goals, including restoring around one billion hectares of degraded land by 2030. The focus now is on translating those commitments into action through stronger partnerships, increased investment and practical solutions. COP17 represents an opportunity to move from ambition to implementation by scaling finance, innovation and partnerships that deliver measurable results on the ground.
One of the key expectations for COP17 is progress in mobilizing finance for land restoration and drought resilience. Addressing land degradation and drought requires around $355 billion annually, yet current investment is only about $77 billion each year. Closing this gap will require stronger engagement from governments, development banks, the private sector and new financing partners.
Another important outcome will be advancing cooperation on drought resilience, sustainable rangeland management and more resilient food systems. COP17 also coincides with the International Year of Rangelands and Pastoralists, creating a unique opportunity to place rangelands and pastoral communities higher on the global policy agenda.
For Mongolia, hosting COP17 is an opportunity to place the challenges and opportunities facing drylands and pastoral communities higher on the global agenda, while strengthening international cooperation on land restoration and drought resilience. I hope the Conference will reinforce a simple but increasingly important message: investing in healthy land is investing in people, economies and a more sustainable future for generations to come.
In 2024, Mongolia committed to spending at least 1 percent of its GDP annually to combat climate change and desertification. What are some of the policies UNCCD recommends for addressing Mongolia’s desertification situation?
Land restoration should be viewed not as an environmental cost, but as an investment in economic resilience, food security, water security, and sustainable development. Countries that invest in healthy landscapes today reduce the far greater economic and social costs associated with land degradation, drought, and ecosystem decline in the future. Healthy land is the foundation of healthy food systems, water security, and resilient economies. Investing in restoration today reduces future costs while creating long-term opportunities for sustainable growth. 
One of UNCCD’s key recommendations is to integrate sustainable land management and restoration into national development planning so that investments in agriculture, water, infrastructure, and climate action reinforce one another rather than operate in isolation. 
Another priority is creating policy and regulatory frameworks that encourage long-term investment in sustainable land use and reward measurable restoration outcomes. Countries should also strengthen policy coherence by integrating land restoration across agriculture, water, infrastructure, climate and food systems policies, ensuring that public investment delivers multiple development benefits.
Public finance remains essential, but it cannot meet the scale of the challenge on its own. It should be used strategically to mobilize additional investment from development banks, the private sector and other financing partners. Innovative financing approaches and stronger public-private partnerships will be critical to scaling up action. Public finance should increasingly be used to reduce investment risks and unlock larger flows of private capital, making restoration more attractive as a long-term investment opportunity.
Globally, implementing countries’ plans to address land degradation and drought requires around $355 billion annually, while current investment is estimated at only $77 billion each year. Yet the cost of inaction is even greater – estimated at nearly $900 billion every year. Investing in restoration is therefore not simply an environmental imperative; it is also a sound economic decision.
Ultimately, every country will determine the financing approaches that best reflect its national priorities and circumstances. UNCCD’s role is to support countries by sharing knowledge, strengthening partnerships, and helping mobilize the investment needed to restore land and build resilience to drought.
In the last decade, Mongolia witnessed hundreds of little lakes and rivers disappear. What are some of the direct and indirect impacts of these catastrophic events? What UNCCD policy recommendations and global knowledge can be shared with Mongolia to solve this crisis?
The disappearance of lakes and rivers is one of the clearest signs that climate change, land degradation, and water scarcity are becoming increasingly interconnected. While changing rainfall patterns and rising temperatures are major drivers, the condition of the land largely determines how landscapes respond to these pressures.
Healthy soils regulate the water cycle by absorbing and storing water, replenishing groundwater, and reducing runoff. As land degrades, soils lose this capacity, making landscapes increasingly vulnerable to drought and water scarcity. In turn, prolonged drought further accelerates land degradation, creating a cycle that becomes increasingly difficult to reverse. Healthy land and healthy water systems are inseparable. Restoring landscapes is one of the most effective ways to improve water security while reducing the impacts of drought.
Mongolia is experiencing these pressures very directly. Nearly 77 percent of its territory is affected by land degradation, while average temperatures have risen by more than 2 degrees Celsius since the 1940s – more than twice the global average. Rainfall has declined, droughts have become more frequent, and the number of sand and dust storm days has more than tripled over the past six decades. These changes reduce pasture productivity, place increasing pressure on water resources and threaten the livelihoods of pastoral communities.
The devastating combination of summer drought followed by the 2023–2024 dzud, which resulted in the loss of more than 7 million livestock, illustrates how climate extremes can cascade through ecosystems, livelihoods, and the wider economy.
From the UNCCD perspective, one of the most important recommendations is to manage land and water together rather than as separate issues. Restoring degraded landscapes improves water retention, strengthens drought resilience, and helps sustain agriculture, biodiversity, and rural livelihoods.
Experience from dryland regions around the world also shows the value of shifting from crisis response to prevention. Sustainable land management, drought preparedness, early warning systems, and science-based decision-making help reduce risks before they become disasters. Solutions are most effective when they combine scientific knowledge with the experience of local communities and pastoralists, who have managed drylands sustainably for generations. Also, artificial intelligence, Earth observation, and digital technologies are also creating new opportunities to strengthen drought forecasting, monitor land conditions, and support evidence-based decision-making.
The implications extend far beyond Mongolia. For countries like Mongolia, protecting water resources begins with restoring the landscapes that sustain them.
Given Mongolia’s vast landscape and its over-dependence on mining resources, what has been the main struggle to balance economic development and environmental protection? What steps should Mongolia take in the next five to ten years to make a major impact?
Finding the right balance between economic development and environmental sustainability is one of the defining policy challenges of the 21st century. It is certainly not unique to Mongolia. 
Many countries rely on natural resources to generate jobs, growth, and public revenues while facing increasing pressures from land degradation, drought, and climate change. Countries rich in mineral resources face the additional challenge of ensuring that economic growth today does not undermine the natural capital on which future prosperity depends. The question is therefore not whether countries should pursue development, but how development can be achieved in ways that sustain the land, water and ecosystems on which economies ultimately depend.
Healthy land underpins food production, water security, biodiversity, and resilient livelihoods. When land degrades, the costs extend well beyond the environment. Globally, land degradation already costs the world nearly $900 billion every year, while drought causes at least another $300 billion in annual losses. By contrast, investing in sustainable land management and restoration generates long-term economic, environmental and social returns. Land should therefore be viewed as productive natural capital that supports long-term economic competitiveness rather than simply an environmental asset.
Prevention is not only more environmentally effective – it is also far more cost-effective than responding once degradation has become entrenched. Mongolia illustrates both the opportunities and the complexity of this challenge. As a resource-rich country, it is also confronting the growing impacts of land degradation, drought, and water stress. Ensuring that natural resource development goes hand in hand with sustainable land management, healthy rangelands and resilient landscapes will be essential to building lasting economic resilience.
articles.summary: COP17, which will be hosted by Mongolia in August, “comes at a pivotal moment for global action on land.”

articles.title: ‘There Is Zero Accountability’: India’s ‘Cockroaches’ Question Modi and a Broken System
articles.full_text:
The Cockroach Janata Party is leading a youth-heavy protest movement that embodies the public anger with the BJP.
India is witnessing a mass movement seeking accountability for education scams.
“Modi, your dictatorship, won’t last, won’t last,” yelled Abhijeet Dipke as he motioned at the crowd to repeat the slogan.
Dipke, the founding president of the Cockroach Janata Party, meets thousands of faces every day. At first glance, the party’s name sounds like a joke. In many ways, that is precisely the point.
On May 15, during a hearing in India’s Supreme Court, Chief Justice Surya Kant said that unemployed young people in India move toward activism and attack the system like “cockroaches.” Kant also said that it was the “unemployed” who questioned the government like cockroaches. So Dipke came up with the name “Cockroach Janata Party” – combining Kant’s epithet with the name of the ruling Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) – and invited people to join the parody political brand, with the cockroach embraced as a proud political metaphor. 
What began as a viral digital movement quickly evolved into something more revealing. After garnering more than 20 million Instagram followers through memes and satire, Cockroach Janata Party (CJP) began raising questions about unemployment, governance, accountability, and political representation. It is creating an alternative form of political participation and digital dissent that is thriving largely outside traditional party structures. 
Since June 6, the road near New Delhi’s Jantar Mantar, a popular protest site, has echoed with the anger, frustration and damage that the 12-year-old government of the BJP has caused.
Under New Delhi’s blazing hot skies, Indians from all sections of society, all religious lines, and all walks of life, have been protesting with one primary demand: the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan. 
Since its inception, the CJP has demanded that Pradhan be held accountable for what it describes as systemic failures in India’s examination system. The CJP argues that repeated controversies – including paper leaks, the cancellation and rescheduling of key entrance examinations, and alleged irregularities in On-Screen Marking valuation process – have severely undermined the credibility of high-stakes government-run exams that determine the futures of millions of Indian students.
It is this lack of accountability that is drawing masses to the protest. Indrija Singh, a retired philosophy professor from north India’s Uttar Pradesh, took an overnight train from her home in Allahabad, some 700 kilometers from Delhi, to reach Jantar Mantar. 
Singh, 65, explained that as a professor – someone who has invested decades in empowering young minds – the BJP’s actions felt like fraud. “This is unethical at all levels. The government’s actions in the last decade have eroded our trust in the way they deal with the citizenry. Students are the future of any country and the BJP is pushing this future to ruin. It must accept its failures,” Singh told The Diplomat.
Singh is one of the many common citizens who are traveling to Delhi and making the protest site their home, proudly calling themselves cockroaches.
Ashutosh Ranka, national spokesperson for the CJP, is outraged at the way the government has dealt with the education cams and even those protesting against them. “This is the most ridiculous government, they don’t believe in dialogue, they only believe in harassing, jailing and robbing the rights of citizens,” Ranka told The Diplomat. “Millions of futures are at stake, but for them [the BJP], students, their stagnant lives, and painful suicides don’t seem to matter.”
As day turns to night, and night brings another day, more and more cockroaches join the movement in what they call a “peaceful strike” for their demands to be heard.
Since the cockroaches arrived at Jantar Mantar, the protest site doesn’t sleep. Volunteers serve food donated by supporters. Protesters camp at the site, sleeping in New Delhi’s extreme humid and hot weather. Those who want to support the movement in their own ways bring to the site home-cooked meals, chilled sherbets and lemonade, India’s tricolor flag, India’s constitution, and unique posters that serve the Modi government some Gen-Z sarcasm.
“We have come here to let Modi know, we understand his politics and we won’t be fooled anymore,” said Prerna, who traveled from Maharashtra, 1200 km from Delhi, to protest against the injustice unfolding in the education sector.
Prerna is a member of the Rashtriya Yuva Sangathan, a national youth organization based on Gandhian ideology. Though the protest began to highlight the ecosystem of evasions in the education sector at the hands of the BJP, today, she views the movement as larger that those primary demands.
Cooling herself with a hand fan, Prerna said that protesters here have shown utmost commitment to the ideas that the Constitution of India embodies. “We won’t leave till Pradhan resigns. This site has become our home, fellow protestors our family. Their tactics cannot break our resolve,” she said.
But the man in question, Pradhan, has refused to resign and even dismissed the movement and its protesters as the “B team of terrorists.” Modi, who has not held a news conference in more than 12 years but is active on social media, has not reacted to the protests yet.
Anjali, a member of All India Students’ Association (AISA), the student wing of the Communist Party of India (Marxist-Leninist), has been at the protest site for weeks now. “This is what bugs them: our unity, our oneness. We represent an India which is secular, which does not fight on caste-lines, religious lines, standing against the same regime which is scared of people coming together and posing questions at it,” Anjali said.
Anjali pointed out how BJP National President Nitin Nabin recently launched an attack on the CJP. Nabin referred to the satirical political outfit as a “virus and cockroach-like gang” that aims to “make the country hollow” and “divide the country.”
India’s police force is actively monitoring the protest site. As one enters the road, the police first makes sure that entrants get their bags checked; they paste stickers on each OK checked bag. From there on, the entrant must pass through two metal detector checks and a physical frisk by officers. The road is heavily guarded by the police; every few steps the police are visibly taking note of every protestor and every poster that lands there. The protest is also digitally monitored, with a dozen CCTV cameras and policemen also filming protestors.
Entering the venue, one sees scores of different organizations’ banners and a sea of tens of thousands of people who want to be heard.
At the site, one particular board has the photos of more than 20 students who committed suicide in the aftermath of the National Eligibility Entrance Test (NEET) 2026 paper leak. “The government shows zero accountability. Even parents of these students made sure to mark their anguish by supporting our protest. They brought their children’s ashes to us,” Vijeta Dahiya, the CJP spokesperson, told The Diplomat.
“We began with education, but look around, India is here. Those who are battling unemployment, exam scams, paper leaks, corruption, and [are] desperately tired of the Modi government are all here. We are peacefully protesting because we want the government to listen, they must wake up,” he said.
In the early hours of July 18, plain-clothed Delhi Police officers marched into Jantar Mantar and forcibly removed activist Sonam Wangchuk from the protest site.
Wangchuk, a 59-year-old climate activist and educator who began a hunger strike to support the cockroaches on June 28, was forcibly taken away from the protest site. When CJP members, students, and protesters at the site tried to stop the police, they were threatened and beaten.
Wangchuk’s indefinite hunger strike was joined by three doctoral students, Neha Bora, Aameen and Manish, all members of AISA. The three ended their hunger strike on July 20, at the request of several opposition leaders amid the government’s silence, apathy, and Delhi’s heat affecting their health.
The police made sure that Dipke, the founder of the CJP, was not around when they removed Wangchuk. Dipke has now demanded Modi’s resignation and announced a march to parliament on July 20, the first day of its monsoon session. 
While Wangchuk was supposed to lead this march, he was taken to a government hospital without his family members’ knowledge and consent, where he was being made to break his hunger strike.
articles.summary: The Cockroach Janata Party is leading a youth-heavy protest movement that embodies the public anger with the BJP.

articles.title: How China Is Aiding Kazakh Efforts to Become Hyper Modern
articles.full_text:
As Kazakhstan pushes forward to become a leading tech hub in the region, China continues to prove itself a crucial partner.
Kazakh President Kassym-Jomart Tokayev speaks at the opening of the 2026 World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai, China, July 17, 2026..
On July 17, Kazakh President Kassym-Jomart Tokayev concluded a trip to Shanghai, China, by speaking at the opening of the 2026 World Artificial Intelligence Conference. While restating Kazakhstan’s efforts to become a regional hub for digital trade and international cooperation, Tokayev also praised China for its advancements in the field during his 16-minute speech.
The day before, the Kazakh president had already expressed his desire to deepen cooperation between the two countries in the field of technology. This was underscored by the signing of deals worth more than $15 billion covering digital infrastructure, transport, and the security sector.
Tokayev’s trip comes amid what the Kazakh government has coined the Year of Digitalization and AI, with energy and resources being directed toward the development of a digital state. His talks with industry-leading companies in Shanghai on July 16 also made it clear that China played a defining role in Kazakhstan’s development.
Even before the conference, China played a visible part in Kazakhstan’s digital strategy, both by sharing relevant knowledge and by integrating Chinese hardware to propel Kazakhstan’s hypermodern ambitions. For example, the Chinese firm Huawei is the provider of several parts of Kazakhstan’s critical infrastructure.
Tokayev’s Shanghai visit was preceded by a trip by Kazakh Foreign Minister Yermek Kosherbayev to Beijing on July 13, where he met with his Chinese counterpart, Wang Yi. There, Wang expressed optimism about the upcoming meeting.
“The two heads of state will conduct in-depth strategic communication, injecting new impetus into the development of bilateral relations,” the Chinese foreign minister stated.
Simultaneously, Kosherbayev showed great interest in maintaining close relations between the two countries, asserting that Kazakhstan always prioritizes cooperation with China.
In his Shanghai speech, Tokayev further emphasized the scale of Sino-Kazakh economic relations: “Last year, bilateral trade volume reached $49 billion. This is a record high. China has invested over $30 billion in Kazakhstan’s economy,” the president said during the roundtable with heads of several Chinese high-tech companies.
The speech was followed by another step toward closer cooperation, with the two countries signing more than 70 agreements and memorandums worth over $15 billion. Besides deals in the logistics sector, some of the largest agreements concerned the digital development of, or deployment of, artificial intelligence in Kazakhstan. Astana became one of 29 governments to sign on to the Shanghai-based World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization.  
Huawei emerged as the biggest beneficiary, agreeing to a strategic partnership with the Kazakh Ministry of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Development while also signing a deal on the delivery of equipment for Kazakhstan’s sovereign wealth fund. Alongside this, Kazakhtelecom also agreed on the basic principles for future cooperation in the construction of the Data Center Valley, a 1 GW data center in northeastern Kazakhstan that, when built, is set to become the biggest of its kind in Central Asia.
Despite the fact that a full list of the more than 70 agreements and memorandums has not been published, the publicly available data suggests that most of the deals center around the delivery of Chinese products to Kazakhstan, and not the other way around. While Kazakhstan exports critical minerals to China, such as copper used in the manufacturing of electric vehicles, Chinese companies have been key to Kazakhstan’s digitalization over the past several years.
China’s focus on Kazakhstan shifted noticeably in 2022. That year, Huawei decided to relocate several of its operations from Russia to Central Asia, following Moscow’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine. Huawei was later also selected to build Kazakhstan’s 5G infrastructure, thereby giving the company leverage over critical infrastructure.
Moreover, the telecommunications company also signed agreements related to future data centers in Kazakhstan just last year, including a memorandum under which Huawei will assist with cybersecurity efforts in the Kazakh energy sector going forward. 
On a broader level, China has already influenced Kazakhstan’s approach to security. According to Radio Azattyk, this can be seen not only in the technology used, which comes from China, but also in the methods of nationwide surveillance.
As Tokayev returns home, Kazakhstan’s technological dependence on China appears set to deepen. With this, a successful hyper-digitalization of Kazakhstan will benefit both the Central Asian nation and the Chinese economy.
