articles.title: China’s Arctic Comeback
articles.full_text:
Many of China’s initial, ambitious Arctic objectives collided with political and logistical realities. Now Beijing is testing out an “Arctic 2.0” strategy.
China’s Yellow River facility in Svalbard – before the stone lions were ordered to be removed.
It has been almost a decade now since Beijing announced its revised Arctic strategy, particularly focused on the development of the Polar Silk Road (PSR), a segment of the Belt and Road Initiative originally conceived as an emerging “blue economic channel” connecting Europe with Northeast Asia via the Northern Sea Route. 
The PSR would later be incorporated into Beijing’s 2018 governmental White Paper on the Arctic, as China sought to “understand, protect, develop, and participate in the governance” of the far north, while positioning itself as a “near-Arctic state” and regional stakeholder. While maintaining emphasis on Arctic scientific cooperation, China began to exhibit stronger interests in regional economic, and to a degree strategic, initiatives in the years following the publication of the White Paper.
However, many of China’s initial, ambitious Arctic objectives soon collided with political and logistical realities. Various planned regional investments, from railways in the Nordic region and Russia, to mines in Greenland and Canada, to natural gas co-development in Alaska, failed to materialize. Policy momentum was further stalled by the COVID-19 pandemic, and subsequently by the aftershocks of Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
Moreover, the political balkanization of the north between Western and Russian spheres of interest further complicated Chinese plans to take a “whole of Arctic” approach to regional economic diplomacy. Beijing soon found itself in the near-impossible position of trying to maintain Arctic cooperation with Russia without slamming the door on other regional governments, especially in the Nordic region.
There was the emerging view that both the PSR and Beijing’s overall Arctic engagement were in danger of falling into abeyance as hard power-based geopolitical concerns swept through the region, and Arctic powers began to fence off their security interests. Around the time China achieved observer status in the Arctic Council in 2013, some Chinese commentators warned that the country was in danger of being shut out of the far north because the region would be “cut up like a melon” by Arctic governments who “refuse to share the cake.”
While the optimism of the last decade has given way to a new practicality, China is showing no sign of ceding the Arctic as it continues to expand its foreign policy arenas. On the contrary, Beijing has undertaken the intricate process of rebuilding its northern policies, steps which have included attempts to breathe new life into the PSR, to expand regional research agendas, to pursue greater science diplomacy, and to take advantage of deepening splits between the United States and other Arctic states within NATO, to present China as an alternative partner in developing and governing the region. 
China’s Arctic comeback has been multifaceted to say the least. Earlier this month, China’s 16th Arctic Expedition, overseen by the country’s Ministry of Natural Resources, was launched, featuring the icebreakers Xuelong, Xuelong 2, and Jidi, and the support vessel Tansuo 3. The vessels are to engage in several different research projects including ice depth measurement, local biology and ecology, seabed surveys, and atmospheric testing, in the Arctic Ocean’s Gakkel Ridge, which arches from the Laptev Sea to the waters north of Greenland. The excursion is scheduled to take place until October this year and includes researchers from Russia, Germany, and other countries.
In addition to accentuating the width and depth of the expedition, Chinese media covering the launch have also frequently used the phrase “providing scientific support for Arctic governance.” It is expected this mission will build on the emerging “three dimensional” approach that China has been taking to its Arctic surveys, reflecting interests in land-based, maritime, and air/space observations. In addition to providing China the means of jump-starting its Arctic research agenda, the expedition sends a loud signal that the country is not prepared to be dissuaded from maintaining a northern presence.
At the same time, while China continues to maintain a cautious approach to its overall engagement with Moscow in the Arctic, there has been an increase in Sino-Russian scientific cooperation in recent years, such as in the study of local undersea geology. 
China also continues to maintain research facilities at Ny-Ålesund, Svalbard (Yellow River), and in northern Iceland (China-Iceland Arctic Research Observatory/CIAO), but both sites have undergone recent scrutiny amid concerns about dual-use research detrimental to regional security. In 2025, restrictions were placed on Chinese nationals seeking to research at Universitetssenteret på Svalbard, the only institute of higher learning on the archipelago, and a pair of iconic, Shanghai-made stone lions were recently removed from the Yellow River site in keeping with Norwegian authorities’ attempts to streamline regulations regarding the settlement’s signage, nomenclature, and decor. 
An additional wildcard in China’s research agenda in Svalbard, however, is the degree to which Chinese researchers might participate in Russian plans for an alternative BRICS+ research and educational facility at Pyramiden, one of the two Russian settlements in Svalbard (the other being Barentsburg). While these facilities have yet to be opened, Chinese research organizations have expressed interest in closer cooperation with Russian counterparts in Svalbard, and in May this year a memorandum of understanding was signed in Barentsburg between representatives of Qingdao University and Russian institutions, including Murmansk Arctic University, in a move to facilitate a “permanent international scientific infrastructure” in Svalbard. Although it is unlikely that China would withdraw from the Yellow River facilities, the planned alternate Russian facilities offer a hedging option for Beijing to ensure an ongoing presence in the area. 
China has also been seeking to make better use of Track II, subgovernmental organizations to both gather further regional data as well as develop platforms for enhanced science diplomacy in the far north. For example, the China-Nordic Arctic Research Center (CNARC) have provided outlets for Chinese collaboration with research centers in Northern Europe, but also an opportunity for China to advertise an Arctic agenda based on “win-win” collaboration. Recent CNARC meetings have included, in and among scientific briefings, periodic references to the “cold war mentality” plaguing U.S. Arctic policy, and the alleged Western practice of “green colonialism” affecting northern Indigenous communities. 
After keeping a comparatively lower profile at the Arctic Circle Assembly (ACA) in Reykjavik for the past few years, China’s northern policies prominently featured at the Arctic Circle Rome Forum earlier this year, and are expected to do so again at the 2026 ACA conference in October. 
This distinct focus on Northern Europe suggests that in addition to Chinese policymakers now drawing distinctions between the “NATO Arctic” and the “Russian Arctic,” there is now a growing acknowledgement of the “Nordic Arctic” in Beijing’s thinking. This aspect of China’s revived Arctic policy was demonstrated on the governmental level during the visits to Nordic capitals earlier this month by Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi. Climate change issues, environmental concerns, and the green transition were common themes during Wang’s discussions with leaders in Denmark, Finland, Norway, and Sweden. There was also a concerted effort by the Chinese delegation to rebalance relations with Stockholm, which have been icy for years over differences in human rights policies and China’s 2015 detainment of a Swedish national. 
The revival last year of a misguided White House initiative to annex Greenland has also provided an opening for Beijing to, if not develop Arctic friendships with the Nordics, then at least be accepted by Northern Europe as a balancing actor in the face of Washington’s regional zero-sum policies. However, the metaphorical anchor slowing down the improvement of Chinese diplomacy with the Nordic states, including in Arctic matters, continues to be regional unease over Sino-Russian regional cooperation as well as Beijing’s neutral stance on the invasion of Ukraine.
Finally, while there is no sign that the investment aspects of the Polar Silk Road can be revived either in Western Europe or North America, Beijing is now seeking to go back to basics by touting the potential of the Northern Sea Route (NSR) as an emerging maritime conduit, now buoyed by global worries over the security of other shipping chokepoints like the Strait of Hormuz. China is joining other Asian governments in developing plans for future use of the NSR as more of the waterway becomes ice-free. In 2013, the cargo vessel Yongsheng, owned by the Chinese shipping concern COSCO, made the first successful crossing of the NSR between China and Europe. Yet since 2022, COSCO has been wary of further expanding NSR operations, mainly due to concerns about Western sanctions for providing aid to the Russian economy. 
This month it was confirmed that another Chinese firm, Hong Kong-based Sea Legend Shipping, would soon commence regular NSR shipping between the Chinese port of Ningbo and Felixstowe in the United Kingdom. Eight voyages are planned between August-October 2026, and a test run of sorts was completed, with much fanfare, last autumn by one of Sea Legend’s vessels, the Istanbul Bridge, inaugurating the China-Europe Arctic Express/CAX. While it remains to be seen whether local conditions and economic demand would sustain such a schedule, the announcement itself underscores Beijing’s renewed determination to be front and center in the further opening of Arctic Ocean maritime routes.
What can be inferred from these events is that although Beijing has endured considerable setbacks in developing its Arctic strategies, there is a new focus on redefining regional goals rather than retreating from the region. Beijing’s evolving strategy reflects the reality that the far north is facing sharper geopolitical divisions; concerns about the long-term effectiveness of the Arctic Council as an effective governance mechanism, given the issue of post-2022 “institutional paralysis”; and ongoing environmental changes that create both risks and opportunities for China. 
There is now a “back to the future”’ approach to Chinese regional scientific endeavors, meaning a renewed emphasis on developing research capabilities while also tying them to the idea of China as a legitimate Arctic actor, geography notwithstanding. Diplomatically, China is seeking to mend fences with some Arctic states, (the Nordics plus Canada), while keeping Russia in front as Beijing’s main regional partner despite the political costs involved. 
In addressing Beijing’s “Arctic 2.0” strategy, it is essential for northern governments and the wider international community to understand how Chinese Arctic goals have changed – and which have not.
It has been almost a decade now since Beijing announced its revised Arctic strategy, particularly focused on the development of the Polar Silk Road (PSR), a segment of the Belt and Road Initiative originally conceived as an emerging “blue economic channel” connecting Europe with Northeast Asia via the Northern Sea Route. 
The PSR would later be incorporated into Beijing’s 2018 governmental White Paper on the Arctic, as China sought to “understand, protect, develop, and participate in the governance” of the far north, while positioning itself as a “near-Arctic state” and regional stakeholder. While maintaining emphasis on Arctic scientific cooperation, China began to exhibit stronger interests in regional economic, and to a degree strategic, initiatives in the years following the publication of the White Paper.
However, many of China’s initial, ambitious Arctic objectives soon collided with political and logistical realities. Various planned regional investments, from railways in the Nordic region and Russia, to mines in Greenland and Canada, to natural gas co-development in Alaska, failed to materialize. Policy momentum was further stalled by the COVID-19 pandemic, and subsequently by the aftershocks of Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
Moreover, the political balkanization of the north between Western and Russian spheres of interest further complicated Chinese plans to take a “whole of Arctic” approach to regional economic diplomacy. Beijing soon found itself in the near-impossible position of trying to maintain Arctic cooperation with Russia without slamming the door on other regional governments, especially in the Nordic region.
There was the emerging view that both the PSR and Beijing’s overall Arctic engagement were in danger of falling into abeyance as hard power-based geopolitical concerns swept through the region, and Arctic powers began to fence off their security interests. Around the time China achieved observer status in the Arctic Council in 2013, some Chinese commentators warned that the country was in danger of being shut out of the far north because the region would be “cut up like a melon” by Arctic governments who “refuse to share the cake.”
While the optimism of the last decade has given way to a new practicality, China is showing no sign of ceding the Arctic as it continues to expand its foreign policy arenas. On the contrary, Beijing has undertaken the intricate process of rebuilding its northern policies, steps which have included attempts to breathe new life into the PSR, to expand regional research agendas, to pursue greater science diplomacy, and to take advantage of deepening splits between the United States and other Arctic states within NATO, to present China as an alternative partner in developing and governing the region. 
China’s Arctic comeback has been multifaceted to say the least. Earlier this month, China’s 16th Arctic Expedition, overseen by the country’s Ministry of Natural Resources, was launched, featuring the icebreakers Xuelong, Xuelong 2, and Jidi, and the support vessel Tansuo 3. The vessels are to engage in several different research projects including ice depth measurement, local biology and ecology, seabed surveys, and atmospheric testing, in the Arctic Ocean’s Gakkel Ridge, which arches from the Laptev Sea to the waters north of Greenland. The excursion is scheduled to take place until October this year and includes researchers from Russia, Germany, and other countries.
In addition to accentuating the width and depth of the expedition, Chinese media covering the launch have also frequently used the phrase “providing scientific support for Arctic governance.” It is expected this mission will build on the emerging “three dimensional” approach that China has been taking to its Arctic surveys, reflecting interests in land-based, maritime, and air/space observations. In addition to providing China the means of jump-starting its Arctic research agenda, the expedition sends a loud signal that the country is not prepared to be dissuaded from maintaining a northern presence.
articles.summary: Many of China’s initial, ambitious Arctic objectives collided with political and logistical realities. Now Beijing is testing out an “Arctic 2.0” strategy.

articles.title: ASEAN Foreign Ministers Convene Meeting Under the Shadow of Iran War, Regional Crises
articles.full_text:
The bloc’s foreign ministers are set to call for “the restoration of the safe, unimpeded, and continuous transit passage of vessels and aircraft in the Strait of Hormuz.”
ASEAN foreign ministers pose for a photo during the opening ceremony of the 59th ASEAN Foreign Ministers’ Meeting in Manila, Philippines, Jul. 21, 2026.
Foreign ministers from the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) formally opened their annual meeting in Manila this morning with a call for unity in the face of maritime tensions and growing global instability.
In her opening address at the 59th ASEAN Foreign Ministers’ Meeting, Philippine Foreign Secretary Ma. Theresa Lazaro said that in a world marked by “upheaval, uncertainty and unpredictability,” the region had to respond with “active, deliberate problem solving.”
“In a fractured global landscape, no single nation can weather these storms alone. Our strength and resilience lie in our community,” she said. “If we do not move forward as one, we risk being pulled apart.”
Today’s meeting kicks off a week of diplomatic conclaves in Manila, involving their counterparts from the United States, China, Japan, Russia, and numerous other countries.
U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio, Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi, and Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov are among the confirmed attendees for the East Asia Summit Foreign Ministers’ Meeting and the ASEAN Regional Forum, both of which will be held on July 23. These major summits will be interspersed with dozens of sideline bilateral meetings between representatives of the participating nations.
Regional security issues are expected to dominate the meetings, including renewed fighting in the Middle East, ongoing tensions in the South China Sea, and efforts to revive ASEAN’s stalled initiative for resolving the conflict in Myanmar.
The Associated Press reported yesterday that at today’s meeting, ASEAN’s foreign ministers were set to express their alarm about the renewed hostilities between the United States and Iran and threats to shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, on which the region is heavily dependent for its supplies of crude oil.
In a draft statement of concern, a copy of which was obtained by the AP, the bloc’s foreign ministers “called for the restoration of the safe, unimpeded, and continuous transit passage of vessels and aircraft in the Strait of Hormuz.”
“We underscored the importance of maintaining regional peace, stability and prosperity, upholding international law … including the need for a complete and immediate cessation of hostilities across all fronts in the Middle East,” they said in the statement, which the AP said would be made public after the meeting.
“We acknowledged the further implications of the continued tensions in the Middle East on regional trade, energy and food security, including energy markets and supply chains, agricultural production.”
The week is also expected to see renewed efforts on the regional energy security front, including the ongoing push for ratification of the bloc’s Framework Agreement on Petroleum Security fuel-sharing plan. The purpose of the plan is “to enable coordinated emergency fuel sharing and collective responses to ​supply disruption,” Philippine Trade Secretary Cristina Roque ​said in April.
Another of the issues expected to dominate the agenda today is the situation in Myanmar, which will be the subject of a standalone “extended informal consultation” today.
The meeting, which is intended to address the implementation of the bloc’s Five-Point Consensus initiative for resolving the country’s conflict, comes after ASEAN’s foreign ministers held their first face-to-face meeting with their Myanmar counterpart since the 2021 coup, which plunged the country into prolonged conflict and economic instability. At the July 12 meeting in Bangkok, the bloc said it wanted to reboot the Five-Point Consensus, an April 2021 initiative that calls for an immediate cessation of violence and inclusive dialogue involving “all parties” to the conflict. The following day, Lazaro, the current ASEAN special envoy on Myanmar, held talks with military-backed negotiators and six rebel groups in Pattaya, which she and Thai Foreign Minister Sihasak Phuangketkaew reported were open to dialogue.
ASEAN remains divided on how to deal with its errant member state. Despite agreeing to the Five-Point Consensus in April 2021, the Myanmar military has done little to implement its most important points, prompting ASEAN to bar its representatives from the organization’s annual summits. At the same time, since taking office in April, the country’s new civilianized military administration has pressed hard for readmission into the ASEAN fold, with the support of sympathetic member states. The leading member of this camp is Thailand, whose foreign minister has pushed to bring Myanmar back into the “ASEAN family.”
Other member states, albeit a shrinking coalition of them, have bemoaned the lack of progress on the Five-Point Consensus, which Myanmar’s military-dominated parliament earlier this month rejected as an unwarranted “interference” in Myanmar’s internal affairs. Malaysian Foreign Minister Mohamad Hasan told reporters in May that his country remained opposed to high-level talks while “oppression is still taking place.”
Today’s meeting will see ASEAN’s foreign ministers reaffirm their commitment to the Five-Point Consensus, and call for Naypyidaw to make greater actions to implement its terms, but is unlikely to marks a substantial shift in the bloc’s approach. The Philippines’ ASEAN spokesperson Dominic Imperial told reporters yesterday that it was important that “there is engagement … continuous engagement with not only with the Myanmar authorities but with other Myanmar stakeholders.”
The South China Sea is also set to feature once again at this week’s meetings, following an incident yesterday in which Chinese and Philippine personnel again exchanged physical blows in contested waters. The Philippine military reported that one of its marines was injured sustained a head injury after being struck with a baton by China Coast Guard personnel during a confrontation at Second Thomas Shoal. Both nations have accused the other of instigating the clash.
Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi is expected to hold talks with Lazaro, which, according to the South China Morning Post, are “tipped to focus on the South China Sea.” The issue is also likely to be a subject of discussion during the planned meeting between U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr.

In her opening address at the 59th ASEAN Foreign Ministers’ Meeting, Philippine Foreign Secretary Ma. Theresa Lazaro said that in a world marked by “upheaval, uncertainty and unpredictability,” the region had to respond with “active, deliberate problem solving.”

U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio, Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi, and Russian Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov are among the confirmed attendees for the East Asia Summit Foreign Ministers’ Meeting and the ASEAN Regional Forum, both of which will be held on July 23. These major summits will be interspersed with dozens of sideline bilateral meetings between representatives of the participating nations.

In a draft statement of concern, a copy of which was obtained by the AP, the bloc’s foreign ministers “called for the restoration of the safe, unimpeded, and continuous transit passage of vessels and aircraft in the Strait of Hormuz.”
“We underscored the importance of maintaining regional peace, stability and prosperity, upholding international law … including the need for a complete and immediate cessation of hostilities across all fronts in the Middle East,” they said in the statement, which the AP said would be made public after the meeting.
“We acknowledged the further implications of the continued tensions in the Middle East on regional trade, energy and food security, including energy markets and supply chains, agricultural production.”
articles.summary: The bloc's foreign ministers are set to call for "the restoration of the safe, unimpeded, and continuous transit passage of vessels and aircraft in the Strait of Hormuz.”

articles.title: Philippine Marine Injured in Clash With China Coast Guard at Disputed South China Sea Shoal
articles.full_text:
The two sides traded accusations over the confrontation at Second Thomas Shoal, a long-standing focus of maritime tensions between Manila and Beijing.
A still from a video released by the China Coast Guard, showing its personnel confronting Philippine Navy personnel at Second Thomas Shoal in the South China Sea, Jul. 20, 2026. The BRP Sierra Madre, a grounded warship that functions as the Philippine outpost at the shoal, can be seen in the background.
Philippine and Chinese personnel exchanged physical blows during a confrontation near a contested shoal in the South China Sea, with the two sides again accusing the other of instigating the incident.
The Armed Forces of the Philippines (AFP) stated yesterday that a Philippine Navy marine sustained a head injury after being struck with a baton by China Coast Guard (CCG) personnel during an encounter at Second Thomas Shoal.
In its statement, the AFP said a CCG rigid-hull inflatable boat (RHIB) carrying eight personnel from vessel CCGV 21560 unlawfully approached within close range of the BRP Sierra Madre, a grounded Philippine warship that serves as the Philippines’ outpost on the shoal.
The CCG boat reportedly circled the area while taking photos and videos, prompting two Philippine Navy rubber boats to “calmly and non-confrontationally” drive it away.
The Chinese personnel then “reacted violently and aggressively” by striking a Philippine marine on the head with a wooden baton, the AFP stated. The injured marine was then taken back to the BRP Sierra Madre to receive treatment. The incident also damaged one of the Navy’s rubber boats.
The AFP released a photo of the wound sustained by the marine, as well as a video, apparently shot from aboard the BRP Sierra Madre, that showed the CCG boat ramming the Philippine dinghy, and the personnel from both sides swinging oars and batons at each other.
The AFP said that the “aggressive actions” of the CCG “demonstrate a blatant disregard for safety, endanger the lives of our personnel, and escalate tensions in the West Philippine Sea.” The Department of National Defense issued its own statement asserting that the incident “adds to a clear pattern of provocative and hostile behavior, and further contributes to the People’s Republic of China’s deficit of trust and credibility.”
Unsurprisingly, the Chinese government has challenged this account. In a statement, the Chinese Embassy in Manila insisted that the CCG acted with “maximum rationality and restraint” while accusing the Philippines of spreading “false propaganda.” The CCG also put out its own statement, claiming that two rubber dinghies were launched “illegally” from the BRP Sierra Madre.
The Philippine vessels “ignored our repeated and explicit warnings, rapidly approached in a dangerous manner and rammed the Chinese patrol vessel from the front,” the CCG said. During the incident, Philippine personnel “first used tools such as oars and long sticks to maliciously attack Chinese law enforcement officers.”
In an attempt to substantiate its version of the story, the CCG released several videos showing two rubber boats driving out from the Sierra Madre to confront the Chinese RHIB. In the videos, Philippine personnel wave the Chinese boat off with oars, while CCG personnel brandish batons in response. The South China Sea Strategic Situation Probing Initiative, a Beijing-based think tank, said that the videos showed that “there is no doubt that the Philippine side took the first move.”
The videos do not appear to show the same footage as was released by the AFP.
Naturally, the legitimacy of each side’s actions – and the relevance of the question of who made the “first move” – depends almost entirely on whether one believes the waters to belong to China or the Philippines. In its statement, the CCG said that it would continue to “resolutely uphold the country’s territorial sovereignty and maritime rights and interests,” just as the AFP said that it “remains steadfast in protecting the country’s sovereignty rights and jurisdiction.”
However, Philippine officials were right to point out that their nation’s maritime claims, unlike China’s, were upheld by an international arbitral tribunal in The Hague and ruled valid under the U.N. Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS).
Handed down 10 years ago this month, the South China Sea Arbitral Award found that China’s expansive “nine-dash line,” which asserts Beijing’s sovereignty over nearly all of the South China Sea, had no standing under UNCLOS, a treaty signed by both nations. It also found that Second Thomas Shoal, which Manila refers to as Ayungin Shoal, is part of the Philippines’ 200-nautical-mile Exclusive Economic Zone and continental shelf.
Last week, 14 nations issued a joint statement in support of the ruling, stating that it was “final and legally binding,” and rejecting any “destabilizing” actions in disputed waters. The 27-nation European Union also expressed its support for the award.
China has refused to acknowledge the tribunal’s ruling, describing it as “null and void,” and even implying that the United States and Japan somehow pushed the Philippines into instigating the case in 2013. It remains unclear how many other nations support China’s claim over Second Thomas Shoal.
Yesterday’s incident broke a stretch of relative quiet at Second Thomas Shoal, which was the primary focus of tensions between China and the Philippines in 2023 and 2024. This stemmed mostly from Chinese attempts to prevent the resupply of the Philippine forces aboard the BRP Sierra Madre. These attempts grew gradually more forceful, involving the ramming of Philippine Coast Guard ships and the use of high-powered water cannons against Philippine vessels.
The tensions culminated in July 2024 in a melee between the two sides that left eight Philippine naval personnel injured, including one who lost a thumb in the fighting. The two sides subsequently de-escalated the situation at Second Thomas Shoal, reaching a “provisional understanding” that allowed the Philippines to resupply the grounded warship. The situation at Second Thomas Shoal then calmed down, even as tensions persisted in other areas of the South China Sea.
The incident also took place just before Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi flew to Manila for a series of meetings hosted by the foreign ministers of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN). According to the South China Morning Post, he is expected to hold talks with Philippine Foreign Secretary Ma. Theresa Lazaro, “with discussions tipped to focus on the South China Sea.”
Philippine and Chinese personnel exchanged physical blows during a confrontation near a contested shoal in the South China Sea, with the two sides again accusing the other of instigating the incident.

The CCG boat reportedly circled the area while taking photos and videos, prompting two Philippine Navy rubber boats to “calmly and non-confrontationally” drive it away.
The Chinese personnel then “reacted violently and aggressively” by striking a Philippine marine on the head with a wooden baton, the AFP stated. The injured marine was then taken back to the BRP Sierra Madre to receive treatment. The incident also damaged one of the Navy’s rubber boats.
The AFP released a photo of the wound sustained by the marine, as well as a video, apparently shot from aboard the BRP Sierra Madre, that showed the CCG boat ramming the Philippine dinghy, and the personnel from both sides swinging oars and batons at each other.
The AFP said that the “aggressive actions” of the CCG “demonstrate a blatant disregard for safety, endanger the lives of our personnel, and escalate tensions in the West Philippine Sea.” The Department of National Defense issued its own statement asserting that the incident “adds to a clear pattern of provocative and hostile behavior, and further contributes to the People’s Republic of China’s deficit of trust and credibility.”
Unsurprisingly, the Chinese government has challenged this account. In a statement, the Chinese Embassy in Manila insisted that the CCG acted with “maximum rationality and restraint” while accusing the Philippines of spreading “false propaganda.” The CCG also put out its own statement, claiming that two rubber dinghies were launched “illegally” from the BRP Sierra Madre.
The Philippine vessels “ignored our repeated and explicit warnings, rapidly approached in a dangerous manner and rammed the Chinese patrol vessel from the front,” the CCG said. During the incident, Philippine personnel “first used tools such as oars and long sticks to maliciously attack Chinese law enforcement officers.”

articles.title: Truth as Resistance: Journalism’s Role in Defending Indigenous Asia
articles.full_text:
Journalism can help ensure that climate governance is guided by transparency, accountability, and justice.
Across South and Southeast Asia, climate action is being subverted by a deliberate, calculated strategy: the weaponization of climate disinformation. Governments and corporations are increasingly deploying misleading narratives – ranging from greenwashing to outright denial – to marginalize Indigenous peoples, facilitate land dispossession, and obscure ecological harm. 
This is a systematic campaign that threatens both human rights and the environmental integrity of the region. As these falsehoods harden into public consensus, the standard model of environmental reporting is no longer sufficient. 
In this clouded information environment, constructive journalism has a critical role to play. By scrutinizing official claims, exposing false climate solutions, and elevating the lived experiences of Indigenous communities, it can help ensure that climate governance is guided by transparency, accountability, and justice.
This is the key argument presented in Asia Centre’s newly published regional report, “Climate Disinformation in Asia: A Deliberate Strategy Against Indigenous Peoples.” The report synthesizes the findings from the broader series of country baseline studies to serve as a toolkit to advance the practice of constructive journalism in response to the growing challenge of climate disinformation in Asia.
The report finds that, across the region, climate disinformation is deployed as a deliberate strategy by selected corporations and governments against Indigenous peoples to access their ancestral lands for development.
Four main forms of climate disinformation are apparent. The first is one-sided media coverage, which selectively amplifies government and corporate narratives while downplaying ecological harm, Indigenous displacement, and government and corporate accountability for these harms.
The second is greenwashing, where corporations use misleading environmental claims and sustainability branding to obscure harmful practices and create a false impression of climate progress.
The third is the promotion of false climate solutions, which present unsustainable or exploitative programs as genuine climate action despite evidence of ecological harm and limited climate benefits.
The fourth is the outright denial of accountability, whereby governments and corporations reject, minimize, or deflect evidence of environmental harm and human rights abuses while shifting responsibility onto Indigenous communities, activists, and other marginalized groups. 
In addition, two regional-level trends can be observed. One, climate disinformation is increasingly reinforced by transnational political and corporate interests that replicate extractive narratives across borders. Two, digital platforms’ inability to moderate disinformation allows such content to proliferate.
Such concerted deployment of climate disinformation has a significant consequence on the Indigenous peoples of the region. The report documents five interconnected impacts. First, climate disinformation excludes Indigenous communities from decision-making processes and undermines their right to free, prior and informed consent (FPIC) on projects affecting their land and livelihood.
Second, it erodes traditional knowledge systems and ways of life by portraying Indigenous practices as inferior to state-led and technocratic approaches and therefore justifying their erasure.
Third, it facilitates land dispossession and forced displacement by presenting harmful development, conservation and climate initiatives as necessary or beneficial.
Fourth, it contributes to the criminalization of Indigenous peoples and their advocates through false evidence of criminality.
Finally, it enables intimidation and physical violence against Indigenous communities and environmental defenders by portraying them as environmental threats, criminals or obstacles to development.
To address these challenges, the report points to the need for a coordinated response involving governments, international organizations, civil society, the media, technology companies, and Indigenous communities themselves. 
Central recommendations include strengthening legal recognition of Indigenous identity and rights, ensuring meaningful implementation of FPIC, improving accountability for climate disinformation, protecting Indigenous land and environmental defenders, and supporting Indigenous-led monitoring and media initiatives.
At the regional level, stronger regional cooperation is needed to counter transnational disinformation networks, corporate greenwashing, and the growing risks posed by AI-enabled climate disinformation.
The report cautions that all of this is unfolding at a time when fact-checking institutions are under increasing strain across Asia and beyond. Hence, constructive journalism has an even more critical role to play.
By investigating one-sided reporting, exposing greenwashing and false climate solutions, and elevating Indigenous knowledge and lived experiences, it can help counter climate disinformation and ensure that climate governance is guided by transparency, accountability and justice rather than misleading narratives. 
Ultimately, addressing climate disinformation requires recognizing Indigenous peoples not merely as vulnerable populations, but as rights holders whose knowledge, participation and self-determination are essential to effective and just climate action.
This op-ed is based on Asia Centre’s new report, “Climate Disinformation in Asia: A Deliberate Strategy Against Indigenous Peoples.” Download the full report here. For more information about the Asia Centre, visit asiacentre.org.

articles.title: Japan’s New Flag Law Tests Free Expression
articles.full_text:
The law forbids publicly damaging, removing, or defacing a Japanese flag in a manner likely to cause others “extreme discomfort or disgust.”
On July 17, Japan’s upper house passed legislation criminalizing the public desecration of the national flag, known as the Hinomaru, after the bill cleared the lower house on June 30. The law provides for up to two years in prison or a fine of up to 200,000 yen for publicly damaging, removing, or defacing a Japanese flag in a manner likely to cause others “extreme discomfort or disgust.” The measure addresses conduct that many people regard as deeply disrespectful, but it also raises a difficult question: how far should the state go in protecting a national symbol from political expression?
Public opinion offers no clear answer to where that line should be drawn. A nationwide public opinion survey conducted by the Social Survey Research Center in April found that 40 percent of respondents supported prohibiting damage to the national flag with criminal penalties. Another 21 percent favored a prohibition without penalties, while 19 percent saw no need for a ban, and 19 percent were unsure. Although a majority supported some form of restriction, the results show no clear public consensus on whether criminal penalties are appropriate, with a substantial share of respondents yet to form a clear view.
Supporters can reasonably argue that deliberate flag burning or trampling can be intended to provoke or insult and that the flag merits legal protection. The law acknowledges the competing concern by requiring consideration for freedom of expression. But that general safeguard does not answer whether the offense is defined clearly enough. Its central test still depends partly on whether an act is likely to cause “extreme discomfort or disgust” among others, making the boundary less predictable than offenses based on property damage, threats, or disruption of public order.
Supporters have also presented the law as a matter of consistency. Japan already criminalizes damaging, removing, or defacing a foreign flag for the purpose of insulting another country. Yet the comparison is incomplete. The foreign-flag offense is part of the Penal Code’s protection of diplomatic relations. It requires a specific intent to insult the foreign state and may be prosecuted only at request of the government of the country whose flag was affected. The domestic offense does not require the same intent. It therefore serves a different and broader purpose: protecting public respect for Japan’s flag.
That difference also matters when assessing whether a separate offense is necessary. Publicized incidents of Hinomaru desecration have been rare, while conduct involving another person’s flag, dangerous fire, trespass, or disruption can already be addressed under existing laws against property damage, fire, trespass, or disruption. A Mainichi editorial similarly questioned the need for a new offense.
As a result, the new provision may be particularly relevant to symbolic protests involving a person’s own flag.
The flag’s history is important to understanding the debate. The red sun motif has roots in ancient sun worship and Japan’s identification as the “land of the rising sun,” although the exact origin of the modern design is uncertain. The Tokugawa shogunate designated the Hinomaru as a maritime ensign in 1854. In 1870, the Meiji government prescribed it for Japanese merchant ships, after which it functioned as the country’s de facto flag. It did not receive full statutory recognition until 1999, when the Act on National Flag and Anthem was enacted.
As the flag became more closely linked to the modern state, it also acquired strong associations with wartime mobilization. Soldiers commonly carried flags bearing messages and signatures from relatives and friends. After Japan’s defeat, the Allied occupation authorities initially restricted its use, and memories of the war continued to shape how some people viewed it. From the 1950s, the education ministry promoted the flag and the anthem, Kimigayo, in schools, with stricter enforcement from the 1980s. Disciplinary action against teachers who refused to participate in ceremonies helped keep both symbols politically contested after 1999.
This divided history helps explain why the Hinomaru carries different meanings today. For many people in Japan, it is an ordinary and positive national symbol displayed at public institutions and sporting events. Others associate it with wartime nationalism or efforts to require patriotic observance in schools. Because these contrasting interpretations remain part of public debate, the state should protect both the freedom to display the flag and the freedom to criticize what it represents.
The parliamentary debate suggests that drawing this line in practice may not always be straightforward. Lawmakers indicated that anime, cartoons, artificial intelligence-generated images, flags depicted within paintings, and the miniature paper flags used to decorate children’s meals would generally fall outside the law. By contrast, livestreaming or uploading footage of cutting or burning a physical flag in a private place could be covered. These examples provide some guidance, but they also show how much enforcement will depend on context.
Context-dependent enforcement does not mean that police or prosecutors will necessarily apply the law broadly. The freedom-of-expression clause, prosecutorial discretion, and judicial review may restrain its use. Still, uncertainty can affect behavior before a case reaches court. Artists, journalists, protesters, and event organizers may avoid borderline uses because the legal consequences are difficult to assess. That possible chilling effect is a practical concern even if prosecutions remain uncommon. Some critics have pressed this concern further, warning that its vague standard could be used to intimidate the public and discourage criticism of the Takaichi government. 
For that reason, the constitutional debate deserves careful attention. In July, 148 academics, including specialists in criminal and constitutional law, publicly opposed the bill, arguing that its reliance on public feelings could restrict expression and lead to uneven enforcement. Their objections do not by themselves establish that the law is unconstitutional. That question would ultimately have to be determined by the courts in a concrete case. They do, however, raise credible concerns about whether the scope of the offense is sufficiently clear and whether it can be applied consistently.
International comparisons offer no single model. Germany criminalizes publicly reviling or damaging its federal flag, while the United Kingdom has no specific flag-desecration offense. France restricts insults to the flag or anthem at publicly organized events. In the United States, the Supreme Court protects flag burning as political expression, even if the Trump administration issued an executive order promising to prosecute such behavior. These differences do not resolve Japan’s debate, but they underscore the need to define prohibited conduct and safeguards for political expression clearly.
Encouraging respect for the national flag is an understandable goal. The more difficult question is whether this law defines the prohibited conduct and the protected interest with enough precision. Authorities should issue clear and narrow enforcement guidelines, courts should examine prosecutions closely, and lawmakers should monitor whether the measure deters lawful protest or artistic expression. 
National symbols are most durable when citizens respect them voluntarily. Criminal penalties should therefore remain a limited response, reserved for conduct defined with sufficient clarity.
On July 17, Japan’s upper house passed legislation criminalizing the public desecration of the national flag, known as the Hinomaru, after the bill cleared the lower house on June 30. The law provides for up to two years in prison or a fine of up to 200,000 yen for publicly damaging, removing, or defacing a Japanese flag in a manner likely to cause others “extreme discomfort or disgust.” The measure addresses conduct that many people regard as deeply disrespectful, but it also raises a difficult question: how far should the state go in protecting a national symbol from political expression?
Public opinion offers no clear answer to where that line should be drawn. A nationwide public opinion survey conducted by the Social Survey Research Center in April found that 40 percent of respondents supported prohibiting damage to the national flag with criminal penalties. Another 21 percent favored a prohibition without penalties, while 19 percent saw no need for a ban, and 19 percent were unsure. Although a majority supported some form of restriction, the results show no clear public consensus on whether criminal penalties are appropriate, with a substantial share of respondents yet to form a clear view.
Supporters can reasonably argue that deliberate flag burning or trampling can be intended to provoke or insult and that the flag merits legal protection. The law acknowledges the competing concern by requiring consideration for freedom of expression. But that general safeguard does not answer whether the offense is defined clearly enough. Its central test still depends partly on whether an act is likely to cause “extreme discomfort or disgust” among others, making the boundary less predictable than offenses based on property damage, threats, or disruption of public order.
Supporters have also presented the law as a matter of consistency. Japan already criminalizes damaging, removing, or defacing a foreign flag for the purpose of insulting another country. Yet the comparison is incomplete. The foreign-flag offense is part of the Penal Code’s protection of diplomatic relations. It requires a specific intent to insult the foreign state and may be prosecuted only at request of the government of the country whose flag was affected. The domestic offense does not require the same intent. It therefore serves a different and broader purpose: protecting public respect for Japan’s flag.
That difference also matters when assessing whether a separate offense is necessary. Publicized incidents of Hinomaru desecration have been rare, while conduct involving another person’s flag, dangerous fire, trespass, or disruption can already be addressed under existing laws against property damage, fire, trespass, or disruption. A Mainichi editorial similarly questioned the need for a new offense.
As a result, the new provision may be particularly relevant to symbolic protests involving a person’s own flag.
The flag’s history is important to understanding the debate. The red sun motif has roots in ancient sun worship and Japan’s identification as the “land of the rising sun,” although the exact origin of the modern design is uncertain. The Tokugawa shogunate designated the Hinomaru as a maritime ensign in 1854. In 1870, the Meiji government prescribed it for Japanese merchant ships, after which it functioned as the country’s de facto flag. It did not receive full statutory recognition until 1999, when the Act on National Flag and Anthem was enacted.
As the flag became more closely linked to the modern state, it also acquired strong associations with wartime mobilization. Soldiers commonly carried flags bearing messages and signatures from relatives and friends. After Japan’s defeat, the Allied occupation authorities initially restricted its use, and memories of the war continued to shape how some people viewed it. From the 1950s, the education ministry promoted the flag and the anthem, Kimigayo, in schools, with stricter enforcement from the 1980s. Disciplinary action against teachers who refused to participate in ceremonies helped keep both symbols politically contested after 1999.
This divided history helps explain why the Hinomaru carries different meanings today. For many people in Japan, it is an ordinary and positive national symbol displayed at public institutions and sporting events. Others associate it with wartime nationalism or efforts to require patriotic observance in schools. Because these contrasting interpretations remain part of public debate, the state should protect both the freedom to display the flag and the freedom to criticize what it represents.
The parliamentary debate suggests that drawing this line in practice may not always be straightforward. Lawmakers indicated that anime, cartoons, artificial intelligence-generated images, flags depicted within paintings, and the miniature paper flags used to decorate children’s meals would generally fall outside the law. By contrast, livestreaming or uploading footage of cutting or burning a physical flag in a private place could be covered. These examples provide some guidance, but they also show how much enforcement will depend on context.
Context-dependent enforcement does not mean that police or prosecutors will necessarily apply the law broadly. The freedom-of-expression clause, prosecutorial discretion, and judicial review may restrain its use. Still, uncertainty can affect behavior before a case reaches court. Artists, journalists, protesters, and event organizers may avoid borderline uses because the legal consequences are difficult to assess. That possible chilling effect is a practical concern even if prosecutions remain uncommon. Some critics have pressed this concern further, warning that its vague standard could be used to intimidate the public and discourage criticism of the Takaichi government. 
For that reason, the constitutional debate deserves careful attention. In July, 148 academics, including specialists in criminal and constitutional law, publicly opposed the bill, arguing that its reliance on public feelings could restrict expression and lead to uneven enforcement. Their objections do not by themselves establish that the law is unconstitutional. That question would ultimately have to be determined by the courts in a concrete case. They do, however, raise credible concerns about whether the scope of the offense is sufficiently clear and whether it can be applied consistently.
International comparisons offer no single model. Germany criminalizes publicly reviling or damaging its federal flag, while the United Kingdom has no specific flag-desecration offense. France restricts insults to the flag or anthem at publicly organized events. In the United States, the Supreme Court protects flag burning as political expression, even if the Trump administration issued an executive order promising to prosecute such behavior. These differences do not resolve Japan’s debate, but they underscore the need to define prohibited conduct and safeguards for political expression clearly.
Encouraging respect for the national flag is an understandable goal. The more difficult question is whether this law defines the prohibited conduct and the protected interest with enough precision. Authorities should issue clear and narrow enforcement guidelines, courts should examine prosecutions closely, and lawmakers should monitor whether the measure deters lawful protest or artistic expression.
articles.summary: The law forbids publicly damaging, removing, or defacing a Japanese flag in a manner likely to cause others “extreme discomfort or disgust.”

articles.title: China’s New AI Club: The World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization
articles.full_text:
Global tech governance is taking an institutional turn as a bloc-based system continues to solidify.
U.N. Secretary General Antonio Guterres attends the World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai, China, July 17, 2026.
On July 16, a day before the annual World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai, officials from 29 countries signed an agreement establishing a new body: the World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization (WAICO). The event was attended by United Nations Secretary-General António Guterres and the deputy prime ministers of Kazakhstan, Laos, and Pakistan, among other dignitaries. Foreign Minister Wang Yi represented China at the signing ceremony.
The new organization, first proposed by China at the 2025 World Artificial Intelligence Conference, will be headquartered in Shanghai and is meant to operate as an independent intergovernmental entity. WAICO’s stated aim is to promote international cooperation on AI so that it remains beneficial, safe, and fair for all, in line with the principles of the U.N. Charter and with a people-centered approach.
In his address at the opening of the 2026 WAIC the next day, President Xi Jinping set out China’s view that AI development should be a shared endeavor across nations, rather than something driven by a single nation. He announced plans to provide 5,000 training spots for developing countries over the next five years and to establish cooperation centers with groups like ASEAN, the African Union, and BRICS. 
The WAICO agreement stresses open participation without conditions tied to political systems or values. It places strong, or rather pointed, emphasis on narrowing the technology-access gap between richer and poorer nations. The founding members include a somewhat predictable list: Russia, Pakistan, Indonesia, Brazil, Kazakhstan, and other countries from Asia, Africa, and Latin America, with more to be revealed. Given the thinly veiled anti-Western edge, it is hardly surprising that no Western or strongly Western aligned country showed up among the founding nations, nor are they expected to do so in the future.
This composition points quite strongly to an emerging split in AI governance along geopolitical lines. Over time, this may well solidify into parallel ecosystems for standards, data governance, and infrastructure, with limited interoperability between them, further complicating an already fragmented landscape.
Although the emergence of WAICO might strike one as incredibly fast – it took just a single year from the proposal to the actual founding – China has been working toward this step for some time. Last year’s proposal formed part of Beijing’s broader Global AI Governance Initiative, which argues that rules should treat development needs as seriously as safety concerns. 
Beijing has criticized international efforts, such as those hosted in the United Kingdom, South Korea, France and India, for they tended to bring together advanced economies and focus heavily on managing high-risk systems and agreeing standards among close partners. Positioning itself as a champion of equitable AI access for developing nations, Beijing has argued that such arrangements exclude too many countries that need help to catch up on skills, data, and computing power. 
Indeed, Xi framed the initiative as a direct response to calls from the Global South. In other words, WAICO is not presented as a China-led initiative, but as a practical forum where members can, without entry barriers, work on training programs, joint projects, and basic governance ideas.
In practice, with this step China has gained a significant venue to shape conversations about standards and policy language, the importance and possible long-term effects of which cannot be understated. Yet this is not necessarily the most important angle for Beijing. WAICO allows China to offer structured support to developing countries in the form of access to models, infrastructure assistance, and technical know-how; this is the prize China has been seeking. Such support can help to expand China’s influence and cement Beijing’s role as the leading alternative provider of AI capacity outside Western-led frameworks. 
There are many ways in which such influence is likely to manifest itself, including standard-setting, preferred technical architectures, and embedded dependencies in training, cloud infrastructure, and access to models. Each of these channels has a strong potential to shape long-term governance outcomes in ways that are far less visible than formal treaties, yet often prove far more enduring.
Reactions in Western capitals have thus far been muted. Among ASEAN countries, the picture looks generally positive in the short term. Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, and Myanmar are founding members of WAICO, and China has included ASEAN among the regional blocs for which it will set up AI application cooperation centers focused on practical uses. Predictably, governments in the region have welcomed the Chinese emphasis on capacity building and reducing the technology gap, especially since many face similar challenges around skills and infrastructure. 
Whether WAICO grows into something substantial beyond a vehicle of Chinese influence depends on what happens next. Turning a signing ceremony into working groups, shared projects, and actual funding, not only in the immediate future but also for the long term, will take time and sustained effort. It also remains to be seen how many more countries might decide to join WAICO or engage without formal membership. An important early indicator of traction observers will have to watch for is whether WAICO outputs begin to be referenced in national AI strategies or regulatory frameworks.
The broader landscape of AI rules is already quite crowded, with U.N. processes, regional initiatives, and national strategies all running at once. Adding a new standing organization has the potential to radically change the existing dynamics: instead of only debating principles at occasional meetings, countries will now have the option of committing to different institutional tracks.
This may open up Pandora’s institutional box as it introduces competition, where states can “forum shop” for venues that best match their political preferences and development priorities rather than converge on a single global framework. This potential fragmentation would dilute consensus building, but on the other hand it will likely also accelerate experimentation, as different blocs test governance models in parallel.
Announced with great fanfare, WAICO matters less as a headline announcement than for what it represents: a move from talking about cooperation to building structures that can deliver it. Whether it will actually become influential will depend on what follows: funded projects, shared tools, and the extent to which countries begin to rely on it in practice rather than simply endorse it in principle. If that happens, WAICO could become a real node in how AI is governed globally. 
Either way, the direction is clear: AI governance is no longer confined to occasional summits but is starting to take shape through competing institutions with their own priorities and constituencies. As a consequence, the global architecture of AI governance has just become more complex – and more contested.

articles.title: With New AI Governance Organization, China Seeks to Formalize Its Global AI Influence
articles.full_text:
The World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization may prove to be China’s most ambitious effort yet to shape the future deployment and governance of AI.
On July 17, Xi Jinping delivered the keynote address at the World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai, marking the first time China’s leader has addressed the event in-person since its launch in 2018. His appearance underscored the growing importance Beijing attaches to shaping the global governance of artificial intelligence (AI). In his speech, Xi emphasized four observations, including the promotion of “true multilateralism,” language Beijing increasingly uses to justify the establishment of a parallel global order under the Chinese Communist Party’s leadership.
In line with China’s ambitions to embed its own institutions into the global AI governance ecosystem, the most consequential announcement of the conference came a day before Xi’s speech, with the formal establishment of the World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization (WAICO), to be headquartered in Shanghai. The new international organization may prove to be China’s most ambitious effort yet to shape the future deployment and governance of AI.
Building on China’s 2023 Global AI Governance Initiative, the idea for a China-led international organization on AI was first proposed by Premier Li Qiang at the 2025 conference and reiterated in the Five-Year Plan (2026-2030) passed in March. WAICO signals China’s shift from articulating principles for AI governance to building permanent institutions through which those principles can be operationalized and normalized.
Precisely because it will be a focal point for entrenching China’s approach to AI, its potential success as a norms-setting venue risks enshrining digital authoritarian approaches to AI governance.
Twenty-nine countries signed the agreement establishing WAICO on July 16. The founding members were largely from Africa and the Middle East (Algeria, Cameroon, Congo, Ethiopia, Kenya, Lesotho, Mozambique, Oman, Senegal, and South Africa) and Asia (Cambodia, China, Indonesia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Pakistan, Tajikistan, and Uzbekistan). From Europe, the signatories were Belarus, Russia, and Serbia, while in Latin America, Brazil, Cuba, Nicaragua, and Venezuela pledged their endorsement. Many of these countries already participate in China’s Digital Silk Road, Belt and Road Initiative, or broader digital cooperation programs, which have fueled rising digital repression.
In his keynote address, Xi Jinping hailed the establishment of the new organization as “a major move by China to answer the call of the Global South and unite the international community together to vigorously promote AI development and governance.”
This framing reflects a broader continuity in China’s approach to global digital governance. The stated commitment to Global South partnership and implied win-win cooperation in AI is guided by China’s earlier rhetorical framing on “a shared future in cyberspace,” which seeks to normalize “cyber sovereignty “– the notion that the digital domain is a natural extension of national sovereignty and that digital governance should be the sole providence of states, unencumbered by international human rights obligations or accountability to independent stakeholders. WAICO risks extending such approaches into the evolving domain of AI governance.
Institutions matter not simply because of the ideas they espouse, but because of the mechanisms through which they diffuse tools and shape norms. International organizations create influence by convening governments, training officials, developing technical standards, coordinating research, and establishing enduring networks. These activities gradually shape what becomes accepted as international best practice.
In that sense, China does not even need WAICO itself to supplant existing fora. China just needs its new organization to become a significant venue for the collective normalization of China’s approaches to AI governance. Participating member-countries will then actively promote China’s preferred approach at other fora that remain more influential.
Xi’s keynote outlined areas of priority for Beijing. To “support global AI development and advance global AI capacity building,” over the next five years, China pledged to provide 5,000 AI training opportunities for developing countries. China will establish AI cooperation centers with ASEAN, the African Union, the League of Arab States, the Community of Latin American and Caribbean States, the Shanghai Cooperation Organization, and BRICS, and invite 30 countries to use China’s AI-powered meteorological warning system, MAZU. These initiatives are more than development assistance. They create channels through which Chinese technologies, regulatory approaches, and standards also become embedded within partner countries’ AI ecosystems.
This approach also reflects China’s growing emphasis on AI as infrastructure. Rather than exporting only individual AI applications, Beijing increasingly seeks to provide integrated ecosystems of models, services, computing resources, standards, training, and digital public infrastructure.
China’s domestic AI industrial strategy reinforces this international ambition. The latest Five-Year Plan calls for accelerating the adoption of Chinese AI technologies globally.
Pointing to one area of strategic priority for Beijing, Xi also used his speech to reiterate China’s support for open-source AI development. Open-source is often presented as a means of democratizing AI, but it also serves a strategic purpose by encouraging the widespread adoption of Chinese models and tools. It is likely no coincidence that China’s Moonshot AI chose to align the release of its latest open-source model, Kimi K3, with Xi’s remarks. Moonshot AI, one of the so-called six AI Tigers, claims Kimi K3 performs as well as leading models from OpenAI and Anthropic at some key tasks. Arguably, this illustrates how China’s commercial AI ecosystem increasingly complements its diplomatic efforts to expand international influence, which will likely see greater facilitation through WAICO partnership.
None of this guarantees that WAICO will become a dominant institution in global AI governance. It remains more aspirational. Even so, when a government whose domestic AI and digital governance approach has consistently normalized digital authoritarian practices seeks to influence the international institutions that may shape the future technologies that govern our lives, it demands heightened caution.
China presented the World AI Cooperation Organization as an expression of “true multilateralism.” Yet its broader record in digital governance suggests the organization will promote a state-centric model of AI governance — one that places governments, rather than multistakeholder institutions and human rights safeguards, at the center of the emerging international AI order. Such an approach risks dislodging human rights safeguards from emerging technologies and AI norms.
International norms are rarely determined by a single declaration or summit. They emerge through the steady accumulation of institutions, standards, technical cooperation, and professional networks. Watching how this institution evolves will be important.
