articles.title: Australia’s Tuvalu Migration Deal Tests the Limits of Climate Mobility
articles.full_text:
The Falepili Union offers Tuvaluans “mobility with dignity.” But unless Australia also protects Tuvalu’s workforce, the landmark agreement could weaken the country years before rising seas make it uninhabitable.
Australia’s climate mobility pact with Tuvalu is often presented as a humane answer to a problem international law has barely begun to confront: how to give people a safe, orderly choice to move before their homeland becomes unliveable.
The first year of the Falepili Mobility Pathway has exposed a harder question. What happens when an escape route begins drawing workers out of a fragile state faster than that state can replace them?
The Australia-Tuvalu Falepili Union treaty, signed in 2023 and in force since August 2024, created a pathway for up to 280 Tuvaluans annually to receive permanent residence in Australia. Visa holders may work, study, and access Australian health, education, and family support. 
Demand has been extraordinary. According to an Australian Department of Home Affairs report, the inaugural ballot attracted 2,474 primary registrations, representing 8,750 people when spouses and dependent children were included. The full allocation of 280 visas was granted for 2025-26, and Australia proceeded with a second ballot in 2026. 
These figures alone do not prove that Tuvalu is being emptied. The annual ceiling includes family members, meaning it does not represent 280 workers. Visa holders are also free to travel between Australia and Tuvalu, and migration can produce remittances, education, and professional networks that benefit those who remain.
In addition, Australian officials have argued that random selection prevents the program from favoring Tuvalu’s most educated applicants. The ballot is designed to reduce institutional and gender imbalances. 
Yet random selection does not prevent a randomly chosen essential worker from leaving. Tuvalu is a country of about 10,000 people; its workforce resilience cannot be assessed using assumptions designed for Australia. A large state can absorb the departure of a dentist, journalist, or specialist technician. In a Pacific microstate, losing one qualified person may mean losing a substantial part of an entire profession.
Tuvalu’s staffing crisis predates the visa.The government’s 2025-26 civil service report listed 1,360 established positions, of which 343, roughly one-quarter, were vacant. The report described persistent recruitment and retention problems; the vacancy rate rose from 21 percent in 2022 to 27 percent in 2023. Thirty-two officials resigned during the reporting period, compared with five who retired. 
The Falepili Mobility Pathway should therefore be understood not as the cause of a new crisis, but as a potential force multiplier for an existing one.
In Tuvalu, many public services depend on a very small number of qualified professionals, meaning the loss of even one nurse, teacher, technician, or senior administrator can create a national-level gap. The Falepili pathway’s lottery system may avoid openly selecting the most educated applicants, but it does nothing to protect critical occupations. 
A random migration scheme can therefore produce highly uneven consequences: manageable for Australia, but potentially destabilizing for a state whose institutions already struggle with vacancies, recruitment and retention.
Individual decisions to migrate are entirely understandable. Better salaries, schools, and health care are precisely what make the pathway valuable. But those advantages also make permanent settlement more likely than the treaty’s language of “circular mobility” suggests.
Circularity is a legal permission, not an economic incentive. A professional who is allowed to return to Tuvalu may still be unable to justify returning to lower pay, limited housing, and an understaffed workplace. Without funded positions and credible career pathways at home, “skills circulation” risks becoming a diplomatic phrase for one-way migration.
Australia’s own Tuvalu development plan reveals this imbalance. Its publicly listed indicators count visas delivered, settlement experiences in Australia, and increases in remittances. They do not establish similarly clear targets for retaining Tuvaluan health workers, teachers, engineers, or civil servants, or for measuring whether essential services in Tuvalu deteriorate after each intake. 
This is the central weakness of many Western climate mobility frameworks: they measure successful departures but rarely measure the institutional capacity left behind. A visa can protect an individual while making the remaining community less capable of surviving the climate threat.
The imbalance also carries geopolitical consequences, as the Falepili Union requires Tuvalu to reach mutual agreement with Australia on certain security and defense relationships with other states. If workforce losses leave Tuvalu increasingly dependent on Australian personnel and technical assistance, migration could deepen the power asymmetry already built into the treaty. 
The answer is not to prevent skilled Tuvaluans from leaving; that would force essential workers to bear the cost of national survival. Instead, Australia should attach a guaranteed “mobility dividend” to every intake: funding to train replacements, salary supplements for scarce occupations, and paid fellowships enabling diaspora professionals to return for fixed periods.
The treaty’s joint committee should also publish an annual workforce impact assessment and adjust the intake when staffing in health, education, utilities, or public administration reaches critical levels.
The Falepili Union is a groundbreaking expansion of choice for Tuvaluans. Its success, however, cannot be judged only by whether migrants settle safely in Australia. “Mobility with dignity” must also protect the right to remain, and ensure Tuvalu’s institutions do not disappear before its islands do.
Australia’s climate mobility pact with Tuvalu is often presented as a humane answer to a problem international law has barely begun to confront: how to give people a safe, orderly choice to move before their homeland becomes unliveable.
The first year of the Falepili Mobility Pathway has exposed a harder question. What happens when an escape route begins drawing workers out of a fragile state faster than that state can replace them?
The Australia-Tuvalu Falepili Union treaty, signed in 2023 and in force since August 2024, created a pathway for up to 280 Tuvaluans annually to receive permanent residence in Australia. Visa holders may work, study, and access Australian health, education, and family support. 
Demand has been extraordinary. According to an Australian Department of Home Affairs report, the inaugural ballot attracted 2,474 primary registrations, representing 8,750 people when spouses and dependent children were included. The full allocation of 280 visas was granted for 2025-26, and Australia proceeded with a second ballot in 2026. 
These figures alone do not prove that Tuvalu is being emptied. The annual ceiling includes family members, meaning it does not represent 280 workers. Visa holders are also free to travel between Australia and Tuvalu, and migration can produce remittances, education, and professional networks that benefit those who remain.
In addition, Australian officials have argued that random selection prevents the program from favoring Tuvalu’s most educated applicants. The ballot is designed to reduce institutional and gender imbalances. 
Yet random selection does not prevent a randomly chosen essential worker from leaving. Tuvalu is a country of about 10,000 people; its workforce resilience cannot be assessed using assumptions designed for Australia. A large state can absorb the departure of a dentist, journalist, or specialist technician. In a Pacific microstate, losing one qualified person may mean losing a substantial part of an entire profession.
Tuvalu’s staffing crisis predates the visa.The government’s 2025-26 civil service report listed 1,360 established positions, of which 343, roughly one-quarter, were vacant. The report described persistent recruitment and retention problems; the vacancy rate rose from 21 percent in 2022 to 27 percent in 2023. Thirty-two officials resigned during the reporting period, compared with five who retired. 
The Falepili Mobility Pathway should therefore be understood not as the cause of a new crisis, but as a potential force multiplier for an existing one.
In Tuvalu, many public services depend on a very small number of qualified professionals, meaning the loss of even one nurse, teacher, technician, or senior administrator can create a national-level gap. The Falepili pathway’s lottery system may avoid openly selecting the most educated applicants, but it does nothing to protect critical occupations.
articles.summary: The Falepili Union offers Tuvaluans “mobility with dignity.” But unless Australia also protects Tuvalu’s workforce, the landmark agreement could weaken the country years before rising seas make it uninhabitable.

articles.title: Philippines’ Lazaro Dismisses Criticisms Over ASEAN’s ‘Informal Meeting’ With Myanmar FM
articles.full_text:
Last week, the Special Advisory Council for Myanmar said that agreeing to the meeting was a sign of the ASEAN special envoy’s “timidity and poor judgment.”
Ma. Theresa Lazaro, Philippine foreign secretary and current ASEAN special envoy on Myanmar, poses with her ASEAN counterparts following an “informal meeting” in Bangkok, Thailand, Jul. 12, 2026.
The Philippines has dismissed a strong criticism from an expert advisory group that the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN)’s recent engagement with Myanmar’s foreign minister undermined the bloc’s peace efforts, stating that it continues to adhere to the bloc’s Five-Point Consensus plan for managing the conflict.
As this year’s chair of ASEAN, the Philippines hosted an “informal meeting” with Myanmar Foreign Minister Tin Maung Swe in Bangkok on July 12, the first such engagement since the coup of 2021.
Following the meeting, the Special Advisory Council for Myanmar (SAC-M), an independent group of international experts, said that ASEAN’s decision to hold the meeting had “rewarded the Myanmar military junta’s bloodshed and recalcitrance” and undermined the Five-Point Consensus.
Agreed in April 2021, two months after Myanmar’s military seized power in a coup, the Consensus called for an immediate cessation of violence, inclusive dialogue involving “all parties” to the country’s conflict, and unimpeded access for humanitarian aid. However, in the five years since, the military has done very little to implement the most important elements of the Consensus.
In a statement authored by former Malaysian Foreign Minister Saifuddin Abdullah, ex-Thai Foreign Minister Kasit Piromya, and three former U.N. experts on Myanmar, SAC-M argued that Philippine Foreign Secretary Ma. Theresa Lazaro, the current ASEAN special envoy on Myanmar, had brought “a disastrous combination of timidity and poor judgment to her role” since taking over at the start of 2026.
SAC-M accused Lazaro of agreeing to the meeting “without extracting a single concession from junta leader and accused war criminal Min Aung Hlaing.”
“ASEAN’s failure on Myanmar while the country burns and the junta entrenches itself poses serious questions about the bloc’s durability,” SAC-M stated. It finished by calling for a “reset” at next week’s ASEAN meetings in Pasay City, when the bloc’s foreign ministers are set to hold a “extended informal consultation” on the implementation of the Five-Point Consensus.
In a response yesterday, Lazaro described SAC-M’s assertions as “malicious and misleading,” and stated that the meeting formed “part of the Special Envoy’s mandate to pursue constructive dialogue among all parties” to Myanmar’s conflict.
“It was not, and should not be mischaracterized as an abandonment of the [Five-Point Consensus],” Lazaro said, arguing that this remained the ASEAN’s “primary reference to address the political crisis in Myanmar.”
“The path to peace can only be obtained through resolute diplomacy with all parties to the conflict,” she wrote.
The exchange between Lazaro and the SAC-M reflects a broader disagreement about how ASEAN should approach the situation in Myanmar.
Since late 2021, the 11-nation bloc has barred Myanmar’s military regime from its summits over its lack of efforts to implement the Five-Point Consensus. But in recent months, ASEAN has begun a process of “calibrated engagement” with the new military-backed civilian government that took office in April – even as the Myanmar military continues to launch fatal air strikes and other military attacks across the country. On top of that, the military-dominated Myanmar parliament recently passed a motion rejecting the Five-Point Consensus as a form of interference by ASEAN that was inconsistent with the country’s “political reality.”
In these circumstances, it certainly seems perverse to seek re-engagement with Naypyidaw. The reality, however, is that after five years, the demand from ASEAN’s member states for a harder line on Myanmar has waned, while some member states, particular Thailand, have pushed hard to bring Myanmar back into the “ASEAN family.”
While SAC-M is right to argue that ASEAN should respond to the Myanmar parliament’s  rejection of the Five-Point Consensus by laying out “an escalating series of consequences triggered by continued junta non-compliance,” it is very unlikely to obtain the necessary consensus among its member-states for such a course of action.
Unfortunately, the most pressing question moving forward is therefore what Myanmar’s military is willing to offer ASEAN in order to obtain full normalization, and whether ASEAN will accept it.

articles.title: 5 Myths About the Five Eyes
articles.full_text:
There are persistent misunderstandings about what the intelligence partnership is, and what it actually does.
Linking Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the United Kingdom and the United States, the Five Eyes is a club that plays in a league of its own.  Starting in the middle years of the 20th century, the intelligence agencies of the five nations have shared – or pooled – much of their work, everything from cryptoanalysis to cybersecurity.  
Although the U.S. agencies are dominant, the arrangement works because it enables each partner to collect and analyze far more information than it could have alone. It also works because of a high degree of mutual trust – the belief that your partners will have your back despite the risks involved.
But thanks to its deep dependency on an increasingly volatile United States, the Five Eyes has now become brittle. 
Trampling constitutional and democratic norms at every step, U.S. President Donald Trump has been fundamentally hostile to the U.S. intelligence system from the beginning.  In his recent speech on election interference on July 16, 2027, he blamed “corrupt” anti-Trump officials in the FBI and the CIA for allegedly covering-up “evidence” about the vulnerability of U.S. election security to China and other nefarious actors. 
The Trump administration has also tried to weaponize the power asymmetry within the Five Eyes itself, vowing to “punish” select partners for failing to support Washington’s policies. 
In 2025, Canada was threatened with removal from the Five Eyes because the government in Ottawa refused to acquiesce to Trump’s tariffs. This year a leaked Pentagon memo suggested the White House threatened to withdraw support for British sovereignty over the Falkland Islands because the U.K. government showed no enthusiasm for the U.S. assault on Iran. 
Partner nations have struggled with this new reality. Last year the U.K. reportedly suspended sharing intelligence on the Caribbean with the United States out of concern that the U.S. military might be using it illegally. This year the Canadian government felt compelled to remind the Trump administration to “respect Canadian sovereignty” after a group of separatist leaders in Alberta visited U.S. government offices in Washington, D.C.  
Yet day-to-day cooperation has stayed the course, with intelligence agencies navigating tensions between their respective governments much as they have done in the past. As with NATO campaign planning and the Ukraine Contact Group,  the Five Eyes continues to have the United States as a full participant at the staff level, especially when seasoned professionals are involved.
The unpredictability of the current geopolitical moment no doubt makes it difficult to assess the future of the Five Eyes. Yet this challenge is also compounded by a set of persistent misunderstandings about what the partnership is, and what it actually does.          
True, the Five Eyes is based on a set of memoranda that guide cooperation on highly technical issues such as signal interception and analysis. Popular representations featuring undersea cables, satellites, or radomes in remote landscapes sustain this view. 
But what ultimately enables and sustains the Five Eyes is the human factor: how professionals interpret, adapt to, and make use of the tools, systems, and resources at their disposal.
The shared workplaces, in their various forms, thus function as incubators of social and institutional relationships that extend across organizational and national boundaries. This is where professional solidarities take shape, grounded in a shared commitment to a common mission and to interests that do not always align with the shifting priorities of political leaders. It is also where trust is built, tested and repaired over time.
Myth #2: It operates under strict oversight 
The Five Eyes derives much of its legitimacy from the claim that it operates under strict democratic oversight. Intelligence agencies often cast themselves as guardians of the free world, yet they have repeatedly been embroiled in controversies over the mass surveillance of citizens’ communications.
These episodes expose a stark gap: oversight bodies often struggle to meaningfully restrain these agencies, while the agencies themselves continue to operate with limited accountability. In this light, some critics welcome the Trump administration’s attacks on intelligence agencies – and what they call the “deep state” – as the most tangible challenge to their unchecked powers. 
Myth #3: It is a closed circle of five nations
Certainly, the partnership came into being when the U.K.-U.S. intelligence partnership expanded to include materiel and personnel from Canada, Australia, and New Zealand. Yet what we call the Five Eyes is also a broader social and political space, encompassing a network of “third-party” intelligence agencies across Europe and beyond.
Rather than emerging solely from U.S.-led efforts during the global war on terror, many of these relationships had already reached high levels of interoperability – and trust – during the Cold War and its aftermath. For example,  Germany’s Federal Intelligence Service (BND) has long maintained close ties with the U.S. National Security Agency. Partners have at times tolerated underhanded tactics. In one striking case, Denmark’s military intelligence service assisted its NSA counterpart in spying on German Chancellor Angela Merkel and other European leaders.
Focusing narrowly on the five member states therefore obscures important insights into the wider patterns and practices of intelligence cooperation.
Beijing has frequently portrayed the Five Eyes as a political, military, and diplomatic bloc aimed at containing China’s rise. This characterization is not entirely unfounded. A strategic rivalry is clearly in play, and China is now described as an “existential threat” and an “epoch-defining challenge” in both public and private Five Eyes discussions.
Yet the Five Eyes “alliance” is far less homogeneous than such portrayals suggest. As recently as five years ago, the government of New Zealand appeared to resist what it called the “Five Eyes-ization” of its foreign policy toward China. More recently, Canada’s prime minister traveled to Beijing to explore new strategic openings, in part as a response to pressure from Donald Trump
Lastly, the geopolitical rhetoric across many Asian democracies suggests that Five Eyes officials are not alone in their concerns about China’s rise.
By any reasonable measure, Trump-driven disruptions are bad news for the Five Eyes. History shows that political and strategic divergence can and sometimes does reduce intelligence sharing. And diplomatic damage control can only go so far, particularly when senior U.S. figures – including ambassadors in Europe and Secretary of State Marco Rubio – are so consistent in taking controversial positions. Should these disruptions continue or intensify, they will likely undermine confidence in exchanged information and erode trust overall. 
Yet specialist research suggests that the human dimension of intelligence work can also absorb some of these shocks. Agencies and their personnel are not merely passive recipients of political pressure; they can resist, adapt, and navigate interference. Recent examples illustrate this dynamic, such as senior officials within the FBI who refused to dismiss employees involved in investigations tied to the January 6 U.S. Capitol attack.
More broadly, despite growing distrust and caution, intelligence agencies continue to share information, deepen cooperation, and even explore ideas such as a “European Five Eyes.” These developments suggest that, rather than acting as passive instruments, intelligence agencies can leverage a hostile political climate to pursue their own institutional interests.
Until these misunderstandings are addressed, assessments of the Five Eyes will remain incomplete, obscuring both its resilience and its vulnerabilities in an increasingly uncertain world.
Linking Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the United Kingdom and the United States, the Five Eyes is a club that plays in a league of its own.  Starting in the middle years of the 20th century, the intelligence agencies of the five nations have shared – or pooled – much of their work, everything from cryptoanalysis to cybersecurity.  
Although the U.S. agencies are dominant, the arrangement works because it enables each partner to collect and analyze far more information than it could have alone. It also works because of a high degree of mutual trust – the belief that your partners will have your back despite the risks involved.
But thanks to its deep dependency on an increasingly volatile United States, the Five Eyes has now become brittle.
articles.summary: There are persistent misunderstandings about what the intelligence partnership is, and what it actually does.

articles.title: Australia Updates Travel Warning For Laos After Announcement of Methanol Poisoning Charges
articles.full_text:
Foreign Minister Penny Wong said that she was “deeply frustrated and bitterly disappointed” at the leniency of the charges announced on Friday.
A thoroughfare in the town of Vang Vieng, a popular tourist destination in central Laos.
Australia has updated its travel warning for Laos after the country’s government laid charges in connection with a methanol poisoning that killed two Australian teenagers in 2024.
The Smartraveller notification for Laos, which is published by the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade (DFAT), was updated on Friday to classify the nation as a level two risk, urging visitors to “exercise a high degree of caution.”
“We’ve reviewed our advice for Laos and have raised the overall travel advice level,” the notification stated. “We now advise exercise a high degree of caution due to crime, the risk of methanol poisoning, and ongoing concerns around lack of transparency in the justice system.”
It repeated that “several foreign nationals, including Australians, died of methanol poisoning through consuming alcoholic drinks” and urged Australians to avoid consuming Tiger Vodka and Tiger Whisky, both of which have been banned by the Lao authorities due to health risks.
The amendment came the same day that the Lao government announced charges in connection with a mass methanol poisoning in November 2024 that cost the lives of two Australians. Holly Morton-Bowles and Bianca Jones, both aged 19, died after they were served alcohol laced with methanol at the Nana Backpacker Hostel in Vang Vieng, a popular tourist destination in central Laos. British lawyer Simone White, Danish nationals Anne-Sofie Orkild Coyman and Freja Vennervald Sorensen, and American James Louis Hutson also died in the incident.
After a long investigation, Lao authorities announced on Friday that the owner of a distillery had been charged in connection with the incident. Denmark’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs said in a statement that the charges included the sale of food products harmful to health and operating an illegal business, which charges carry a penalty ranging from three months to four years’ imprisonment, as well as a $1,600 fine.
The leniency of the charges prompted an outcry from the Australian victims’ relatives and a strong rebuke from Canberra.
In a statement, Foreign Minister Penny Wong said her government was “deeply frustrated and bitterly disappointed that authorities in Laos are not pursuing the most serious charges in relation to the methanol poisoning deaths.”
“This devastating news will only add to the immense pain and grief suffered by the families and friends of Holly and Bianca,” Wong said. “We have consistently made clear our expectations that charges should reflect the gravity of the tragedy that claimed the lives of Holly and Bianca in November 2024. This includes the Prime Minister and I expressing these expectations directly to our respective counterparts.”
Wong said that DFAT had summoned the Lao ambassador to Canberra to convey its displeasure at the charges and dispatched Pablo Kang, its special envoy for the methanol poisoning issue, to travel to Laos today to “convey the Australian Government’s objections and reinforce our expectations for an investigation that delivers justice for Holly, Bianca, and the other victims of the methanol poisoning.”
Wong said that she also planned to “again put Australia’s views directly” to Lao Foreign Minister Thongsavanh Phomvihane when she travels to Manila this week for meetings of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN).
In the immediate aftermath of the incident, the Lao Foreign Ministry said that it was “profoundly saddened” by the deaths of the six foreign nationals and promised to find “the causes of the incident and to bring the perpetrators to justice in accordance with the law.”
Given this assurance, and the repeated representations of the Australian government, it is baffling that the Lao authorities have gone so lightly on thee people that it found responsible. Laos’ tourism sector is small by regional standards – the country welcomed around 4.6 million foreign visitors in 2025, compared to 21.2 million for Vietnam and nearly 33 million for Thailand – but it plays a disproportionate role in the country’s economy. With this in mind, one would expect that the authorities would take decisive action to reassure foreign visitors that the country is a safe destination.
The Danish Foreign Ministry said in its statement that the case will “formally remain open” for 15 years, “meaning that more serious charges carrying higher penalties such as negligent manslaughter may still be brought if sufficient evidence can be established,” it stated. Whether or not the Lao government takes this opportunity remains to be seen.

Whether or not the Lao government takes this opportunity remains to be seen.

articles.title: Why Europe’s China Anxiety Is About More Than China
articles.full_text:
Brussels no longer sees China only as an economic actor. It sees China as a challenge to the organization of global order itself.
When EU Trade Commissioner Maroš Šefčovič met Chinese Commerce Minister Wang Wentao in Brussels on June 29, the encounter could easily be read as another episode in Europe’s growing anxiety over Chinese economic pressure. In 2025, China’s goods trade surplus with the EU reached roughly 360 billion euros, and by early 2026 the imbalance was still widening. Šefčovič himself warned after the Brussels talks that rising Chinese exports to Europe, combined with shrinking European market share in China, were “not sustainable.”
Yet the meeting also revealed why Europe’s China debate is about more than trade. The new EU-China consultation mechanism was not limited to market access or the bilateral trade deficit. According to the European Commission, both sides also discussed export controls, intellectual property rights, and WTO reform. The disagreement, then, is not only technical trade issues, but about who gets to define the rules under which interdependence operates. 
This is why China’s rise produces a kind of anxiety that cannot be explained through power politics alone. Europe has long understood itself not merely as a participant in global governance, but as one of its principal authors. This can be seen through the EU’s 2016 Global Strategy, where it defines its international role through democracy, human rights, multilateralism, international law, and the defense of a rules-based global order.
China unsettles Europe because it increasingly contests the rules, values, and hierarchies through which Europe has understood its own place in the international order. The tension was codified in the European Commission’s 2019 EU-China Strategic Outlook, which described China simultaneously as a cooperation partner, an economic competitor, and a “systemic rival” promoting alternative models of governance. The phrase matters because it shows that Brussels no longer sees China only as an economic actor. It sees China as a challenge to the organization of global order itself.
Economically, China is no longer only a market or manufacturing base, but a competitor in sectors Europe considers essential to its future: electric vehicles, machinery, chemicals, steel, batteries, and clean-tech supply chains. Germany illustrates this shift most sharply. In 2025, German goods exports to China fell to their lowest level in a decade, while German car exports to China had fallen by 66 percent compared with 2022.
This is not simply a German problem. It strikes at the heart of the European economic model: if Europe loses industrial capacity and technological leverage in key sectors, its ability to defend a rules-based order becomes weaker in practice, even if the language of that order remains intact.
This is why “de-risking” has become such an important formula for Brussels. In her 2023 speech on EU-China relations, European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen argued that decoupling from China was neither viable nor in Europe’s interest. Instead, she framed Europe’s approach as one of reducing risk while preserving engagement. The phrase captures Europe’s dilemma: it cannot sever economic ties with China, but it can no longer treat interdependence as politically neutral.
The disagreement is also diplomatic and normative. China is no longer simply adapting to existing institutions but increasingly seeking to shape the terms of global governance whether through BRICS, WTO reform debates, export controls, technological standards, or AI governance. Politically, China offers many states a form of partnership that does not rely on the same moral vocabulary Europe often attaches to its external relations. Beijing’s language of sovereignty, non-interference, development, and opposition to “hegemonism and power politics” has long been central to Chinese diplomacy. Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi has repeatedly used this vocabulary to present China as a defender of international justice against Western dominance.
The Global South is central to this conversation. It is not simply a diplomatic constituency or a convenient counterweight to the West; it is increasingly the arena in which new practices of development, infrastructure, finance, technology, and global governance are being tested. The 2024 FOCAC Beijing Action Plan is one example. FOCAC 2024 marked a shift away from large infrastructure deals alone and toward “small yet beautiful” projects, governance exchange, and a broader form of Chinese normative power.
However, this political opening contains a paradox. As China becomes more powerful and more central to global governance, it must confront a difficult question: can a state expand its rule-making power without becoming the kind of hierarchical force it once criticized?
Anti-hegemonic language does not automatically prevent hegemonic practice. A country can oppose Western dominance and still create new dependencies; it can claim to democratize global governance while concentrating influence around its own institutions, financing structures, technological standards, and diplomatic preferences. The risk of domination today is not in its old territorial form, but a hierarchy that emerges when access to infrastructure, finance, technology, markets, and standards becomes dependent on one increasingly powerful actor.
For Europe, this makes the China question even more complicated. If Europe treats China only as a strategic competitor, it risks ignoring the genuine dissatisfaction many countries feel toward the Western-led order. Yet if it treats the Global South merely as a battlefield for influence, it reproduces precisely the paternalism that has weakened its credibility. 
Europe has tried to respond through its own connectivity and infrastructure agenda, particularly the Global Gateway. But the challenge is not only to offer an alternative to China; it is to make Europe’s offer coherent, material, and credible to countries that do not want to choose between rival blocs.
The result is a European debate marked by contradiction. Europe wants cooperation with China on climate, trade, development, and global stability. It also wants protection from dependency, coercion, and technological vulnerability. It wants to defend the rules-based order, but it must also face the fact that many outside the West see that order as selective, unequal, or historically compromised. It wants to remain open, but not naïve; principled, but not powerless; autonomous, but not isolated.
This is why Europe’s China policy is so difficult to define. The problem is not simply that member states have different economic interests, although they do. Nor is it only that European institutions disagree over how hard or soft the EU should be toward Beijing, although they often do. Contemporary debates over China’s trade imbalance have shown broad concern, still a unified response remains elusive: France has pushed for a tougher line, while Germany and Spain have been more cautious about escalation. The real issue China poses to Europe is a confrontation with the limits of Europe’s own global identity.
For decades, Europe could imagine itself as a post-imperial, post-national, normative power: a political project that had transformed its own violent past into a commitment to law, rights, and multilateral order. China’s rise unsettles that self-image. It reminds Europe that rules are never only legal principles; they are also distributions of power. It reminds Europe that moral authority is not permanent. And it reminds Europe that the future of global order will not be decided only in Brussels, Washington, or Beijing, but also across the many states and societies that refuse to remain passive recipients of rules written elsewhere.
The true question, then, is not whether Europe should be anxious about China, but what Europe chooses to do with that anxiety. If the anxiety is only defensive, Europe will try to preserve an international order that many states already experience as unequal or incomplete. But if it becomes reflective, it could push Europe toward a more serious form of renewal: one that combines industrial capacity with political credibility, resilience with openness, and principles with greater reciprocity.
When EU Trade Commissioner Maroš Šefčovič met Chinese Commerce Minister Wang Wentao in Brussels on June 29, the encounter could easily be read as another episode in Europe’s growing anxiety over Chinese economic pressure. In 2025, China’s goods trade surplus with the EU reached roughly 360 billion euros, and by early 2026 the imbalance was still widening. Šefčovič himself warned after the Brussels talks that rising Chinese exports to Europe, combined with shrinking European market share in China, were “not sustainable.”
Yet the meeting also revealed why Europe’s China debate is about more than trade. The new EU-China consultation mechanism was not limited to market access or the bilateral trade deficit. According to the European Commission, both sides also discussed export controls, intellectual property rights, and WTO reform. The disagreement, then, is not only technical trade issues, but about who gets to define the rules under which interdependence operates. 
This is why China’s rise produces a kind of anxiety that cannot be explained through power politics alone. Europe has long understood itself not merely as a participant in global governance, but as one of its principal authors. This can be seen through the EU’s 2016 Global Strategy, where it defines its international role through democracy, human rights, multilateralism, international law, and the defense of a rules-based global order.
China unsettles Europe because it increasingly contests the rules, values, and hierarchies through which Europe has understood its own place in the international order. The tension was codified in the European Commission’s 2019 EU-China Strategic Outlook, which described China simultaneously as a cooperation partner, an economic competitor, and a “systemic rival” promoting alternative models of governance. The phrase matters because it shows that Brussels no longer sees China only as an economic actor. It sees China as a challenge to the organization of global order itself.
Economically, China is no longer only a market or manufacturing base, but a competitor in sectors Europe considers essential to its future: electric vehicles, machinery, chemicals, steel, batteries, and clean-tech supply chains. Germany illustrates this shift most sharply. In 2025, German goods exports to China fell to their lowest level in a decade, while German car exports to China had fallen by 66 percent compared with 2022.
This is not simply a German problem. It strikes at the heart of the European economic model: if Europe loses industrial capacity and technological leverage in key sectors, its ability to defend a rules-based order becomes weaker in practice, even if the language of that order remains intact.
This is why “de-risking” has become such an important formula for Brussels. In her 2023 speech on EU-China relations, European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen argued that decoupling from China was neither viable nor in Europe’s interest. Instead, she framed Europe’s approach as one of reducing risk while preserving engagement. The phrase captures Europe’s dilemma: it cannot sever economic ties with China, but it can no longer treat interdependence as politically neutral.
The disagreement is also diplomatic and normative. China is no longer simply adapting to existing institutions but increasingly seeking to shape the terms of global governance whether through BRICS, WTO reform debates, export controls, technological standards, or AI governance. Politically, China offers many states a form of partnership that does not rely on the same moral vocabulary Europe often attaches to its external relations. Beijing’s language of sovereignty, non-interference, development, and opposition to “hegemonism and power politics” has long been central to Chinese diplomacy. Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi has repeatedly used this vocabulary to present China as a defender of international justice against Western dominance.
The Global South is central to this conversation. It is not simply a diplomatic constituency or a convenient counterweight to the West; it is increasingly the arena in which new practices of development, infrastructure, finance, technology, and global governance are being tested. The 2024 FOCAC Beijing Action Plan is one example. FOCAC 2024 marked a shift away from large infrastructure deals alone and toward “small yet beautiful” projects, governance exchange, and a broader form of Chinese normative power.
However, this political opening contains a paradox. As China becomes more powerful and more central to global governance, it must confront a difficult question: can a state expand its rule-making power without becoming the kind of hierarchical force it once criticized?
Anti-hegemonic language does not automatically prevent hegemonic practice. A country can oppose Western dominance and still create new dependencies; it can claim to democratize global governance while concentrating influence around its own institutions, financing structures, technological standards, and diplomatic preferences. The risk of domination today is not in its old territorial form, but a hierarchy that emerges when access to infrastructure, finance, technology, markets, and standards becomes dependent on one increasingly powerful actor.
For Europe, this makes the China question even more complicated. If Europe treats China only as a strategic competitor, it risks ignoring the genuine dissatisfaction many countries feel toward the Western-led order. Yet if it treats the Global South merely as a battlefield for influence, it reproduces precisely the paternalism that has weakened its credibility.
articles.summary: Brussels no longer sees China only as an economic actor. It sees China as a challenge to the organization of global order itself.

articles.title: Is Thailand’s PM More Concerned About Sound Cannabis Policy or Political Optics?
articles.full_text:
Amid a rise in smuggling, Anutin Charnvirakul has recently come out against the policy he once championed: the decriminalization of marijuana.
Thai Prime Minister Anutin Charnvirakul is most known outside Thailand for two things: his 2026 center-right electoral victory and his advocacy for the legalization of marijuana in Thailand. Those two things will always be related, as recently he warned he might shut down the booming industry due to a rise in cannabis trafficking out of Thailand. “We need to figure out what we should do to prevent other countries from blaming Thailand as being the weak link in this problem,” Anutin warned.
Critical European partners have seen a rise in cannabis smuggling, as Germany recently reported two seizures of 1.2 tonnes with a street value of $13 million, both inbound from Thailand. And the pressure is rising, since the issue may threaten Thailand’s OECD bid.
Internal criticism of Anutin for these failures came right as the Thai leader saw a major drop in public support, from 29.4 percent of the public preferring him as the leading choice for prime minister in a NIDA poll in January to just 21.7 percent in early July. Consequently, Anutin might very well pull the trigger and reverse one of the country’s most significant policy shifts in recent memory – one that he himself advocated for years.
The cost of this, however, would be to doom an emerging industry, and there are other ways of solving the short-term optical challenges facing his government.
First, Thailand’s initial attempt to manage cannabis through milder policy reforms failed to curb unregulated consumption, eventually forcing the government to introduce medical-only restrictions between late 2025 and mid-2026. While these restrictions were catastrophic for the domestic industry, forcing 7,000 cannabis shops to close due to the heavy costs of compliance, they have also failed to stop drug trafficking. Instead, the domestic market has created incentive for growers to smuggle excess supply overseas. According to the Thai Customs Department, between October 1, 2025, and June 30, 2026, Thai authorities intercepted more than 3,300 illegal shipments, seizing over 37,000 kilograms of cannabis valued at $14 million. The regulations have thus pushed the domestic market into an active ring of drug smuggling.
This is where the logic of a proposed shutdown fails. The legal marijuana industry is not the source of the problem. Further, when Thailand’s health minister argued earlier this month in favor of a draft Cannabis and Hemp Act, he called for greater action to combat smuggling and stop the spread of fake licenses, but not regulation of the cannabis industry itself. If the rationale of for the new Act is to fight criminality, there is no reason to punish thousands of legally-compliant businesses and producers.
The process of passing the Act itself has been problematic, as it has repeatedly failed to overcome legislative hurdles. Passage of the bill appears to be trapped by a regulatory and institutional minefield, where the market is subject to an array of different legal statutes, and businesses must navigate several ministerial decrees. The patchwork system does not centralize authority with one government agency but several, including the Department of Thai Traditional and Alternative Medicine, and the Office of the Narcotics Control Board. Further, there have been divisions among coalition partners in the past, most notably from the prime minister himself, who has taken a 180-degree position.
There’s little question that regulation of the industry is required. Thailand’s draft Cannabis and Hemp Act restricts cannabis to medical, research, and industrial use, and bans recreational consumption. It enforces strict regulations, including medical-facility standards for dispensaries and mandatory prescriptions, as well as reclassifying extracts containing more than 0.2 percent THC as Category 5 narcotics.
While the Act remains on the precipice of becoming law, there have been meaningful recent steps to streamline regulation. For instance, the government has cut approval times for cannabis licenses by setting up digital licensing platforms and introduced harsher customs penalties of 30,000 baht per kilogram for anyone caught attempting to smuggle cannabis flowers or cannabis products out of the country, in addition to forfeiture of the products themselves – each of which are just weeks into implementation. This makes Anutin’s shutdown threat even more difficult to comprehend. Why not wait? The results might already be visible. In the United Kingdom, the number of cannabis arrivals by mail from Thailand declined by 90 percent in the first three months of 2025 alone due to a partnership between the U.K. Border Force and Thai customs. If the recent concerns stem from criminality, again, why the need to cripple an entire industry?
After all, it was Anutin himself who successfully pitched legalization to the Thai public. As public health minister, he argued that a legal cannabis industry could reduce prison overcrowding and create a hemp industry that was projected to reach $9.6 billion by 2030, hence alleviating rural poverty. To shut this all down now would only harm the same people it was projected to help, which include rural farmers, hill communities, and patients with chronic pain.
There are vital issues that Anutin rightly should consider, such as increases in cannabis addiction, the impacts on the public health sector, and the creation of a robust regulatory environment. But the process of creating a regulated cannabis market has been so badly managed and Anutin deserves considerable criticism for his role in creating the legislative and regulatory mess facing producers and law enforcement.  These issues should have all been weighed and considered before Thailand made the leap to legalization.
However, as things stand, these issues cannot easily be solved in one fell swoop. Sending the entire cannabis industry to its doom would be less an act of mature public policymaking than the act of a center-right politician confronting public criticism with only short-term gains in mind.

Those two things will always be related, as recently he warned he might shut down the booming industry due to a rise in cannabis trafficking out of Thailand. “We need to figure out what we should do to prevent other countries from blaming Thailand as being the weak link in this problem,” Anutin warned.
Critical European partners have seen a rise in cannabis smuggling, as Germany recently reported two seizures of 1.2 tonnes with a street value of $13 million, both inbound from Thailand. And the pressure is rising, since the issue may threaten Thailand’s OECD bid.

articles.title: The Romanovs and ‘Invictus’ in the Impeachment Trial of Philippine Vice President
articles.full_text:
Sara Duterte claimed that she is “bloodied but unbowed” in the face of persecution by President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. and his administration.
The Philippine Senate holds a hearing during the impeachment trial of Vice President Sara Duterte, Jul. 14, 2026.
Russia’s Romanov dynasty and William Ernest Henley’s poem “Invictus” were repeatedly mentioned during the first two weeks of the impeachment trial of Philippine Vice President Sara Duterte, sparking heated commentaries and online debates on history, violence, and accountability.
After being impeached twice by the House of Representatives for betrayal of public trust, Duterte is now on trial in the Senate, which is constitutionally mandated to deliver a verdict and decide if the vice president should be allowed to stay in office or be removed and barred from holding another elected position.
During the second day of her trial, Duterte appeared in the Senate to consult with her lawyers. She also faced the press and delivered a short sound bite: “In this bloodbath and bludgeoning, I will be bloodied but unbowed.”
The statement was obviously inspired by the Victorian-era poem “Invictus,” written by Henley. Invictus is Latin for being unconquered in the face of adversity.
The poem seems to be a favorite of the Dutertes. A few months ago, former President Rodrigo Duterte, Sara’s father, quoted another line from the poem, “I am the master of my fate, I am the captain of my soul.” Duterte is currently under the custody of the International Criminal Court in The Hague, and is facing trial for alleged crimes against humanity in connection with the bloody anti-drug campaign during his term.
The two Dutertes could be trying to assure their supporters that they will survive their trials while seeking to generate public sympathy. It echoes their earlier assertion that they are being unfairly persecuted by the government of President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. However, their critics insist that the suffering endured by the narrator in the poem is incomparable to the situation of the Dutertes, who are being held to account for their abuses of power.
The first issue presented by the prosecution was the allegation that the vice president was involved in a conspiracy to harm the First Family. Duterte is accused of making a threat to kill Marcos and his wife and cousin in a Zoom video press conference in November 2024. Through her lawyers, Duterte has denied the charge and claimed that her statements were taken out of context.
During the cross-examination of a witness, Duterte’s lawyer asked about an “Operation Romanov,” mentioned in the Zoom video, which prompted the vice president to react and rant against the First Family. Duterte’s camp believes it was a plot aimed against the family of the vice president.
But law enforcement officials testified that when they probed the issue, they were able to trace it to a speech given by Duterte’s brother, Davao City Mayor Baste Duterte, in January 2024. The mayor’s speech was actually directed to President Marcos.
”From now on, before you go to bed, think of the Romanovs, think of Benito Mussolini and his wife, think of what happened to you in 1986,” he said.
The Romanov monarchy was deposed and murdered after the Russian Revolution in 1917. Mussolini was killed by partisans during World War II. The Marcoses fled the country during the 1986 People Power uprising.
Based on this historical reading, the so-called “Operation Romanov” could be interpreted as a threat against Marcos, since he is the country’s current ruler.
Complicating the matter was the manifestation of a pro-Duterte senator-judge, who pointed out that the Romanovs and Mussolini were killed by communists. Based on this premise, he warned that Duterte’s life could also be in danger since her father waged an all-out war against local communists.
The whole “Operation Romanov” line of questioning was confusing and disappointing for the general public, but it reflects the capability of the Duterte camp to stretch even ludicrous narratives to provide justification for the vice president’s online outburst against the First Family and her critics.
The next issue in the impeachment trial concerns the alleged misuse of confidential funds and the suspicious financial transactions of Duterte and her spouse. Anti-corruption advocates consider this the substantive aspect of the impeachment, and in fact, many are hoping that the prosecution panel will focus on providing strong evidence to prove Duterte’s culpability.
If the first two weeks of the trial showed a glimpse of Duterte’s defense strategy, we can expect long debates on procedural matters. Duterte’s lawyers have, in fact, already opposed the release of financial records by citing privacy and bank secrecy laws.
Hopefully, there will be no more dramatic lines and improper historical references that only serve as a distraction in the trial. It may be mildly entertaining to see and hear politicians reveal the depth of their knowledge of literature and history, but the demand for truth and accountability warrants a more serious public discourse from all stakeholders involved in the impeachment process.

articles.title: Beyond the South China Sea: 4 Other Maritime Flashpoints in East Asia
articles.full_text:
These disputes could reshape the geopolitical order in East Asia – or even spark a war.
In this Dec. 22, 2024 file photo, a China Coast Guard ship is seen near the disputed Senkaku/Diaoyu Islands in the East China Sea.
You probably have heard of the South China Sea disputes – a major flashpoint involving China, the Philippines, Vietnam, and others. But the South China Sea isn’t the only maritime dispute in Asia. 
Today, we’re going to talk about four other maritime flashpoints in East Asia – and how they are reshaping the geopolitical landscape today. Everyone knows about Taiwan, and the South China Sea; we’re looking at other disputes that haven’t appeared in headlines lately. 
Stay tuned, because we’re ranking them in order: from the least likely to spark an actual shooting war, to the highest risk of conflict.

Stay tuned, because we’re ranking them in order: from the least likely to spark an actual shooting war, to the highest risk of conflict.
